entries.text: Chaga 🍄 fungi fungi supp A black tumor-like growth on birch trees in cold climates — the highest ORAC antioxidant score of any food on Earth, and traditionally used in Siberia and the Boreal as a daily tonic. Highest ORAC antioxidant score of any food — superoxide dismutase (SOD) content Powerful immune modulator — activates NK cells and macrophages Betulinic acid has anti-cancer and anti-viral properties Reduces inflammation throughout the body Supports healthy blood sugar regulation Adaptogenic — helps the body resist physical and mental stress Massive SOD content (100x higher than most superfoods) neutralizes superoxide radicals Betulinic acid derived from host birch — selectively cytotoxic to cancer cells Polysaccharides activate immune surveillance Wild-harvested from live birch trees in cold climates (Russia, Canada, Northern US) Dual-extracted (hot water + alcohol) — necessary for full benefits Powdered for slow simmer (1+ hour) — single brew unlocks little Cacao + chaga — earthy hot-drink classic With other adaptogens (reishi, ashwagandha) Honey + chaga decoction — flavor and antimicrobial synergy High oxalate content — caution with kidney stones Anticoagulants — bleeding risk Diabetes meds — additive blood sugar effect Sustainability concern — slow-growing; many products are now cultivated mycelium (lower potency) 500–1000 mg dual-extracted daily, or 1 tsp powder simmered 30+ min, 1–2x daily.

entries.title: Shiitake
entries.text: Shiitake 🍄 fungi fungi food The most-consumed medicinal mushroom in the world — a culinary mushroom that also boasts genuine clinical research, especially for cholesterol lowering and immune support. Lentinan is a beta-glucan used in cancer therapy in Japan Rich in eritadenine which lowers LDL cholesterol Contains all essential amino acids — rare for a mushroom High in B vitamins including B5, B6, and folate Immune-boosting polysaccharides activate white blood cells Supports cardiovascular health and reduces inflammation Lentinan binds Dectin-1 → activates Th1 immune response Eritadenine reduces methylation of phosphatidylethanolamine → lowers cholesterol Ergosterol → vitamin D2 with UV exposure Dried (rehydrated for cooking) — concentrated nutrients UV-exposed dried — high vitamin D Hot-water extract supplement for therapeutic dosing Sea vegetables (kombu, wakame) — traditional Japanese broth base Garlic, ginger, scallions — savory umami stir-fries Eggs — vitamin D + choline Shiitake dermatitis — rare rash from raw consumption (cook thoroughly) Histamine release possible Mushroom allergies 3–10 dried shiitake (5–20 g) daily in cooking. Lentinan extract: 1–3 mg daily for therapeutic use.

entries.title: Turkey Tail
entries.text: Turkey Tail 🍄 fungi fungi supp The most extensively researched medicinal mushroom — PSK and PSP are approved cancer adjuvants in Japan, and turkey tail's prebiotic effect on the gut microbiome is unmatched among mushrooms. PSK (polysaccharide-K) is an approved cancer adjunct therapy in Japan Most researched mushroom for immune modulation and cancer support Powerful prebiotic — feeds Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus Supports gut microbiome diversity more than most foods Anti-viral properties against HPV and other viruses Rich in phenolic antioxidants that reduce oxidative stress PSK and PSP bind TLR-2 and Dectin-1, priming both innate and adaptive immunity Selectively feeds beneficial gut microbes (acts as prebiotic to microbiome) Reduces tumor angiogenesis in animal models Hot-water extract (PSK/PSP-rich) Standardized to >30% polysaccharides Fruiting body verified (not mycelium on grain) Probiotics — synergistic gut-immune effects Reishi or chaga — broader immune support With cancer treatments — discussed with oncologist as adjunct Anticoagulants — bleeding risk Autoimmune disease — immune-stimulating Mushroom allergy Generally very safe even at high doses 1–3 g extract daily, split between doses. Higher doses (3–9 g) used in cancer adjunct protocols under medical supervision.

entries.title: Oyster Mushroom
entries.text: Oyster Mushroom 🍄 fungi fungi food An accessible, easy-to-grow mushroom with a serious nutritional resume — natural statins, ergothioneine, and high in B12-active corrinoids that may benefit plant-based diets. Rich in beta-glucans that activate innate immune response Contains statins (lovastatin) that naturally lower cholesterol High in ergothioneine — a unique antioxidant protective for organs and brain Good source of B12 precursors — beneficial for plant-based diets Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant polyphenols Supports healthy blood pressure through potassium and fiber Lovastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase — same target as prescription statins Ergothioneine actively transported into mitochondria, protecting organs Beta-glucans modulate innate immune response Fresh — sautéed in olive oil with garlic Dried — for soups and broths Pearl, king, golden varieties — flavor variations, similar nutrients Garlic + thyme + butter — classic preparation With eggs or in risotto Broths and ramen — releases umami glutamates Statin interactions if also on prescription statins Mushroom allergy Spores can trigger respiratory sensitivity in growers (less so in cooking) 1 cup cooked (~85 g) provides modest nutritional dose. 3–5x weekly for therapeutic benefit.

entries.title: Maitake
entries.text: Maitake 🍄 fungi fungi supp The 'dancing mushroom' of Japan — D-fraction is one of the most-studied anti-cancer compounds in the mushroom world, with particular research on glycemic control. D-fraction extract is among the most studied anti-cancer mushroom compounds Powerfully regulates blood sugar — used in type 2 diabetes management Stimulates NK cells and T-cells for immune defense Supports weight management through metabolic effects Lowers blood pressure and LDL cholesterol Adaptogenic properties reduce stress-related hormonal imbalance D-fraction (1,6/1,3 beta-glucan) activates macrophages and dendritic cells Improves insulin sensitivity via PPAR-gamma pathway Reduces angiotensin-converting enzyme activity → lowers BP D-fraction or MD-fraction standardized extract Whole cooked mushroom — rich flavor for soups, stir-fries Hot-water dual extract for therapeutic dosing Combined with reishi or shiitake — synergistic immune stack With sea vegetables — traditional Japanese miso soup base Anti-cancer protocols often combine with turkey tail Diabetic medication — additive blood sugar lowering Anticoagulants — bleeding risk Autoimmune disease — immune stimulation Generally safe 500–2000 mg standardized extract daily, or 1–2 oz fresh mushroom 3–5x weekly.

entries.title: Reishi Mushroom
entries.text: Reishi Mushroom 🍄 fungi fungi supp The 'mushroom of immortality' in Chinese medicine — bitter and not a culinary mushroom; used exclusively as a tonic, with a 2000-year track record for stress, sleep, and immune function. Triterpenes have powerful anti-cancer and immune-modulating properties Reduces stress and anxiety — natural adaptogen Supports liver detoxification and protection Improves sleep quality through nervous system calming Anti-inflammatory throughout the body Supports cardiovascular health by lowering blood pressure Ganoderic acids (triterpenes) modulate immune response and inhibit histamine release Polysaccharides stimulate T-cell and macrophage activity Adenosine-like compounds support relaxation and sleep Red reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) — most-studied species Dual-extracted to capture both polysaccharides AND triterpenes Fruiting body verified, ≥4% triterpenes Cacao + reishi — bitter-sweet evening tonic Chamomile or passionflower — sleep blend Long-term daily use builds effect Anticoagulants — bleeding risk Immunosuppressants Hypotension — additive BP lowering Mild GI upset, dizziness, dry mouth occasionally 1–3 g dual-extracted reishi daily, often at night for sleep benefits. Take consistently for 4+ weeks.

entries.title: Rhodiola Rosea
entries.text: Rhodiola Rosea 🌸 herb herb supp A high-altitude adaptogen from the Russian Arctic — fast-acting (same-day effects), specifically researched for mental fatigue, depression, and athletic performance under stress. Adaptogen that reduces physical and mental fatigue dramatically Increases resistance to stress without sedation Improves cognitive function and memory under stress May increase ATP synthesis in mitochondria Anti-depressant effects comparable to some pharmaceuticals in studies Supports athletic performance and recovery Rosavins and salidroside modulate dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine Inhibits MAO-A — mild anti-depressant effect Improves mitochondrial ATP synthesis SHR-5 (the Swedish-tested extract used in most clinical trials) Standardized to 3% rosavins and 1% salidroside (3:1 ratio) Avoid raw root powder — variable potency L-theanine — calmer focus Mucuna pruriens — dopamine support Best taken in AM/midday — can be stimulating Bipolar disorder — can trigger mania Insomnia if taken too late MAOI and SSRI interactions Generally well tolerated 200–600 mg standardized extract daily, AM or pre-stress. Effects within hours; long-term safe with breaks.

entries.title: Sage
entries.text: Sage 🌿 herb herb spice More than a Thanksgiving herb — sage extracts have shown notable cognitive enhancement in healthy adults and even improvements in Alzheimer's patients in small studies. Improves memory and cognitive function — studied in Alzheimer's research Rosmarinic acid is a potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant Antimicrobial against bacteria and fungi Supports hormone balance — used traditionally for menopause symptoms Reduces excessive sweating Antidiabetic effects through enzyme inhibition Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor — increases acetylcholine, like donepezil Rosmarinic acid reduces inflammation and oxidative stress Phytoestrogens reduce hot flashes via estrogen receptor binding Spanish sage (Salvia lavandulifolia) — most-studied for cognition Common garden sage — traditional cooking and tea Tincture for therapeutic dosing Rosemary + sage tea — cognitive enhancement blend Butter + sage on pasta — Italian classic Lemon + sage — herbal flavor brightener Thujone content — avoid high-dose sage essential oil Pregnancy and breastfeeding (may reduce milk supply) Seizure disorders — high-dose risk Diabetic meds — additive blood sugar lowering Tea: 1–2 tsp dried leaves, 2–3 cups daily. Extract: 300–600 mg standardized daily.

entries.title: Sauerkraut
entries.text: Sauerkraut 🫙 food food supp Lacto-fermented cabbage — one of the richest natural probiotic sources, with up to 10x the vitamin C of fresh cabbage and unique post-fermentation compounds like indole-3-carbinol. One of the richest natural sources of probiotics Vitamin C levels increase through fermentation Supports gut microbiome diversity K2 produced by fermentation bacteria supports bone and cardiovascular health Supports immune function through gut-immune connection Digestive enzymes improve overall nutrient absorption Lactobacillus plantarum, brevis, sakei dominate during fermentation Bacteria produce vitamin K2 (MK-7) — directs calcium to bones, away from arteries Indole-3-carbinol from cabbage supports estrogen detoxification Raw, unpasteurized (refrigerated section) — live cultures Homemade — most diverse strains Avoid heat-pasteurized canned kraut — no probiotic benefit With fatty meats (German tradition) — probiotic + protein On eggs or avocado toast — modern brunch Side of olive oil + lemon — Mediterranean spin Histamine-rich — issue for histamine intolerance Sodium content — limit if hypertensive Tyramine — MAOI interactions Start small to avoid die-off symptoms 1–2 tbsp daily, increasing to ¼ cup. Best with meals to support digestion.

entries.title: Schisandra
entries.text: Schisandra 🌸 herb herb supp The 'five-flavor berry' of Chinese medicine — uniquely tonifies all five organ systems (liver, lung, kidney, heart, spleen) and is increasingly studied for liver protection and stress. Adaptogen that improves physical performance and endurance Supports liver health and detoxification through schisandrin compounds Improves mental focus and reduces anxiety simultaneously Anti-aging antioxidant properties Supports adrenal function and stress resilience Traditional use for skin health and beautification Schisandrin B is one of the most powerful natural inducers of phase II liver enzymes Adaptogenic via HPA axis modulation Antioxidant 'lignans' protect mitochondrial DNA Dried whole berries — chewable, traditional preparation Tincture or extract standardized to schisandrin Powder added to smoothies Goji + schisandra — traditional Chinese 'beauty' tonic With rhodiola or ashwagandha — broader adaptogen stack Berry-rich smoothies GERD — acidic berries can trigger reflux Pregnancy Drug interactions via CYP3A4 induction — affects many medications Generally well tolerated 1–3 g dried berries daily, or 500–1500 mg standardized extract.

entries.title: Stevia
entries.text: Stevia 🌿 herb herb food A South American shrub whose leaves contain glycosides 200–300x sweeter than sucrose with no glycemic impact — but processing matters; whole leaf differs from refined Reb-A. Zero calorie natural sweetener 200-300x sweeter than sugar Does not spike blood sugar — safe for diabetics May slightly lower blood pressure Antioxidant properties from stevioside Anti-inflammatory effects Does not contribute to tooth decay unlike sugar Stevioside and rebaudioside-A bind sweet receptors without activating glucose pathways Stevioside has mild calcium-channel blocking effect — lowers BP Doesn't feed oral bacteria → no cavities Whole green leaf (powder or tea) — full plant profile, slight licorice note Pure stevia liquid (alcohol- or glycerin-based) — concentrated, no fillers Avoid 'stevia' blends with maltodextrin or erythritol if pure intake desired Lemon water + stevia drops — zero-calorie 'lemonade' Berries — sweetened without sugar Coffee or tea — common application Asteraceae allergy Bitter aftertaste — some find unpalatable Possible mild blood pressure lowering Truvia and similar blends often contain erythritol — different compound; GI effects in some As needed; pure stevia: 1–4 drops liquid per beverage. Whole leaf: ¼ tsp.

entries.title: Strawberries
entries.text: Strawberries 🍓 food food The vitamin C, ellagic acid, and anthocyanin trifecta — a single cup exceeds full daily vitamin C and provides a clinically meaningful dose of anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer compounds. Exceptionally high in vitamin C — one cup provides full daily value Anthocyanins reduce inflammation and oxidative stress Ellagic acid has anti-cancer properties Improves insulin sensitivity and blood sugar response Supports heart health by lowering LDL and blood pressure High in folate for DNA repair and cell growth Ellagitannins metabolized by gut microbiome to urolithins — anti-aging and mitochondrial benefits Pelargonidin (anthocyanin) inhibits inflammatory pathways Fisetin — senolytic compound that may clear senescent cells Organic — strawberries are top of the EWG Dirty Dozen pesticide list In-season summer berries — peak flavor and nutrients Freeze-dried for off-season — retains nutrients well Spinach salad + walnuts — vitamin C boosts iron absorption Greek yogurt — protein and probiotic pairing Dark chocolate — flavanol synergy Top pesticide-load fruit — buy organic Common food allergen (oral allergy syndrome) Histamine release possible in sensitive individuals Oxalates — caution with kidney stones 1–2 cups (150–300 g) daily in season. Pesticide-free essential.

entries.title: Thyme
entries.text: Thyme 🌿 herb herb spice A culinary herb with serious antimicrobial credentials — thymol is used in pharmaceutical mouthwashes and is one of the few herbal compounds with broad activity against respiratory bacteria. Thymol is a powerful antimicrobial against bacteria and fungi Supports respiratory health — used for bronchitis and cough Rich in antioxidants including vitamin C and A Anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation Supports digestion and reduces bloating Antifungal properties helpful for Candida Thymol and carvacrol disrupt bacterial cell membranes — broad antimicrobial Bronchodilator and expectorant effects Antispasmodic in GI smooth muscle — relieves cramping and bloating Fresh thyme — for cooking and infusions Dried thyme — concentrated; use ⅓ the amount Thyme essential oil — for steam inhalation or diluted topical use Honey + thyme tea — cold and cough remedy Lemon and garlic — synergistic antimicrobial Roasted vegetables, fish, and poultry — culinary classic Anticoagulants — mild bleeding risk Pregnancy — avoid essential oil Thyroid medication — thymol may interfere Estrogenic — caution with hormone-sensitive conditions Tea: 1–2 tsp dried, 2–4 cups daily for respiratory issues. Cooking: generously. Essential oil: dilute to 1–2% topically.

entries.title: Turmeric
entries.text: Turmeric 🟡 spice spice herb The single most-studied medicinal herb in the world (over 17,000 published studies) — curcumin's broad anti-inflammatory effects are real, but absorption is the persistent challenge. Curcumin is one of the most studied anti-inflammatory compounds Reduces joint pain and stiffness comparable to some NSAIDs Powerful antioxidant that stimulates the body's own antioxidant enzymes May reverse early Alzheimer's pathology in research models Supports liver health and detoxification Anti-cancer properties through multiple mechanisms — best absorbed with black pepper Curcumin inhibits NF-κB, COX-2, and TNF-alpha — anti-inflammatory cascade Activates Nrf2 antioxidant response Piperine in black pepper inhibits glucuronidation → 2000% absorption increase With black pepper + fat (turmeric latte) — traditional preparation Meriva (phytosome), Theracurmin, Longvida — patented high-absorption forms Fresh root grated into curries Black pepper — essential for absorption Coconut milk or fat — fat-soluble curcumin Ginger — anti-inflammatory synergy Bile duct obstruction — increases bile flow Anticoagulants and antiplatelets Iron absorption inhibitor Some commercial powders adulterated with lead chromate — buy from reputable sources 500–2000 mg curcumin extract daily with black pepper and fat. Culinary: 1 tsp turmeric in cooking.

entries.title: Vitex (Chaste Tree)
entries.text: Vitex (Chaste Tree) 🌸 herb herb supp The premier female hormone herb — works on the pituitary rather than the ovaries, gently rebalancing the menstrual cycle without exogenous hormones. Effects build over months. Supports female hormone balance by acting on pituitary gland Reduces PMS symptoms including mood swings and breast tenderness May support fertility by normalizing luteal phase Reduces prolactin levels — helpful for certain hormonal conditions Traditionally used for menopause symptom relief Adaptogenic effects on reproductive hormones Dopaminergic action on pituitary D2 receptors — suppresses prolactin Normalizes LH/FSH ratio over time Supports luteal phase progesterone via shifted feedback Standardized extract (Ze 440 — German clinical extract) Tincture from fresh berries Capsules with ≥0.5% agnusides Black cohosh for menopause Magnesium + B6 — additive PMS support Cycle-tracking apps — measure effect over months Hormonal birth control — may reduce effectiveness Pregnancy after first trimester Dopamine-affecting medications (e.g., bromocriptine, antipsychotics) Effects take 3+ cycles to assess 400–500 mg standardized extract daily, or 40 drops tincture. Use consistently for at least 3 menstrual cycles.

entries.title: Wheatgrass
entries.text: Wheatgrass 🌱 food food supp Young wheat plant juiced before grain formation — gluten-free at this stage, packed with chlorophyll, and the closest dietary structural analog to hemoglobin. Extremely concentrated source of chlorophyll for blood oxygenation Complete amino acid profile Rich in enzymes that support digestion High in iron, magnesium, and calcium Supports liver detoxification and cleansing Alkalizing effect balances body pH Chlorophyll molecule structurally similar to hemoglobin (with magnesium vs iron) — may support erythropoiesis Active superoxide dismutase (SOD) provides antioxidant defense High enzymatic content aids digestion Fresh-juiced from living wheatgrass — best enzymes Frozen wheatgrass juice cubes — convenient and preserve most nutrients Powdered for travel — lowest activity but acceptable Pineapple or apple juice — masks grassy taste On empty stomach for best absorption With a small fat (e.g., coconut oil) for fat-soluble compound absorption Wheat allergy/celiac — controversial; grain protein gluten not present at grass stage but cross-contamination risk Nausea common when starting — start with 1 oz Mold contamination possible in poorly grown wheatgrass Histamine release 1–2 oz fresh juice on empty stomach. Increase gradually. Powdered: 1–2 tsp daily.

entries.title: Yerba Mate
entries.text: Yerba Mate 🍵 drink drink South America's caffeinated tea — unique trifecta of caffeine, theobromine, and theophylline produces sustained energy with less crash than coffee, and ritualistic communal drinking. Unique stimulant blend — caffeine + theobromine + theophylline Produces energy without jitters, often compared to clean coffee Rich in antioxidants — higher than green tea Supports fat oxidation and weight management Improves physical performance and reduces fatigue Immune-boosting saponins and polyphenols Caffeine + theobromine combination provides longer half-life energy curve Saponins modulate cholesterol absorption Polyphenols (chlorogenic and quinic acids) activate AMPK — fat oxidation Loose-leaf yerba mate (cured in gourd) — traditional preparation Bottled yerba mate (cold-brewed) — less PAH content Roasted vs green leaf — different flavor profiles Mint or citrus — flavor balance With breakfast (not on empty stomach if sensitive) Bombilla straw and gourd for traditional method Higher PAH (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon) content in smoked/cured leaves — esophageal cancer link in heavy traditional drinkers Caffeine sensitivity Stains teeth High-temperature drinking (the traditional way) is the cancer-link risk, not the leaf itself 1–2 cups (8 oz) daily, ideally cool to warm (not boiling). 100–200 mg caffeine equivalent per cup.

entries.title: Chia Seeds
entries.text: Chia Seeds ⚫ food food supp Aztec endurance food — when soaked, the mucilage forms a gel that slows hydration loss and glucose absorption simultaneously. Highest plant omega-3 per gram. Richest plant source of omega-3 ALA — higher than flaxseed per gram Complete protein containing all essential amino acids Absorb 10x their weight in water — promotes sustained hydration and satiety Excellent source of calcium — more per gram than dairy milk Soluble fiber lowers LDL cholesterol and stabilizes blood sugar Rich in antioxidants that prevent rancidity of the healthy fats ALA → small percentage converts to EPA/DHA (5–10%) Mucilage gel slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption Calcium and magnesium ratio favorable for bone metabolism Whole seeds (don't need grinding — chia is digestible whole) Soaked overnight to release mucilage and reduce phytic acid Black or white seeds — minimal nutritional difference Coconut milk + cinnamon → chia pudding Lemon water + chia — Aztec endurance drink ('iskiate') Yogurt and berries Choking risk if eaten dry without sufficient water High fiber — start small to avoid GI distress Anticoagulants — bleeding risk from omega-3 + saponins Diverticulitis caution 1–2 tbsp (15–30 g) daily, soaked. ~5 g omega-3 ALA per tbsp.

entries.title: Tuna
entries.text: Tuna 🐟 food food One of the most protein-dense and lowest-calorie protein foods — but the mercury contamination, especially in larger species, demands careful sourcing and portion control. Exceptionally high protein — 25g per 100g with minimal fat Richest food source of omega-3 DHA and EPA for brain and heart health Complete amino acid profile supports muscle repair and growth High in selenium — a critical antioxidant mineral for thyroid health Rich in B12, B3, and B6 for energy and nervous system function Low calorie, high satiety — excellent for weight management DHA forms structural lipid in brain — 60% of brain dry weight EPA reduces triglycerides and inflammation Selenium binds and protects against mercury's effects (partially) Smaller species (skipjack 'light' tuna) — much lower mercury than albacore or bluefin Pole-and-line caught (avoids bycatch) Sashimi-grade for raw applications — frozen first per FDA standards Avocado — healthy fat with brain support Lemon + olive oil — vitamin C reduces mercury toxicity slightly Cilantro/parsley — supports mercury chelation Mercury bioaccumulation — limit albacore to 1x weekly, skipjack 2–3x Pregnancy: skipjack only, 2 servings/week max Histamine — scombroid poisoning from improperly stored fish Sustainability — choose certified options 4–6 oz cooked, 1–2x weekly (skipjack/light). Albacore: max 6 oz weekly. Pregnant: 8 oz weekly skipjack max.

entries.title: Yogurt
entries.text: Yogurt 🥛 food food supp Live fermented milk — the bacteria predigest lactose, raise mineral bioavailability, and produce vitamins and bioactive peptides that fresh milk lacks. Rich in live probiotics — Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains High in calcium and phosphorus for bone strength Complete protein including casein which is slow-digesting for muscle repair B12 and B2 support energy production and red blood cell formation May reduce risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease Fermentation increases bioavailability of nutrients Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus convert lactose to lactic acid Bioactive peptides released during fermentation lower BP (ACE-inhibition) Casein protein digests slowly, providing 6–8 hours of amino acids Full-fat plain (not low-fat) — vitamin K2 and CLA Grass-fed milk source — better fatty acid profile Greek yogurt — strained for higher protein Avoid yogurts with added sugar, gums, modified starches Berries + walnuts + honey — classic breakfast Cucumber + dill + garlic (tzatziki) — Mediterranean classic Probiotic foods stack — kefir on alternate days for diversity Lactose intolerance — strained Greek and aged yogurts have less lactose Dairy sensitivity (casein) — common in autoimmune conditions Histamine intolerance — yogurt is histamine-rich Many commercial yogurts are dessert-level sugar ½–1 cup daily, plain. Pair with low-glycemic toppings to maximize benefits.

entries.title: Alfalfa
entries.text: Alfalfa 🌱 herb herb food A legume traditionally used as livestock fodder ('al-fal-fa' = father of all foods in Arabic) — a deep-rooted plant that pulls minerals up from subsoil, making it nutrient-dense like few other greens. One of the most nutrient-dense plants — contains vitamins A, C, E, K, and full B complex High in plant estrogens (phytoestrogens) that support hormone balance Rich in chlorophyll for blood oxygenation and detoxification Natural diuretic that supports kidney and urinary tract health Alkalizing effect helps balance body pH Saponins may lower LDL cholesterol and support immune function Saponins bind cholesterol in gut, preventing reabsorption Coumestrol (phytoestrogen) modulates estrogen receptors Deep taproot accesses mineral-rich subsoil — high in chlorophyll, vitamin K Leaf and tops (not seed) — leaf is the medicinal/nutritional part Sprouted seeds — high enzyme content (caution: canavanine) Dried leaf tea or capsules Greens combination (with parsley, dandelion) — broad nutrient profile With turmeric and ginger — anti-inflammatory blend In smoothies — mask grassy taste L-canavanine in seeds and sprouts — can trigger lupus or autoimmune flares Anticoagulants — high vitamin K Hormone-sensitive cancers — phytoestrogenic Pregnancy — uterine stimulant in larger doses Leaf tea: 1–2 tsp dried, 1–3 cups daily. Capsules: 500–1000 mg leaf. Sprouts: avoid if autoimmune.

entries.title: Vitamin D
entries.text: Vitamin D vitamin ☀️ #c8922a very-common Affects 1 billion+ people Bone pain, muscle weakness, and frequent fractures severe Chronic fatigue and low energy despite adequate sleep common Depression, mood disorders, and seasonal affective disorder common Impaired immune function — frequent infections and slow healing common Hair loss beyond normal shedding mild Back pain and joint pain mild Rickets in children — soft, deformed bones severe Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) Egg yolks Fortified milk Beef liver Cod liver oil Sun exposure high Get tested via 25-OH vitamin D blood test. Most people need 2,000–5,000 IU daily.

entries.title: Vitamin B12
entries.text: Vitamin B12 vitamin 🔴 #e05c5c very-common Common in vegans, elderly Megaloblastic anemia — fatigue, weakness, pale skin severe Neurological damage — tingling, numbness in hands and feet severe Cognitive decline, memory loss, and brain fog common Depression and mood instability common Glossitis — swollen, inflamed tongue common Mouth ulcers and angular cheilitis mild Vision problems from optic nerve damage severe Meat (beef, liver) Fish and shellfish Eggs Dairy Nutritional yeast (fortified) B12 supplements high Neurological damage from B12 deficiency can be permanent. Vegans must supplement.

entries.title: Vitamin C
entries.text: Vitamin C vitamin 🍊 #8fbe6b common Common in low-fruit diets Scurvy — bleeding gums, loose teeth, skin hemorrhages severe Slow wound healing and easy bruising common Frequent infections and poor immune response common Fatigue and irritability common Dry, rough, bumpy skin (keratosis pilaris) mild Joint pain and swelling mild Iron deficiency anemia (C aids iron absorption) common Bell peppers Citrus fruits Strawberries Broccoli Kale Kiwi med Smokers need 35mg more per day. Stress rapidly depletes vitamin C stores.

entries.title: Vitamin A
entries.text: Vitamin A vitamin 🥕 #c8922a common Leading cause of childhood blindness globally Night blindness — difficulty seeing in low light severe Xerophthalmia — dry eyes, corneal damage, blindness severe Impaired immune function and increased infection risk common Dry, scaly skin and keratosis pilaris common Delayed growth in children severe Infertility and reproductive issues common Increased risk of acne and skin conditions mild Liver (richest source) Sweet potato Carrots Kale and spinach Eggs Dairy med Preformed vitamin A from animal sources is readily absorbed. Beta-carotene from plants requires conversion.

entries.title: Vitamin K2
entries.text: Vitamin K2 vitamin 🥬 #8fbe6b very-common Widely underappreciated deficiency Arterial calcification — calcium depositing in arteries instead of bones severe Osteoporosis and low bone density despite calcium intake severe Easy bruising and impaired blood clotting common Tooth decay and dental problems common Heart disease risk from arterial plaque severe Natto (fermented soybeans — richest source) Hard cheese Egg yolks Butter from grass-fed cows Sauerkraut Liver high K2 directs calcium to bones and teeth, not arteries. Works synergistically with vitamin D3 and magnesium.

entries.title: Vitamin B6
entries.text: Vitamin B6 vitamin 🧬 #9b7de8 common Common in processed food diets Peripheral neuropathy — numbness and tingling in extremities severe Depression and anxiety from impaired serotonin synthesis common Microcytic anemia — fatigue and weakness common Weakened immune system common Cracked lips and skin inflammation around the mouth mild Confusion and cognitive impairment common Poultry Fish (tuna, salmon) Potatoes Bananas Chickpeas Fortified cereals med B6 is essential for over 100 enzyme reactions including neurotransmitter synthesis.

entries.title: Folate (B9)
entries.text: Folate (B9) vitamin 🌿 #8fbe6b common Critical in pregnancy Neural tube defects in unborn babies (spina bifida, anencephaly) severe Megaloblastic anemia — large, immature red blood cells severe Fatigue, weakness, and shortness of breath common Mouth sores and tongue inflammation common Elevated homocysteine — cardiovascular disease risk severe Depression and cognitive impairment common Dark leafy greens (spinach, kale) Legumes (lentils, beans) Asparagus Avocado Eggs Fortified grains high Women of childbearing age should supplement with 400-800mcg daily. MTHFR gene variants affect folate metabolism.

entries.title: Magnesium
entries.text: Magnesium mineral 💎 #5b9bd5 very-common Up to 75% of people deficient Muscle cramps, twitches, and restless legs at night common Anxiety, irritability, and inability to relax common Insomnia and poor sleep quality common Heart arrhythmias and palpitations severe Migraines and frequent headaches common High blood pressure and cardiovascular risk severe Constipation and poor digestion mild Fatigue and low energy despite rest common Pumpkin seeds Dark chocolate Spinach and leafy greens Almonds Avocado Black beans Whole grains high Involved in 300+ enzymatic reactions. Stress and alcohol deplete magnesium rapidly. Glycinate form absorbs best.

entries.title: Iron
entries.text: Iron mineral 🔩 #e05c5c very-common Most common deficiency worldwide Iron deficiency anemia — extreme fatigue, pale skin, shortness of breath severe Brittle nails and hair loss common Cold hands and feet from poor circulation common Pica — cravings for ice, clay, or dirt severe Restless leg syndrome common Impaired cognitive function and concentration common Frequent infections from impaired immune function common Red meat and liver (heme iron) Shellfish (oysters, clams) Spinach Lentils Pumpkin seeds Dark chocolate high Women of reproductive age, vegans, and athletes are highest risk. Vitamin C doubles non-heme iron absorption.

entries.title: Zinc
entries.text: Zinc mineral ⚡ #9b7de8 common 2 billion people affected globally Impaired immune function — frequent colds and slow healing common Loss of taste and smell (anosmia) severe Skin conditions — acne, eczema, psoriasis common Infertility and low testosterone in men severe Hair thinning and loss common Delayed wound healing common Night blindness and eye problems mild Delayed growth and development in children severe Oysters (richest source) Beef and lamb Pumpkin seeds Chickpeas Cashews Fortified cereals high Phytates in grains and legumes block zinc absorption. Meat-based zinc is 2-3x more bioavailable.

entries.title: Calcium
entries.text: Calcium mineral 🦴 #5b9bd5 common Common in dairy-free diets Osteoporosis and stress fractures in adults severe Rickets and poor bone development in children severe Muscle cramps and spasms common Dental problems — cavities, weak enamel common Numbness and tingling in extremities common Heart arrhythmias in severe cases severe Weak, brittle nails mild Dairy (milk, cheese, yogurt) Sardines and canned salmon (with bones) Kale and broccoli Fortified plant milks Chia seeds Almonds med Calcium requires vitamin D and K2 for proper absorption and bone deposition. Too much calcium without cofactors can calcify arteries.

entries.title: Potassium
entries.text: Potassium mineral 🍌 #c8922a very-common 97% of Americans don't meet intake Hypokalemia — dangerous heart rhythm abnormalities severe Muscle weakness, cramps, and paralysis in severe cases severe High blood pressure and cardiovascular disease risk severe Fatigue and constant tiredness common Constipation and digestive issues common Kidney stones (low potassium reduces citrate) common Increased salt sensitivity mild Bananas Sweet potatoes Avocado Spinach White beans Salmon Coconut water high Diuretics, excessive sweating, and vomiting rapidly deplete potassium. Essential for every cell in the body.

entries.title: Selenium
entries.text: Selenium mineral 🌰 #8fbe6b common Soil depletion affects many regions Hypothyroidism and impaired thyroid hormone conversion severe Weakened immune system and poor infection resistance common Muscle weakness and pain (Keshan disease in extreme deficiency) severe Impaired cognitive function common Infertility in men from impaired sperm motility common Increased cancer risk from reduced antioxidant defense severe Brazil nuts (2-3 per day meets daily needs) Tuna and sardines Eggs Chicken Sunflower seeds Brown rice med Too much selenium is also toxic — do not supplement aggressively. 2 Brazil nuts daily is ideal.

entries.title: Iodine
entries.text: Iodine mineral 🧂 #5bbfb5 common Leading cause of preventable brain damage Hypothyroidism — fatigue, weight gain, cold sensitivity, brain fog severe Goiter — enlarged thyroid gland in the neck severe Impaired fetal brain development during pregnancy severe Cognitive impairment and intellectual disability in severe cases severe Hair loss and dry skin common Heavy periods and fertility issues in women common Iodized salt Seaweed (nori, kelp, wakame) Fish and shellfish Dairy Eggs high Fluoride, chlorine, and bromide compete with iodine. Avoid excess raw cruciferous vegetables if iodine is low.

entries.title: Copper
entries.text: Copper mineral 🟤 #c8922a less-common Often missed in testing Anemia unresponsive to iron supplementation severe Bone fragility and increased fracture risk common Neuropathy — weakness and numbness severe Premature gray hair mild Impaired immune function common Poor wound healing common Shellfish (oysters, crab) Liver Dark chocolate Nuts and seeds Mushrooms Avocado med Excess zinc supplementation depletes copper. Copper and zinc must be balanced.

entries.title: Omega-3 (DHA/EPA)
entries.text: Omega-3 (DHA/EPA) fat 🐟 #5bbfb5 very-common Most people severely low Depression, anxiety, and mood instability severe Brain fog and poor cognitive function common Dry, flaky skin and eczema common Poor memory and concentration common Cardiovascular disease — high triglycerides, inflammation severe Joint pain and stiffness from chronic inflammation common Poor eye health and dry eyes common Impaired fetal brain development in pregnancy severe Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines, herring) Fish oil supplements Algae oil (vegan DHA/EPA) Flaxseed (ALA only — poor conversion) Walnuts Chia seeds high ALA from plants converts poorly to DHA/EPA (<5%). Vegans should take algae-based omega-3. Aim for 2g EPA+DHA daily.

entries.title: Omega-6 / Omega-3 Imbalance
entries.text: Omega-6 / Omega-3 Imbalance fat ⚖️ #e05c5c very-common Modern diet is 15-20:1 ratio (should be 4:1) Chronic systemic inflammation — root of most modern disease severe Cardiovascular disease and atherosclerosis severe Autoimmune conditions — RA, lupus, Crohn's severe Obesity and metabolic syndrome common Depression and anxiety common Accelerated aging and cancer risk severe Reduce: vegetable oils (corn, soy, sunflower) Reduce: processed and fried foods Increase: fatty fish Increase: olive oil Increase: grass-fed beef Increase: walnuts high This is not a true deficiency but a ratio imbalance. Reducing omega-6 is as important as increasing omega-3.

entries.title: Vitamin E
entries.text: Vitamin E fat 🌻 #c8922a common 90% of Americans below adequate intake Peripheral neuropathy — nerve damage in hands and feet severe Muscle weakness and coordination problems severe Weakened immune system common Retinopathy — vision problems common Dry, damaged skin accelerating aging mild Hemolytic anemia in infants severe Sunflower seeds Almonds Hazelnuts Wheat germ oil Spinach and Swiss chard Avocado med Fat-soluble vitamin — absorbs best with dietary fat. Mixed tocopherols are more beneficial than synthetic alpha-tocopherol alone.

entries.title: Tryptophan
entries.text: Tryptophan amino 😴 #9b7de8 common Depleted by stress and poor diet Depression from insufficient serotonin synthesis severe Insomnia and inability to fall asleep severe Anxiety and emotional dysregulation common Increased pain sensitivity common Poor appetite regulation mild Cognitive impairment and poor memory common Turkey Eggs Cheese Pumpkin seeds Tofu Salmon Chicken med Tryptophan is the precursor to serotonin and melatonin. Needs B6, zinc, and magnesium as cofactors for conversion.

entries.title: Tyrosine
entries.text: Tyrosine amino ⚡ #9b7de8 less-common Worsened by chronic stress Depression from insufficient dopamine and norepinephrine severe Hypothyroidism — tyrosine is required for thyroid hormone production severe Fatigue, low motivation, and anhedonia common Poor focus and ADHD-like symptoms common Low libido and reduced drive common Chicken and turkey Eggs Dairy Almonds Avocado Bananas Pumpkin seeds med Tyrosine is the precursor to dopamine, adrenaline, and thyroid hormones. Depleted rapidly by stress and illness.

entries.title: Lysine
entries.text: Lysine amino 🧬 #9b7de8 common Common in plant-based diets Recurring cold sores and herpes outbreaks severe Impaired collagen synthesis — slow wound healing common Anxiety and inability to concentrate common Muscle loss and weakness common Impaired calcium absorption and bone loss common Fatigue and low energy mild Meat (beef, pork, chicken) Fish Eggs Dairy Legumes Quinoa med Grains are low in lysine — vegetarians relying heavily on grains may be deficient. Lysine suppresses arginine — helpful for HSV management.

entries.title: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10)
entries.text: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) other ⚡ #5bbfb5 common Depleted by statins and aging Severe fatigue and low cellular energy (mitochondrial dysfunction) severe Heart failure and cardiomyopathy severe Muscle pain and weakness — especially on statin drugs severe Brain fog and cognitive decline with aging common Migraines — CoQ10 deficiency strongly linked common High blood pressure common Organ meats (heart, liver) Fatty fish Beef Pork Spinach Broccoli Peanuts high Statin drugs block CoQ10 synthesis — everyone on statins should supplement 100-300mg daily. Production declines 50% by age 80.

entries.title: Choline
entries.text: Choline other 🧠 #5b9bd5 very-common 90% of people don't meet daily needs Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) severe Cognitive decline and poor memory severe Muscle damage and weakness common Impaired fetal brain development in pregnancy severe Mood disorders and anxiety common Poor nerve signaling and motor function common Eggs (yolk has highest concentration) Liver Beef Fish Soybeans Quinoa Broccoli high Choline is the precursor to acetylcholine — the primary memory and learning neurotransmitter. Essential during pregnancy.

entries.title: Fiber
entries.text: Fiber other 🌾 #8fbe6b very-common 95% of Americans deficient Constipation and irregular bowel movements common Elevated LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk severe Blood sugar spikes and insulin resistance severe Gut dysbiosis — loss of beneficial bacteria severe Increased risk of colorectal cancer severe Obesity and difficulty managing weight common Inflammatory bowel conditions common Legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas) Vegetables (broccoli, artichoke, carrots) Fruit Oats and whole grains Psyllium husk Chia and flaxseed high Soluble fiber feeds gut bacteria and lowers cholesterol. Insoluble fiber adds bulk and speeds gut transit. Increase slowly to avoid gas.

entries.title: Probiotics / Gut Flora
entries.text: Probiotics / Gut Flora other 🦠 #5bbfb5 very-common Antibiotics and diet devastate gut flora Irritable bowel syndrome — bloating, gas, cramping, alternating diarrhea/constipation severe Weakened immune system — recurrent infections severe Anxiety and depression via disrupted gut-brain axis common Food intolerances and sensitivities common Skin conditions — acne, eczema, rosacea common Brain fog and cognitive impairment common Obesity and metabolic dysfunction common Yogurt with live cultures Kefir Sauerkraut Kimchi Kombucha Miso Tempeh high One course of antibiotics can disrupt gut flora for up to 2 years. Diversity of species is as important as quantity.

entries.title: Glutathione
entries.text: Glutathione other 🛡️ #8fbe6b common Depleted by toxins, stress, aging Increased oxidative stress and cellular damage severe Impaired liver detoxification severe Weakened immune system common Chronic fatigue and low energy common Accelerated aging and neurodegeneration severe Increased cancer risk from free radical damage severe Sulfur-rich foods: garlic, onion, broccoli Whey protein Avocado Spinach Asparagus Raw eggs med The body synthesizes glutathione from cysteine, glycine, and glutamate. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the most effective precursor supplement.

entries.title: Peanut Allergy
entries.text: Peanut Allergy 🥜 top9 #c8922a high One of the most common and severe food allergies worldwide Top allergen Anaphylaxis risk Hidden in many foods Peanut allergy is an immune response to peanut proteins (Ara h1, Ara h2, Ara h3). Unlike many childhood allergies, peanut allergy typically persists into adulthood. Even trace amounts can trigger severe reactions in sensitive individuals. Anaphylaxis — throat closing, difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure severe Hives, skin flushing, and itching common Swelling of lips, tongue, and throat severe Vomiting, nausea, and stomach cramps common Runny nose and watery eyes mild Peanuts and peanut butter Peanut oil (cold-pressed — refined is usually safe) Mixed nuts Trail mix Many Asian sauces (satay, pad thai) Some baked goods and candy bars African, Asian, and Mexican cuisines often use peanut Lupin flour can cross-react Some sunflower butters processed in peanut facilities Certain pet foods Tree nuts (if no cross-allergy) Sunflower seed butter Tahini (sesame seed butter) Pumpkin seed butter Always carry an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen). Inform restaurants. Read all labels — peanut must be declared on food packaging. var(--red)

entries.title: Tree Nut Allergy
entries.text: Tree Nut Allergy 🌰 top9 #c8922a high Almonds, cashews, walnuts, pistachios and more Top allergen Often lifelong Cross-contamination risk Tree nut allergy covers a range of nuts: almonds, walnuts, cashews, pistachios, hazelnuts, Brazil nuts, pecans, and macadamia. You may be allergic to one or all. Cross-contamination is a major concern in manufacturing. Anaphylaxis in severe cases severe Hives and skin reactions common Oral allergy syndrome — tingling and itching in mouth mild Vomiting and abdominal pain common Swelling of face and throat severe All tree nuts (almonds, walnuts, cashews, pistachios, hazelnuts, Brazil, pecan, macadamia) Nut butters and nut oils Marzipan (almond paste) Pralines Many granolas and trail mixes Pesto (pine nuts) Natural flavors may include nut extracts Some cheeses aged with nuts Coconut (technically a tree nut — check with allergist) Many Asian desserts Amaretto liqueur (almond) Peanut butter (unless also peanut allergic) Seeds (sunflower, pumpkin, hemp) Coconut (if cleared by allergist) Carry epinephrine. Even if allergic to only one tree nut, cross-contamination risk means avoiding all tree nut products in high-risk situations. var(--red)

entries.title: Gluten Intolerance / Celiac
entries.text: Gluten Intolerance / Celiac 🌾 top9 #e05c5c high Immune reaction to gluten — damages small intestine Celiac disease NCGS Autoimmune Lifelong Celiac disease is an autoimmune condition where gluten (found in wheat, barley, rye) triggers immune damage to the small intestinal lining. Non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) causes similar symptoms without autoimmune damage. Both require gluten elimination. Celiac: intestinal damage causing nutrient malabsorption severe Chronic diarrhea, constipation, and bloating common Fatigue and brain fog from nutrient deficiency common Skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis) in celiac common Joint pain, mouth ulcers, and headaches mild Depression and anxiety — gut-brain connection common Anemia from iron malabsorption common Wheat (all varieties: spelt, kamut, farro, durum) Barley Rye Triticale Malt (from barley) Most bread, pasta, and cereals Beer (unless gluten-free) Many sauces and soups (wheat as thickener) Soy sauce (contains wheat — use tamari) Communion wafers Some medications and supplements Lipstick and lip balm Oats (cross-contamination — use certified GF oats) Deli meats and sausages Rice and rice pasta Quinoa Corn and polenta Potatoes and sweet potatoes Certified gluten-free oats Buckwheat (despite name, not wheat) Amaranth and millet Celiac requires strict lifelong elimination — even trace amounts cause damage. Get tested before going gluten-free as elimination changes test accuracy. var(--red)

entries.title: Dairy / Lactose
entries.text: Dairy / Lactose 🥛 top9 #5b9bd5 med Lactose intolerance (enzyme) vs milk protein allergy (immune) — very different conditions Lactose intolerance Milk allergy Casein sensitivity Very common Two distinct conditions: Lactose intolerance is a digestive issue (missing lactase enzyme) causing GI symptoms. Milk allergy is an immune response to milk proteins (casein or whey) — can cause anaphylaxis. Know which you have — management differs significantly. Milk allergy: hives, vomiting, anaphylaxis severe Lactose intolerance: bloating, gas, diarrhea 30–120 min after dairy common Eczema and skin reactions (especially in children) common Mucus production and congestion mild Abdominal cramping common Milk, cream, butter, and ghee Cheese (all types for allergy; hard cheeses often tolerated in intolerance) Ice cream and yogurt Whey and casein protein powders Most baked goods Many protein bars Lactose in medications and supplements (as filler) Deli meats and hot dogs Margarine (often contains dairy) Caramel and chocolate Non-dairy creamers (may contain casein) Many sauces and soups Lactase enzyme drops or tablets (for intolerance) Aged hard cheeses (most lactose removed — often tolerated) Coconut, almond, oat, soy milk Dairy-free cheese and yogurt Ghee (casein removed — often tolerated in intolerance) Lactose intolerance: lactase enzyme supplements before meals allow dairy consumption. Milk allergy: strict avoidance required — carry epinephrine if severe. var(--blue)

entries.title: Egg Allergy
entries.text: Egg Allergy 🥚 top9 #c8922a high Common in children, often outgrown by adulthood Top allergen Often outgrown Baking challenge Egg allergy is most common in children — about 70% outgrow it by age 16. Reactions can be to egg white proteins (albumin, ovalbumin) or egg yolk. White allergy is more common. Cooking partially denatures proteins, so some tolerate cooked eggs but not raw. Skin reactions: hives, eczema, swelling common Gastrointestinal: nausea, vomiting, cramps common Respiratory: runny nose, asthma symptoms common Anaphylaxis in severe cases severe Eggs in any form Mayonnaise Meringue and custard Most baked goods (cakes, cookies, muffins) Egg noodles and pasta Caesar dressing Many breaded and battered foods Albumin, globulin, lysozyme on labels Flu vaccines (grown in egg medium) Some pasta (fresh, not dried) Lecithin (soy lecithin is safe; egg lecithin is not) Foam in coffee drinks (some cafes use egg white foam) Many sauces including hollandaise and aioli Flax egg (1 tbsp ground flax + 3 tbsp water) Chia egg (same ratio) Aquafaba (chickpea water — excellent egg white substitute) Commercial egg replacers Applesauce or banana in baking Check flu and yellow fever vaccine safety with your allergist. Many people with egg allergy can receive MMR vaccine (not grown in egg). var(--accent)

entries.title: Shellfish Allergy
entries.text: Shellfish Allergy 🦐 top9 #9b7de8 high Crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster) and molluscs (clams, oysters) Top allergen Often adult-onset Lifelong Anaphylaxis risk Shellfish allergy is one of the most common adult-onset allergies and rarely outgrown. Two categories: crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster, crayfish) and molluscs (clams, oysters, scallops, mussels, squid). You may react to one or both categories. Anaphylaxis — often severe and rapid onset severe Hives, itching, and skin flushing common Vomiting, diarrhea, and stomach pain common Swelling of lips, throat, and tongue severe Wheezing and shortness of breath severe Shrimp, crab, lobster, crayfish Clams, oysters, mussels, scallops Squid and octopus Abalone Fish sauce and oyster sauce Worcestershire sauce (anchovies — not shellfish but often confused) Many Asian dishes and sauces Caesar salad dressing (anchovies — different from shellfish) Bouillabaisse and seafood soups Restaurant cross-contamination from shared fryers Glucosamine supplements (from shellfish shells) Some calcium supplements Fish (separate allergy — many shellfish allergic people tolerate fish) Seaweed and algae Plant-based seafood alternatives Shellfish allergy is often severe. Carry epinephrine. Be cautious at seafood restaurants even ordering non-shellfish — cross-contamination on grills and fryers is common. var(--purple)

entries.title: Fish Allergy
entries.text: Fish Allergy 🐟 top9 #5bbfb5 high Salmon, tuna, cod, halibut and other finfish Top allergen Distinct from shellfish Often lifelong Fish allergy is separate from shellfish allergy — you may have one or both. Common culprit proteins include parvalbumin found in many species. You may be allergic to all fish or specific types. Often lifelong and can be severe. Anaphylaxis in severe cases severe Hives, itching, and skin reactions common Nausea, vomiting, and GI distress common Nasal symptoms and asthma from cooking fumes common Oral allergy syndrome mild Salmon, tuna, cod, halibut, tilapia, bass Anchovies, sardines, herring Fish sauce and Worcestershire sauce Caesar dressing (anchovies) Surimi (imitation crab — often made from fish) Omega-3 fish oil supplements Bouillabaisse and seafood soups Some Asian sauces and stocks Cooking fumes from fish can trigger reactions in highly sensitive people Some pet foods Isinglass used in some beer and wine clarification Shellfish (if not also allergic) Algae-based omega-3 supplements (vegan DHA/EPA) Seaweed and algae for iodine Algae-based omega-3 is the ideal alternative for fish-allergic people needing DHA and EPA. Get specific allergy testing to determine which fish species you react to. var(--teal)

entries.title: Soy Allergy
entries.text: Soy Allergy 🫘 top9 #8fbe6b med Common in infants, often outgrown — soy is in nearly everything processed Top allergen Often outgrown Widespread in processed food Soy allergy is an immune response to soy proteins. More common in infants and children — about 70% outgrow it. Soy is one of the most pervasive ingredients in the food supply — it appears in hundreds of processed foods under various names. Anaphylaxis (less common than peanut/shellfish but possible) severe Hives, eczema, and itching common Vomiting, gas, and diarrhea common Runny nose and asthma symptoms mild Eczema in infants common Soybeans and edamame Tofu, tempeh, and miso Soy sauce and tamari Soy milk and soy yogurt Soy protein isolate and concentrate Textured vegetable protein (TVP) Soy lecithin (usually tolerated — protein level very low) Vegetable broth and flavoring Many protein bars and meal replacements Some medications use soy oil Asian cuisine broadly Many deli meats and processed meats Coconut aminos (soy sauce replacement) Pea protein Hemp seeds and hemp protein Rice and oat milk Soy lecithin is usually safe even with soy allergy — highly refined with minimal protein. Confirm with your allergist. Highly refined soy oil is also often tolerated. var(--green)

entries.title: Wheat Allergy
entries.text: Wheat Allergy 🍞 top9 #c8922a high Immune response to wheat proteins — different from celiac disease Top allergen Distinct from celiac IgE-mediated Wheat allergy is an IgE-mediated immune response to wheat proteins (not exclusively gluten). Unlike celiac (autoimmune), wheat allergy can cause anaphylaxis. Exercise-induced wheat allergy (WDEIA) is a unique form triggered by exercise after eating wheat. Anaphylaxis — can be life-threatening severe Hives, swelling, and skin reactions common Vomiting and stomach cramps common Asthma and respiratory symptoms common Exercise-induced anaphylaxis after wheat consumption (WDEIA) severe Wheat in all forms (bread, pasta, flour, crackers) Spelt, kamut, farro, durum, semolina Wheat starch and wheat bran Many sauces and soups Beer and malt beverages Most baked goods Modified food starch (may be wheat) Natural flavors Surimi Soy sauce (wheat-based) Many Asian noodles Licorice candy Some medications Rice and rice flour Corn and cornmeal Quinoa, amaranth, buckwheat Oats (certified wheat-free — different from GF) Potatoes and sweet potatoes Bean flours Unlike celiac, some people with wheat allergy tolerate other gluten-containing grains (barley, rye). Get specific allergy testing to clarify exact triggers. var(--accent)

entries.title: Sesame Allergy
entries.text: Sesame Allergy 🌱 top9 #c86b8f high Now a top 9 US allergen — common in Middle Eastern and Asian foods Top 9 allergen (2023) Growing prevalence Hidden in many foods Sesame became the 9th major allergen in the US in 2023. It is widely used in Middle Eastern, Asian, and health food cuisines. Reactions can be severe and anaphylaxis is possible. Cross-reactivity with nuts and seeds is common. Anaphylaxis in severe cases severe Hives, itching, and skin flushing common Vomiting and GI distress common Swelling of lips and throat severe Sesame seeds (whole, ground, or hulled) Tahini (sesame paste) Sesame oil Hummus (contains tahini) Halvah Gochujang (Korean paste) Many Asian and Middle Eastern dishes Burger buns with sesame seeds Breadsticks and crackers Health bars and granola Some supplements and vitamins Cosmetics and skin care products Falafel Baba ganoush Sunflower seed butter Pumpkin seed butter Hemp seeds (test carefully) Coconut aminos As of 2023, sesame must be declared on US food labels. Check labels carefully as reformulation lags behind labeling requirements. var(--pink)

entries.title: Pork Allergy & Intolerance
entries.text: Pork Allergy & Intolerance 🐷 meat #e05c5c med Alpha-gal syndrome, pork-cat syndrome, and histamine sensitivity Alpha-gal Pork-Cat syndrome Tick-triggered Histamine Pork reactions can come from several different mechanisms: Alpha-gal syndrome (from tick bite), pork-cat syndrome (cross-reaction with cat dander), histamine intolerance, or fat intolerance. Identifying the mechanism determines management. Alpha-gal: delayed anaphylaxis 2–6 hours after eating pork or red meat severe Pork-cat: immediate oral allergy symptoms in cat-allergic people common Histamine: flushing, headache, hives from processed pork common Fat intolerance: nausea, bloating, slow digestion mild Sulfite sensitivity (from cured meats): headache, breathing difficulty common Pork and pork products for alpha-gal (also beef, lamb, venison) All red meat for confirmed alpha-gal Cured pork (bacon, ham, sausage) for histamine sensitivity Processed pork for sulfite sensitivity Pork gelatin in capsules, gummies, and marshmallows Lard in tortillas, refried beans, pastries Pork enzymes in some cheeses Pork-based broths and stocks Collagen supplements (often pork-derived) Poultry and fish (safe for alpha-gal) Plant proteins (all safe for alpha-gal) Fresh unprocessed pork (lower histamine) Kosher or halal alternatives Alpha-gal syndrome is diagnosed with a specific blood test (alpha-gal IgE). Caused by Lone Star tick bite. Reactions are delayed — making it notoriously difficult to identify without testing. var(--red)

entries.title: Chicken Allergy
entries.text: Chicken Allergy 🐔 meat #c8922a med Less common but can cross-react with egg and feather allergy Bird-egg syndrome Cross-reactive Less common Chicken allergy is uncommon but can occur. Bird-egg syndrome occurs when people allergic to bird feathers (including parakeets, parrots) develop sensitivity to chicken and eggs due to cross-reacting serum albumin proteins. Hives and skin reactions after eating chicken common GI distress — nausea and stomach pain common Respiratory symptoms in bird-feather sensitive people common Anaphylaxis in severe cases severe Chicken in all forms Turkey (may cross-react) Eggs (bird-egg syndrome) Bone broth made from chicken Chicken-based stock and soups Chicken broth in many soups and sauces Chicken fat in some products Hidden chicken powder in seasoning blends Beef, pork, and lamb (unrelated proteins) Fish and seafood Plant proteins Bird-egg syndrome is the main mechanism linking chicken allergy to egg allergy and pet bird exposure. Test for both if you have respiratory symptoms around birds and reactions to chicken or eggs. var(--accent)

entries.title: Lactose Intolerance
entries.text: Lactose Intolerance 🧀 intolerance #5b9bd5 low Missing lactase enzyme — not an allergy, but very common Not an allergy Digestive Very common Manageable Lactose intolerance is the inability to digest lactose (milk sugar) due to insufficient lactase enzyme. Extremely common — affects up to 68% of adults globally. Not dangerous, but causes significant discomfort. Different from milk protein allergy. Bloating and gas within 30 minutes to 2 hours of dairy common Diarrhea and loose stools common Abdominal cramping and pain common Nausea (less common) mild Milk (whole, low-fat, skim) Ice cream and soft-serve Soft cheeses (ricotta, cottage cheese, cream cheese) Yogurt (regular — Greek is lower lactose) Cream soups and sauces Prescription medications (lactose as filler) Deli meats (milk solids added) Margarine and butter spreads Instant mashed potatoes Processed cereals Some bread products Hard aged cheeses (cheddar, parmesan, swiss — lactose mostly gone) Lactase enzyme tablets before eating dairy Lactose-free milk and dairy Greek yogurt (lower lactose) All plant milks Lactase enzyme supplements (Lactaid) allow most people with lactose intolerance to consume dairy without symptoms. Tolerance varies — experiment with quantities. var(--blue)

entries.title: Histamine Intolerance
entries.text: Histamine Intolerance 🍷 intolerance #9b7de8 med Inability to break down histamine in food — mimics allergy symptoms Not a true allergy DAO enzyme deficiency Fermented foods Often misdiagnosed Histamine intolerance occurs when the body cannot break down dietary histamine fast enough, often due to low diamine oxidase (DAO) enzyme activity. Causes allergy-like symptoms but is not an IgE-mediated immune response. Often misdiagnosed as multiple food allergies. Headaches and migraines — often after red wine or aged cheese common Flushing, hives, and skin itching common Runny nose and sneezing common Irregular heartbeat and palpitations common GI symptoms — diarrhea, nausea, stomach pain common Fatigue and brain fog mild Aged and fermented cheeses Alcohol (especially red wine and beer) Fermented foods (sauerkraut, kimchi, vinegar) Cured and smoked meats (bacon, salami) Canned fish (tuna, sardines, anchovies) Leftovers (histamine increases as food sits) Tomatoes, spinach, avocado, eggplant Vinegar in salad dressings Soy sauce and Worcestershire sauce Many sauces and condiments Dried fruits Chocolate and cocoa Yeast and yeast extracts (Marmite) Fresh meat (cook and eat immediately) Fresh fish Fresh vegetables (most) Rice, oats, and quinoa Coconut milk Fresh herbs DAO enzyme supplements taken before meals can reduce histamine reactions. Vitamin C, B6, and copper support DAO activity. Keep a food diary to identify your personal trigger threshold. var(--purple)

entries.title: FODMAP Sensitivity
entries.text: FODMAP Sensitivity 🫁 intolerance #5bbfb5 med Fermentable carbs trigger IBS symptoms — not an allergy IBS SIBO Digestive Temporary elimination FODMAP sensitivity means certain fermentable carbohydrates (fructose, lactose, fructans, galactans, polyols) are poorly absorbed and fermented by gut bacteria, causing gas, bloating, and pain. Common in people with IBS and SIBO. Severe bloating and distension after eating common Gas and flatulence common Abdominal pain and cramping common Alternating diarrhea and constipation common Nausea in severe cases mild Onion and garlic (fructans — very high FODMAP) Wheat and rye Apples, pears, peaches, mangoes Most legumes (fructans and GOS) Milk and soft cheeses (lactose) Cauliflower, mushrooms, artichokes Sugar alcohols (sorbitol, xylitol, mannitol) Garlic and onion powder in seasoning mixes Inulin and chicory root in 'high fiber' products Honey and agave (high fructose) Many protein bars (use inulin as fiber) Fruit juices concentrate Rice, oats, quinoa Chicken, fish, eggs, meat Firm tofu Low-FODMAP vegetables: carrots, cucumber, spinach, zucchini, bell pepper Blueberries, strawberries, oranges Lactose-free dairy Aged cheeses The low-FODMAP diet is a 3-phase protocol (elimination, reintroduction, personalization). Best done with a dietitian. Most people identify specific trigger FODMAPs and don't need to avoid all of them permanently. var(--teal)

entries.title: Nightshade Sensitivity
entries.text: Nightshade Sensitivity 🍅 intolerance #e05c5c low Tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, potatoes — linked to inflammation Autoimmune Inflammation Not proven allergy Joint pain Nightshade sensitivity is not a clinically recognized allergy but many people — particularly those with autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis — report symptom improvement when avoiding nightshades. Alkaloids (solanine, capsaicin) are thought to be the trigger. Joint pain and inflammation — particularly in autoimmune conditions common Digestive upset and IBS-like symptoms mild Skin flares in people with eczema or psoriasis mild Heartburn and acid reflux (tomatoes) mild Tomatoes and tomato products Peppers (bell, chili, paprika) Eggplant White potatoes (sweet potatoes are fine) Goji berries Tobacco Paprika and cayenne in spice blends Tomato paste and puree in many sauces Potato starch as a thickener Ketchup and hot sauce Sweet potatoes and yams All other vegetables Citrus and other fruits Mushrooms and brassicas Evidence for nightshade intolerance is anecdotal but strong in autoimmune communities. Try a 30-day elimination to assess personal response. Sweet potatoes are not nightshades despite the name. var(--red)

entries.title: Sulfite Sensitivity
entries.text: Sulfite Sensitivity 🍾 other #c8922a med Preservatives in wine, dried fruit, and processed food Asthma trigger Wine sensitivity Processed food Not an allergy Sulfite sensitivity is more common in people with asthma. Sulfites (sulfur dioxide, sodium bisulfite) are used as preservatives in wine, dried fruit, and processed foods. True allergy is rare — most reactions are intolerance or asthma-triggered. Asthma attack or wheezing — most common reaction severe Headache and flushing after red wine common Hives and skin reactions common Nausea and stomach upset mild Dried fruits (highest sulfite levels) Wine (especially white and rosé) Beer Vinegar Canned vegetables Shrimp and seafood (often treated with sulfites) Processed meats Bottled lemon and lime juice Restaurant salad bars (sulfites on lettuce) Maraschino cherries Grape juice Fruit toppings on desserts Some medications Fresh fruits and vegetables Fresh meat and fish Organic wine (lower sulfite levels — not sulfite-free) Homemade foods without preservatives Sulfites must be declared on food labels when present at 10 ppm or more. Molybdenum supplementation may help sulfite-sensitive individuals process sulfites more efficiently. var(--accent)

entries.title: Fructose Malabsorption
entries.text: Fructose Malabsorption 🍎 intolerance #8fbe6b low Unable to absorb fructose efficiently — different from hereditary fructose intolerance IBS-linked Fruit sensitivity Sugar intolerance Common Fructose malabsorption is when the gut cannot absorb fructose quickly enough, leading to it reaching the colon and being fermented by bacteria. Very common and often overlaps with IBS. Different from hereditary fructose intolerance (a rare serious metabolic disorder). Bloating and gas after fruit or sweet foods common Diarrhea and loose stools common Abdominal cramping common Fatigue and brain fog (in some people) mild High-fructose fruits: apples, pears, mangoes, watermelon, cherries Honey and agave High-fructose corn syrup Dried fruits Fruit juices Agave in health food products Honey in many dressings and sauces HFCS in sodas, condiments, and processed food Sorbitol (converts to fructose in gut) Lower-fructose fruits: berries, citrus, bananas (small), kiwi Glucose-containing foods (glucose aids fructose absorption) Vegetables Whole grains Eating fructose together with glucose (equal or greater amounts) significantly improves absorption. Strawberries and table sugar (50/50 glucose-fructose) are better tolerated than pure fructose sources like apples. var(--green)

entries.title: Ketogenic (Keto)
entries.text: Ketogenic (Keto) 🥑 lowcarb #c8922a Very low carb, high fat — forces the body into fat-burning ketosis Weight loss good Brain fuel good Restrictive hard Diabetes management good The ketogenic diet drastically reduces carbohydrate intake to under 50g per day, forcing the liver to produce ketones from fat as the primary fuel source. Most effective for rapid fat loss and blood sugar control. Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) Meat and poultry Eggs Avocado and olive oil Cheese and butter Low-carb vegetables (spinach, broccoli, zucchini) Nuts and seeds Heavy cream Bread, pasta, rice Fruit (except small amounts of berries) Sugar and sweets Grains and legumes Root vegetables (potatoes, carrots) Most alcohol Rapid fat loss — especially visceral fat Reduces blood sugar and insulin levels dramatically Improves brain clarity and focus for many people Reduces triglycerides and increases HDL cholesterol Effective for epilepsy — original medical use May reduce inflammation Best for: people with type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance, those who want rapid fat loss, people with epilepsy, or those who do well mentally on high fat. Requires commitment — not ideal for competitive endurance athletes. var(--accent)

entries.title: Carnivore
entries.text: Carnivore 🥩 lowcarb #e05c5c Animal products only — zero plant foods Elimination diet good Autoimmune good Very restrictive hard Zero fiber hard The carnivore diet consists exclusively of animal products — meat, fish, eggs, and some dairy. Often used as an elimination protocol to identify food sensitivities or for autoimmune conditions. Beef (especially fatty cuts — ribeye, brisket) Lamb and pork Organ meats (liver, heart — nutrient dense) Fatty fish Eggs Butter and tallow Bacon Hard cheeses (optional) All plants — vegetables, fruits, grains Legumes and nuts Sugar and sweeteners Seed and vegetable oils Any processed food Eliminates most common food triggers and allergens Reduces autoimmune symptoms in many people Simplifies eating — no tracking needed May reduce inflammation from plant antinutrients Rapid improvement in digestive issues for some Best for: people with severe autoimmune conditions, gut issues, or as a short-term elimination protocol. Not recommended long-term without monitoring. Lacks fiber and plant antioxidants — supplement carefully. var(--red)

entries.title: Mediterranean
entries.text: Mediterranean 🫒 cultural #8fbe6b Whole foods, olive oil, fish, and vegetables — most studied diet in the world Longevity good Heart health good Sustainable good Flexible neutral Modeled after traditional diets of Mediterranean countries. Emphasizes whole foods, healthy fats, lean protein, and minimal processed food. Consistently ranked the healthiest overall diet in research. Olive oil (primary fat) Fish and seafood (2–3x/week) Vegetables and leafy greens (daily) Legumes (lentils, chickpeas) Whole grains (farro, bulgur, whole wheat) Fresh fruit Nuts and seeds Moderate red wine (optional) Some poultry and eggs Processed and packaged foods Added sugars Red meat (limited to 1–2x/week) Refined grains (white bread, white pasta) Butter (replaced by olive oil) Reduces cardiovascular disease risk by 30% Improves longevity and healthspan Reduces inflammation throughout the body Supports brain health and reduces Alzheimer's risk Sustainable and enjoyable long-term Improves blood sugar control without restriction Best for: almost everyone — especially those focused on long-term health, heart disease prevention, and sustainable eating. One of the few diets that is both evidence-based and genuinely enjoyable. var(--green)

entries.title: Intermittent Fasting (IF)
entries.text: Intermittent Fasting (IF) ⏱️ fasting #9b7de8 Time-restricted eating — not what you eat, but when Metabolic reset good Flexible good Weight loss good Difficult to start hard Intermittent fasting is an eating pattern that cycles between fasting and eating windows. Common protocols: 16:8 (fast 16h, eat 8h), 18:6, OMAD (one meal a day), and 5:2 (eat normally 5 days, restrict 2 days). Any whole foods during eating window High protein to preserve muscle Healthy fats for satiety Vegetables and fiber-rich foods Black coffee and tea are allowed during fasting Calories during fasting window (including milk in coffee) Processed foods and sugar during eating window Breaking fast with high-carb, high-sugar foods Triggers autophagy — cellular cleanup and renewal Improves insulin sensitivity dramatically Promotes fat burning — especially visceral fat Reduces inflammation and oxidative stress Simplifies meal planning May extend lifespan through metabolic pathways Best for: people wanting to lose fat without counting calories, improve metabolic health, or experiment with longevity protocols. Not ideal during pregnancy, for people with a history of eating disorders, or underweight individuals. var(--purple)

entries.title: Paleo
entries.text: Paleo 🦴 lowcarb #c8922a Eat like our ancestors — whole, unprocessed foods only No processed food good Anti-inflammatory good No grains/dairy hard Moderate carbs neutral The paleo diet mimics the diet of Paleolithic ancestors — whole meats, fish, vegetables, fruits, nuts, and seeds. Eliminates all processed foods, grains, legumes, and dairy. Grass-fed beef and wild game Fish and seafood Eggs Vegetables (all types) Fruits Nuts and seeds Coconut and avocado oils Sweet potatoes and root vegetables Grains (wheat, rice, oats, corn) Legumes (beans, lentils, peanuts) Dairy products Refined sugar Processed oils (canola, soybean) Any packaged or processed foods Eliminates most inflammatory and processed foods Improves blood sugar stability Supports gut health by removing common irritants Naturally high in protein and healthy fats Often results in weight loss without counting calories Reduces autoimmune symptoms for many people Best for: people sensitive to grains or dairy, those with autoimmune conditions, or anyone wanting to clean up their diet without complex tracking. More sustainable than keto for active people. var(--accent)

entries.title: Plant-Based / Vegan
entries.text: Plant-Based / Vegan 🥦 plant #8fbe6b No animal products — all nutrition from plants Environmental good Heart health good B12 supplement needed hard Protein planning required hard A vegan diet eliminates all animal products. When well-planned with complete protein combinations and key supplements, it can be nutritionally complete. Requires attention to B12, iron, zinc, omega-3, and calcium. Legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas) Tofu and tempeh Quinoa and whole grains Nuts, seeds, and nut butters Leafy greens (kale, spinach) Nutritional yeast (B12 source) Fortified plant milks Fruits and vegetables of all kinds All meat, fish, poultry Eggs and dairy Honey (strict veganism) Gelatin and other animal-derived additives Reduces cardiovascular disease risk Lowers LDL cholesterol significantly Associated with lower BMI and cancer risk Rich in fiber and plant antioxidants Reduces environmental footprint May lower inflammation markers Best for: those motivated by ethics or environment, people with cardiovascular risk, or anyone who genuinely enjoys plant foods. Must supplement B12, vitamin D, omega-3 (algae-based), and monitor iron and zinc. var(--green)

entries.title: Vegetarian
entries.text: Vegetarian 🥕 plant #8fbe6b No meat — but may include eggs and dairy Flexible good Heart health good Iron management hard Sustainable good Vegetarianism excludes meat and fish but typically includes eggs and dairy. Easier to achieve nutritional completeness than veganism. Many subtypes: lacto-ovo (eggs + dairy), lacto (dairy only), ovo (eggs only). Eggs and dairy (lacto-ovo) Legumes and beans Whole grains Tofu, tempeh, seitan Nuts and seeds All vegetables and fruits Cheese, yogurt, milk Red meat, poultry, pork Fish and seafood Gelatin and animal-based additives Lower risk of heart disease and type 2 diabetes Easier to meet protein needs than vegan (eggs and dairy help) High in fiber and plant compounds Environmentally more sustainable than omnivore diets Flexible — many cuisines are naturally vegetarian Best for: people who want to reduce meat intake without full commitment to veganism. Good balance of health benefits and practical sustainability. Monitor iron and ensure adequate B12 through eggs/dairy. var(--green)

entries.title: DASH Diet
entries.text: DASH Diet ❤️ therapeutic #e05c5c Designed to lower blood pressure — Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension Blood pressure good Heart health good Doctor recommended good Low sodium neutral The DASH diet was specifically developed to reduce high blood pressure. Emphasizes foods rich in potassium, calcium, and magnesium while reducing sodium. Clinically proven to lower systolic blood pressure by 8–14 mmHg. Fruits (especially potassium-rich — banana, avocado) Vegetables (particularly dark leafy greens) Whole grains Low-fat dairy (calcium source) Lean meats, poultry, fish Nuts and legumes Low-sodium foods High-sodium processed foods Red meat in excess Full-fat dairy Sweets and added sugar Alcohol in excess Clinically proven to reduce blood pressure Reduces cardiovascular disease risk Improves insulin sensitivity Rich in potassium, magnesium, and calcium Sustainable as a lifelong approach Reduces risk of kidney disease Best for: people with hypertension (high blood pressure), cardiovascular disease risk, or those looking for a balanced medically-endorsed diet. Very practical and not overly restrictive. var(--red)

entries.title: FODMAP (Low-FODMAP)
entries.text: FODMAP (Low-FODMAP) 🫁 therapeutic #5b9bd5 Eliminates fermentable carbs that trigger IBS symptoms IBS relief good Gut health good Complex protocol hard Temporary neutral Low-FODMAP eliminates fermentable oligosaccharides, disaccharides, monosaccharides, and polyols — short-chain carbohydrates that cause gas, bloating, and IBS symptoms. Done in phases: elimination, reintroduction, personalization. Rice and oats Chicken, fish, eggs Leafy greens (spinach, kale, bok choy) Carrots, zucchini, eggplant Blueberries, strawberries, oranges Lactose-free dairy Firm tofu Maple syrup and stevia Wheat and rye Onion and garlic (high FODMAP) Most legumes Milk and soft cheese (lactose) Apples, pears, mangoes Cauliflower, mushrooms High-fructose corn syrup Sugar alcohols (sorbitol, xylitol) Reduces IBS symptoms in 75% of people Identifies specific food triggers through reintroduction Reduces bloating, gas, and abdominal pain Improves quality of life for people with IBD Evidence-based — developed at Monash University Best for: people diagnosed with IBS or functional gut disorders. Should be done with dietitian guidance. Not meant to be permanent — reintroduction phase identifies which specific FODMAPs you react to. var(--blue)

entries.title: Whole30
entries.text: Whole30 🎯 therapeutic #c8922a 30-day elimination reset — removes most common food triggers Reset protocol good Elimination good Strict 30 days hard Identifies triggers good A 30-day strict elimination protocol that removes sugar, alcohol, grains, legumes, soy, and dairy. Designed to identify food sensitivities by eliminating inflammatory foods completely, then reintroducing them one at a time.
