Meat, seafood, and eggs Vegetables (all) Fruit (in moderation) Nuts and seeds Healthy fats (olive oil, coconut oil, avocado) Coffee (black only) Sugar and sweeteners (all forms) Alcohol Grains (including whole grains) Legumes Dairy Soy products Processed foods Carrageenan, MSG, sulfites Identifies specific food sensitivities Resets relationship with food Reduces sugar cravings dramatically Improves energy, sleep, and digestion Many report skin improvements Weight loss common (though not the primary goal) Best for: people wanting a structured elimination protocol, those curious about food sensitivities, or those wanting to break sugar and processed food habits. Requires full commitment — any slip resets the clock. var(--accent)

entries.title: MIND Diet
entries.text: MIND Diet 🧠 therapeutic #9b7de8 Mediterranean + DASH — specifically optimized for brain health Dementia prevention good Brain health good Flexible good Long-term neutral MIND (Mediterranean-DASH Intervention for Neurodegenerative Delay) combines Mediterranean and DASH principles with specific emphasis on brain-protective foods. Reduces Alzheimer's risk by up to 53% in research. Green leafy vegetables (daily — spinach, kale) Other vegetables Berries (especially blueberries — 2x/week minimum) Nuts (5x/week) Olive oil (primary cooking fat) Whole grains (3x/day) Fish (1x/week minimum) Beans (4x/week) Poultry (2x/week) Red meat (limit to 4x/week) Butter and margarine Cheese (limit to 1x/week) Pastries and sweets Fried or fast food Reduces Alzheimer's and dementia risk by 35–53% Protects against cognitive decline in aging Reduces oxidative stress in brain tissue Supports neuroplasticity through BDNF Combines best features of two proven diets Flexible and practical to follow Best for: anyone concerned about cognitive aging, Alzheimer's prevention, or brain performance. Particularly important after age 40. Easy to combine with an active lifestyle. var(--purple)

entries.title: Flexitarian
entries.text: Flexitarian 🌿 plant #5bbfb5 Mostly plant-based with occasional meat — flexible vegetarianism Sustainable good Flexible good Health benefits good No strict rules neutral Flexitarianism encourages primarily plant-based eating while allowing occasional meat and animal products. No strict rules — the goal is to dramatically increase plant food intake without complete elimination of meat. Plant protein (beans, lentils, tofu) as primary Vegetables and fruits (variety) Whole grains Nuts and seeds Meat and fish occasionally (1–3x/week) Eggs and dairy freely Processed meat (as much as possible) Highly processed foods Excessive red meat Refined grains and sugar Easier to maintain than strict vegan or vegetarian Retains most health benefits of plant-based diet Reduces meat consumption without complete restriction Lowers cardiovascular disease and cancer risk Environmentally sustainable Very flexible and socially adaptable Best for: people wanting health benefits of plant-based eating without full commitment. Perfect for those who love meat but want to reduce intake. Easiest gateway diet for transitioning to more plant-forward eating. var(--teal)

entries.title: Anti-Inflammatory Diet
entries.text: Anti-Inflammatory Diet 🫐 therapeutic #5bbfb5 Foods that reduce chronic inflammation — root of most disease Chronic disease good Joint pain good Autoimmune good Sustainable good Not a strict protocol but a framework — prioritizing anti-inflammatory foods and eliminating pro-inflammatory ones. Chronic inflammation is the root cause of heart disease, cancer, diabetes, and autoimmune conditions. Fatty fish (omega-3 rich — salmon, mackerel, sardines) Colorful vegetables and fruits (antioxidants) Berries (especially blueberries, tart cherries) Olive oil (oleocanthal — natural anti-inflammatory) Turmeric and ginger Green tea and matcha Dark chocolate (70%+) Nuts, seeds, and avocado Refined carbohydrates and sugar Vegetable/seed oils (corn, soybean, sunflower) Processed and fried food Excessive alcohol Trans fats Red meat in excess Artificial additives and preservatives Reduces C-reactive protein (CRP) — inflammation marker Improves joint pain and stiffness Supports gut microbiome diversity Reduces cardiovascular disease risk Improves skin health and reduces acne May reduce cancer risk through antioxidant activity Best for: anyone with chronic pain, autoimmune conditions, cardiovascular risk, or general aging. Anti-inflammatory eating is beneficial for virtually everyone as a preventive framework. var(--teal)

entries.title: Elemental Diet
entries.text: Elemental Diet 💉 therapeutic #e05c5c Pre-digested liquid nutrition — for severe gut disorders Medical use neutral Crohn's / IBD good SIBO good Doctor supervised hard The elemental diet consists of pre-digested nutrients in liquid form that require no digestion. Used medically for Crohn's disease, severe IBS, SIBO, and post-surgery recovery. Not for general use. Amino acid-based formula drinks Elemental formula products (Vivonex, Tolerex, etc.) Electrolyte water Clear broths (some protocols) All whole foods (strict elemental protocol) Fiber of any kind Any complex macronutrients requiring digestion Reduces digestive burden to near zero Induces remission in Crohn's disease Starves SIBO bacteria by eliminating food substrate Allows gut to heal from inflammation Effective when medications have failed Best for: people with active Crohn's disease flares, severe SIBO, or other serious gut disorders. Must be done under medical supervision. Typically a short-term intervention (2–6 weeks). var(--red)

entries.title: GAPS Diet
entries.text: GAPS Diet 🧬 therapeutic #9b7de8 Gut and Psychology Syndrome — heals gut-brain connection Gut healing good Autism / ADHD neutral Complex protocol hard Long commitment hard Developed by Dr. Natasha Campbell-McBride. Focuses on healing the gut lining through specific foods, broth, and fermented foods. Claims connections between gut health and neurological conditions. Six introduction stages. Bone broth (cornerstone of the protocol) Well-cooked meats and vegetables Fermented foods (yogurt, sauerkraut, kefir) Eggs and fish Homemade ghee Probiotic-rich foods Specific fruits after initial stages Grains of any kind Processed foods and sugar Starchy vegetables initially Most legumes Pasteurized dairy (use fermented only) May improve gut permeability (leaky gut) Rich in collagen from bone broth — supports gut lining High probiotic content supports microbiome Anecdotally improves neurological symptoms Anti-inflammatory food framework Best for: people with severe gut disorders, autism spectrum conditions, ADHD (as claimed), or chronic digestive issues. Very complex and restrictive — requires commitment and ideally professional guidance. var(--purple)

entries.title: Raw Food Diet
entries.text: Raw Food Diet 🥗 plant #8fbe6b Only uncooked, unprocessed plant foods Enzyme-rich good Detoxifying good Protein deficient risk hard Very restrictive hard The raw food diet consists primarily of uncooked, unprocessed plant foods. Proponents argue that cooking destroys enzymes and nutrients. In practice, some cooking can increase bioavailability of certain nutrients. Fresh and dried fruits Raw vegetables Sprouted grains and legumes Raw nuts and seeds Cold-pressed oils Fermented raw foods (raw sauerkraut, kimchi) Seaweed and algae Smoothies and raw juices All cooked foods Meat, poultry, fish Dairy and eggs Processed foods Refined sugars and oils Very high in antioxidants and plant compounds Rich in enzymes from raw foods High fiber content supports gut health Often results in weight loss High hydration from raw produce Eliminates processed foods completely Best for: short-term cleanse or increased raw food intake rather than full commitment. Full raw vegan diet risks deficiencies in B12, D, zinc, iron, and protein. Best combined with some cooked and fermented foods. var(--green)

entries.title: Warrior Diet
entries.text: Warrior Diet ⚔️ fasting #c8922a Undereating through the day, feasting at night — 20:4 fasting One main meal neutral Fat loss good Ancient pattern neutral Not for everyone hard The Warrior Diet involves eating small amounts of raw fruits and vegetables during the day and one large meal at night within a 4-hour eating window. Based on the eating patterns of ancient warriors. Small raw snacks during day (fruits, vegetables, juices) One large feast at night (within 4h window) Whole protein sources at main meal Complex carbs included at main meal Salads and vegetables Large meals during the day Processed foods Eating outside the feeding window once adapted Promotes fat burning during undereating phase Allows larger satisfying meal at night (psychologically easier) May improve insulin sensitivity Simpler than multiple meal timing protocols Anecdotally improves energy levels through the day Best for: people who naturally aren't hungry in the morning, those who prefer eating one large meal, or those who find 16:8 too short a fasting window. Not ideal for heavy athletes needing post-workout nutrition timing. var(--accent)

entries.title: Alkaline Diet
entries.text: Alkaline Diet ⚗️ therapeutic #5b9bd5 Foods that maintain optimal blood pH and reduce acidity pH balance neutral Anti-inflammatory good Plant-forward good Pseudoscience concerns hard The alkaline diet categorizes foods as acid-forming or alkaline-forming based on their effect on urine pH after digestion. While blood pH is tightly regulated by the body, the dietary pattern itself promotes healthy plant-forward eating. Most fruits and vegetables Spinach, kale, cucumber Avocado and olive oil Nuts and seeds Legumes Tofu and plant proteins Herbal teas and lemon water Meat and dairy (acid-forming) Processed foods Sugar and artificial sweeteners Refined grains Alcohol and caffeine (limit) Promotes high vegetable and fruit intake Reduces processed food and sugar consumption Anti-inflammatory food patterns Often results in weight loss and improved energy May improve bone density markers Supports kidney health Best for: people wanting a framework to eat more plants and less processed food. The pH theory itself is controversial — blood pH doesn't actually change significantly. The diet works because of what it promotes, not pH. var(--blue)

entries.title: Blueberries
entries.text: Blueberries 🫐 superfoods brain antiinflam longevity #9b7de8 Antioxidant powerhouse Anthocyanins protect brain cells from oxidative damage Improves memory and delays cognitive aging High ORAC antioxidant score among fruits Anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer properties Improves insulin sensitivity Supports cardiovascular health Wild blueberries have 2x the antioxidants of cultivated. Frozen preserves nutrients. Daily consumption linked to reduced Alzheimer's risk. ORAC champion Brain protective Low sugar

entries.title: Spirulina
entries.text: Spirulina 🟢 superfoods immune detox energy #8fbe6b Most nutrient-dense food on earth Complete protein — 60–70% protein by dry weight Phycocyanin is a potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant Rich in iron, B vitamins, and essential fatty acids Heavy metal detoxification support Boosts NK cell and antibody production Reduces blood pressure and cholesterol 1 tbsp provides significant nutrition. Mix in smoothies — strong taste. Choose tested brands to avoid contamination. Complete protein Iron-rich Detox

entries.title: Chlorella
entries.text: Chlorella 🌱 superfoods detox immune #8fbe6b Heavy metal chelator Binds and removes heavy metals (mercury, lead, cadmium) Complete protein with chlorophyll for blood health Boosts immune function and NK cell activity Supports liver detoxification Rich in B12 (plant-sourced) Growth factor (CGF) supports cellular repair Start with small doses — detox reactions possible. Take with cilantro for enhanced metal chelation. Heavy metal detox Chlorophyll B12

entries.title: Moringa
entries.text: Moringa 🌿 superfoods energy antiinflam #8fbe6b Miracle tree — all parts edible Gram for gram: more vitamin C than oranges, more calcium than milk Complete protein source from a plant Powerful anti-inflammatory isothiocyanates Lowers blood sugar and cholesterol Rich in iron, potassium, and folate Antioxidant content comparable to matcha Leaf powder is most practical form. Add to smoothies or teas. Widely used in traditional medicine across Africa and Asia. Nutrient dense Complete protein Anti-inflammatory

entries.title: Maca Root
entries.text: Maca Root 🟤 superfoods hormone energy aphrodisiac #c8922a Andean adaptogen for energy and libido Increases energy and stamina without stimulants Supports testosterone and estrogen balance Improves sexual function and libido in men and women Reduces menopause symptoms (hot flashes, mood) Adaptogen that supports adrenal function Rich in minerals — iron, copper, zinc, manganese Black maca for men (testosterone/sperm), red maca for women (estrogen), yellow maca general purpose. Gelatinized form easier to digest. Adaptogen Libido Hormone support

entries.title: Acai Berry
entries.text: Acai Berry 🫐 superfoods antiinflam skin #9b7de8 Amazonian antioxidant berry Extremely high ORAC antioxidant score Anthocyanins protect against cardiovascular disease Oleic acid (same as olive oil) supports heart health Fiber content supports gut health Anti-inflammatory properties reduce cellular aging Boosts collagen production for skin Frozen pulp is most nutrient-dense. Avoid sweetened products — naturally unsweetened acai is very tart. ORAC Skin health Amazonian

entries.title: Cacao (Raw)
entries.text: Cacao (Raw) 🍫 superfoods brain heart mood #c8922a Unprocessed chocolate — nutritional powerhouse Highest magnesium content of any food Theobromine provides calm, sustained energy Flavonoids improve blood flow and brain function PEA (phenylethylamine) boosts mood and focus Rich in iron and zinc Anandamide — the 'bliss molecule' naturally found in cacao Raw cacao vs cocoa: raw retains more antioxidants. 70%+ dark chocolate retains most benefits. Mix raw cacao powder into smoothies. Magnesium Mood booster Flavonoids

entries.title: Oysters
entries.text: Oysters 🦪 aphrodisiac hormone immune #c86b8f Classic aphrodisiac — richest zinc source Richest zinc source in nature — critical for testosterone Zinc deficiency directly reduces libido and sexual function Rich in dopamine precursors High in omega-3 for cardiovascular and hormone health Contains D-aspartic acid — studied for testosterone elevation Complete protein with all essential amino acids 6 oysters provide the entire daily zinc requirement. Raw oysters have highest bioavailability. Cooked retain most benefits. Zinc king Testosterone Classic aphrodisiac

entries.title: Avocado
entries.text: Avocado 🥑 aphrodisiac heart brain hormone #8fbe6b Fertility fruit — named by Aztecs for its shape High in vitamin E — important for reproductive health Monounsaturated fats support hormone production Folate essential for sperm and egg quality Potassium supports cardiovascular health for sexual function B6 supports sex hormone regulation Glutathione precursor supports cellular health Aztecs called avocado 'ahuacuatl' meaning testicle tree due to its shape. High vitamin E supports fertility in both men and women. Fertility Vitamin E Healthy fat

entries.title: Dark Chocolate
entries.text: Dark Chocolate 🍫 aphrodisiac brain heart mood #c8922a Romance food — scientifically backed PEA triggers release of endorphins and serotonin Phenylethylamine mimics feelings of being in love Flavonoids increase nitric oxide — improves blood flow Theobromine enhances mood and energy gently Magnesium reduces stress and anxiety Antioxidants support cardiovascular health 70%+ cacao content required for benefits. Milk chocolate dilutes the effect. 1–2 squares daily is optimal dose. PEA Mood Nitric oxide

entries.title: Pomegranate
entries.text: Pomegranate 🍎 aphrodisiac heart antiinflam #e05c5c Ancient symbol of fertility and vitality Ellagic acid increases testosterone levels in studies Nitric oxide production improves blood flow Powerful antioxidant — 3x the antioxidants of red wine Reduces cortisol — the stress hormone that kills libido Lowers blood pressure Anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory properties Daily pomegranate juice shown to increase testosterone by 24% in one study. Seeds (arils) contain fiber. 100% juice without added sugar. Testosterone Fertility Antioxidant

entries.title: Chili Peppers
entries.text: Chili Peppers 🌶️ aphrodisiac energy antiinflam #e05c5c Capsaicin triggers endorphin rush Capsaicin triggers endorphin release — natural euphoria Increases heart rate and circulation — mirrors arousal physiologically Boosts metabolism and thermogenesis Anti-inflammatory for sexual health Rich in vitamin C Releases adrenaline which heightens physical sensations The endorphin rush from capsaicin creates a mild euphoric state. Jalapeños have the right potency — ghost peppers may be counterproductive. Endorphins Circulation Metabolism

entries.title: Figs
entries.text: Figs 🫐 aphrodisiac hormone energy #9b7de8 Ancient fertility symbol Rich in amino acids that increase libido and sexual stamina High in iron supporting healthy blood and energy Zinc and magnesium support testosterone Fiber and antioxidants support gut and hormonal health Phenols and antioxidants reduce oxidative stress High potassium for cardiovascular function Figs were sacred to Dionysus in Greek mythology. Historically used as fertility symbol and aphrodisiac across cultures. Fertility Ancient Iron-rich

entries.title: Saffron
entries.text: Saffron 🟡 aphrodisiac brain mood #c8922a Most expensive spice — proven aphrodisiac Clinically shown to improve sexual function in both men and women Antidepressant effect comparable to fluoxetine in studies Crocin and safranal improve mood and reduce anxiety Increases dopamine and serotonin levels Aphrodisiac effects confirmed in multiple clinical trials Anti-oxidant protection for brain cells Effective dose: 15–30mg (a pinch) daily. Steep in warm water before adding to recipes. One of few aphrodisiacs with genuine clinical evidence. Clinical evidence Antidepressant Dopamine

entries.title: Fatty Fish (Salmon, Mackerel)
entries.text: Fatty Fish (Salmon, Mackerel) 🐟 brain heart antiinflam #5b9bd5 DHA is the brain's structural fat DHA makes up 30% of brain grey matter — essential for function Reduces risk of Alzheimer's and cognitive decline EPA reduces neuroinflammation Improves mood and reduces depression Supports myelin sheath integrity for nerve signaling Lowers triglycerides and cardiovascular inflammation 2–3 servings per week provides adequate DHA/EPA. Wild-caught salmon has higher omega-3 than farmed. Sardines are affordable equivalent. DHA Neuroprotective Depression

entries.title: Walnuts
entries.text: Walnuts 🌰 brain heart antiinflam #9b7de8 Brain-shaped for a reason Highest ALA omega-3 of all nuts Polyphenols reduce neuroinflammation Improve memory and cognitive performance in older adults Reduce blood pressure and cardiovascular risk Rich in vitamin E protecting brain cell membranes Melatonin for sleep supports brain consolidation 1 ounce (28g) provides meaningful brain benefit. Shape similarity to brain is coincidental but memorable. Raw walnuts have highest polyphenol content. ALA Memory Polyphenols

entries.title: Eggs
entries.text: Eggs 🥚 brain hormone energy #c8922a Choline powerhouse for memory Richest dietary source of choline — acetylcholine precursor Choline deficiency directly impairs memory and learning Complete protein with all essential amino acids Lutein and zeaxanthin protect against macular degeneration B12 supports myelin and nerve function Tyrosine supports dopamine and focus Egg yolk contains the choline — don't skip it. 2 eggs provide approximately 50% of daily choline needs. Choline Memory Complete protein

entries.title: Matcha
entries.text: Matcha 🍵 brain energy antiinflam longevity #8fbe6b L-theanine + caffeine = calm focus L-theanine promotes alpha brain waves — calm alertness without jitters Caffeine combination produces sustained focus without crash EGCG is one of the most powerful antioxidants known Chlorophyll from whole leaf supports detoxification Boosts metabolism and fat oxidation Neuroprotective effects against cognitive decline Ceremonial grade for drinking, culinary grade for cooking. Whisk with 70°C water — boiling water destroys L-theanine. L-theanine EGCG Focus

entries.title: Lion's Mane Mushroom
entries.text: Lion's Mane Mushroom 🦁 brain longevity immune #9b7de8 NGF stimulator — regrows brain cells NGF (nerve growth factor) stimulation promotes neuroplasticity Improves memory and cognition — studied for Alzheimer's Reduces anxiety and depression through nervous system support Myelin sheath repair supports nerve damage recovery Gut-brain axis support through prebiotic beta-glucans Anti-cancer and immune-modulating properties Powder form: 1–3g daily. Takes 4–8 weeks for cognitive effects. Best taken consistently — not as needed. NGF Neuroplasticity Alzheimer's research

entries.title: Turmeric + Black Pepper
entries.text: Turmeric + Black Pepper 🟡 brain antiinflam longevity #c8922a Curcumin crosses blood-brain barrier with piperine Curcumin reduces amyloid plaques — Alzheimer's marker Crosses blood-brain barrier (with piperine) to reduce neuroinflammation Boosts BDNF — brain-derived neurotrophic factor for new neurons Powerful systemic anti-inflammatory Antidepressant effects comparable to some medications Anti-cancer properties through multiple pathways Black pepper (piperine) increases curcumin absorption by 2,000%. Fat-soluble — take with a meal. Combine with healthy fat for best absorption. BDNF Piperine Anti-amyloid

entries.title: Olive Oil (Extra Virgin)
entries.text: Olive Oil (Extra Virgin) 🫒 heart brain antiinflam longevity #8fbe6b Cornerstone of the Mediterranean diet Oleocanthal has anti-inflammatory activity similar to ibuprofen Reduces LDL oxidation — most dangerous form of cholesterol Monounsaturated fats lower cardiovascular disease risk Polyphenols protect arterial walls Improves HDL cholesterol function Reduces blood pressure Extra virgin only — refined olive oil lacks polyphenols. Store in dark bottle away from heat. Robust peppery taste indicates high polyphenol content. EVOO Oleocanthal Mediterranean

entries.title: Garlic
entries.text: Garlic 🧄 heart immune antiinflam detox #5b9bd5 Allicin — nature's most powerful antimicrobial Allicin reduces LDL cholesterol and blood pressure Reduces platelet aggregation — natural blood thinner Antimicrobial against 23+ bacteria, viruses, and fungi Detoxifies heavy metals — organosulfur compounds chelate lead Reduces homocysteine — cardiovascular inflammation marker Anti-cancer properties shown in multiple studies Crush and let sit 10 min before cooking to activate allicin. Raw is most potent. Aged black garlic has different but also significant benefits. Allicin Antimicrobial Blood pressure

entries.title: Beets
entries.text: Beets 🔴 heart energy antiinflam #e05c5c Nitrates convert to nitric oxide Dietary nitrates convert to nitric oxide — dilates blood vessels Reduces blood pressure by 4–10 mmHg in studies Improves VO2 max and athletic endurance by 1–3% Betalains are powerful anti-inflammatory antioxidants Supports liver detoxification Rich in folate for cardiovascular health Beet juice effects peak 2–3 hours after consumption. Ideal 2–3 hours before exercise or performance event. Red discoloration in urine (beeturia) is harmless. Nitric oxide Blood pressure Athletic performance

entries.title: Berries (General)
entries.text: Berries (General) 🍓 heart brain antiinflam longevity skin #c86b8f Anthocyanins protect heart and brain Anthocyanins reduce LDL oxidation and arterial inflammation Improve endothelial function (arterial flexibility) Polyphenols reduce blood pressure High antioxidant content protects against aging Fiber supports microbiome diversity Low glycemic — good for blood sugar management Frozen berries retain equal or more antioxidants than fresh. Wild varieties are more potent. Rotate: blueberries, raspberries, blackberries, strawberries. Anthocyanins Low GI Arterial health

entries.title: Sauerkraut & Kimchi
entries.text: Sauerkraut & Kimchi 🫙 gut immune antiinflam #5bbfb5 Live fermented foods — probiotic powerhouses Billions of live Lactobacillus bacteria per serving Vitamin C increases dramatically during fermentation K2 produced by bacteria supports bone and heart health Enzymes improve nutrient absorption across the gut Diversity of probiotic strains supports microbiome balance Anti-cancer compounds from fermentation process Refrigerated, unpasteurized only — heat kills probiotics. Start with 1–2 tbsp/day to avoid gas from microbiome shift. Lactobacillus K2 Live cultures

entries.title: Bone Broth
entries.text: Bone Broth 🍲 gut bone skin immune #c8922a Collagen, gelatin, and gut-sealing glycine Gelatin seals and heals gut lining (leaky gut support) Glycine reduces inflammation in gut and throughout body Collagen supports joint, skin, and bone health Glutamine is the primary fuel for intestinal cells Rich in minerals extracted from bones Supports sleep and muscle recovery through glycine Simmer bones 12–24 hours for maximum collagen extraction. Add apple cider vinegar to extract more minerals. Slow cooker is easiest method. Gelatin Leaky gut Collagen

entries.title: Kefir
entries.text: Kefir 🥛 gut immune bone #5bbfb5 More probiotic than yogurt — fermented milk Contains 30+ strains of beneficial bacteria and yeasts Highly bioavailable protein and calcium Can improve lactose tolerance — bacteria digest lactose Reduces allergy and asthma symptoms Powerful immune modulator Antibacterial properties even against antibiotic-resistant bacteria Full-fat kefir is best. Coconut milk kefir is dairy-free alternative. Water kefir is another dairy-free option. 30+ strains Lactose friendly Antibacterial

entries.title: Ginger
entries.text: Ginger 🫚 gut antiinflam immune energy #c8922a The universal digestive aid Gingerols and shogaols reduce nausea effectively Speeds gastric emptying — relieves indigestion Reduces intestinal inflammation Anti-nausea for pregnancy, chemotherapy, and motion sickness Antimicrobial against H. pylori Warming effect improves circulation throughout body Fresh ginger for acute nausea and digestion. Dried ginger is 5–10x more concentrated. Ginger tea: steep 1-inch piece for 10 min. Anti-nausea Gingerols Gastric motility

entries.title: Elderberry
entries.text: Elderberry 🫐 immune antiinflam #9b7de8 Most studied natural antiviral Anthocyanins inhibit viral replication directly Reduces duration of influenza by 2–4 days in studies Stimulates cytokine production for immune signaling Antioxidant protection from free radicals Reduces upper respiratory infection symptoms Anti-inflammatory properties Syrup or tincture. Most effective at first sign of illness — not for continuous prevention. Cooked elderberries only — raw contain toxic compounds. Antiviral Flu Cytokins

entries.title: Propolis
entries.text: Propolis 🍯 immune antiinflam detox #c8922a Bee's antimicrobial resin Broad-spectrum antimicrobial against bacteria, viruses, fungi Flavonoids and phenolic acids are potent antioxidants Speeds wound healing when applied topically Protects liver from toxin damage Anti-cancer properties from caffeic acid phenethyl ester Supports gut health and reduces pathogenic bacteria Use as tincture (30–40% propolis). Start with small doses. Avoid if allergic to bee stings. One of the most complete antimicrobial natural compounds. Antimicrobial Bees Wound healing

entries.title: Tart Cherry
entries.text: Tart Cherry 🍒 antiinflam energy longevity brain #e05c5c Recovery and sleep — two benefits in one Anthocyanins reduce exercise-induced muscle inflammation Melatonin content improves sleep onset and duration Reduces gout flare frequency and uric acid levels Antioxidant activity exceeds most berries Reduces markers of oxidative stress after exercise Anti-cancer properties from quercetin Tart cherry juice (not sweet/black cherry). 8–12oz before bed for sleep. 2x daily around training for recovery. Montmorency variety is most studied. Recovery Melatonin Gout

entries.title: Flaxseed
entries.text: Flaxseed 🌰 antiinflam hormone heart gut #8fbe6b Richest plant omega-3 and lignans Richest plant source of ALA omega-3 Lignans have phytoestrogenic and anti-cancer properties Mucilage (soluble fiber) reduces cholesterol Reduces blood pressure in hypertensive individuals Supports healthy hormone balance (estrogen metabolism) Promotes gut motility and relieves constipation Ground flaxseed only — whole seeds pass through undigested. 2 tbsp/day. Store in freezer to prevent rancidity. Freshly grind for maximum benefit. Lignans ALA Phytoestrogen

entries.title: Brazil Nuts
entries.text: Brazil Nuts 🌰 hormone immune longevity #c8922a 2–3 nuts = entire daily selenium Selenium is essential for thyroid hormone T4 to T3 conversion Supports testosterone production and male fertility Glutathione peroxidase activity — master antioxidant cofactor Reduces thyroid antibodies in Hashimoto's thyroiditis Cardiovascular protection through selenium Rich in magnesium and zinc for hormone support 2–3 Brazil nuts daily is the optimal dose. More is not better — selenium toxicity (selenosis) occurs at high doses. Selenium Thyroid 2-3 nuts only

entries.title: Cruciferous Vegetables
entries.text: Cruciferous Vegetables 🥦 hormone detox antiinflam longevity #8fbe6b DIM and sulforaphane for hormone detox DIM (diindolylmethane) supports healthy estrogen metabolism Sulforaphane activates Phase 2 liver detox enzymes Reduces excess estrogen — important for both men and women Anti-cancer properties — glucosinolates converted to isothiocyanates Supports liver detoxification pathways Rich in folate, vitamin C, and vitamin K Lightly cook or steam — raw cruciferous may suppress thyroid in large quantities (goitrogens). Broccoli sprouts have 100x more sulforaphane than mature broccoli. DIM Sulforaphane Estrogen metabolism

entries.title: Beet Root
entries.text: Beet Root 🔴 energy heart antiinflam #e05c5c Natural pre-workout — nitric oxide booster Nitrates convert to nitric oxide — increases blood and oxygen delivery Improves exercise efficiency — muscles need less oxygen for same output Reduces muscle fatigue and post-exercise soreness Natural ergogenic — no stimulants Improves high-intensity interval performance Supports brain blood flow and cognitive performance Peak effects 2–3 hours after consumption. Beet root powder or 250–500ml juice. Tested by professional athletes. Avoid mouthwash before — kills nitrate-converting bacteria. Nitrates Pre-workout Ergogenic

entries.title: Ashwagandha
entries.text: Ashwagandha 🌿 energy hormone brain aphrodisiac #9b7de8 King of adaptogens Reduces cortisol up to 30% — lowers stress-induced fatigue Improves VO2 max and cardiorespiratory endurance Increases testosterone by 15–22% in deficient men Improves thyroid function in subclinical hypothyroidism Anti-anxiety effects comparable to lorazepam in some studies Supports deep restful sleep through nervous system calming KSM-66 or Sensoril are the most studied standardized extracts. 300–600mg/day. Take at night — calming effect. 8 weeks for full effect. Adaptogen Cortisol Testosterone

entries.title: Dandelion
entries.text: Dandelion 🌼 detox gut liver #c8922a Liver tonic and diuretic Stimulates bile production — essential for fat digestion and liver detox Natural diuretic that supports kidney function without electrolyte loss Prebiotic inulin feeds beneficial gut bacteria Rich in vitamins A, C, K, and minerals Liver protective — prevents oxidative stress in liver cells Anti-inflammatory taraxacin reduces systemic inflammation All parts edible: leaves (salad), roots (tea/coffee alternative), flowers (wine). Root tea is most medicinal. Found everywhere — free food. Liver tonic Diuretic Prebiotic

entries.title: Activated Charcoal
entries.text: Activated Charcoal ⬛ detox #5b9bd5 Emergency toxin binder Binds toxins, chemicals, and drugs in the GI tract Used in emergency rooms for poisoning and overdose Reduces gas and bloating from fermentation May reduce cholesterol by binding bile acids Reduces hangover symptoms from alcohol toxins Kidney support by filtering urea Not for long-term use — binds nutrients and medications too. Take 2+ hours away from any medications or supplements. Emergency toxin exposure use. Emergency use Toxin binder Short-term only

entries.title: Milk Thistle
entries.text: Milk Thistle 🌸 detox liver immune #c86b8f Gold standard liver protector Silymarin is the most studied hepatoprotective compound Regenerates liver cells after toxin damage Protects against alcohol, medication, and environmental liver damage Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant within liver tissue Reduces insulin resistance in people with fatty liver Improves bile flow for digestion 140–200mg silymarin standardized extract 3x daily. Used for decades in clinical settings. Safe for long-term use. Milk thistle tea is gentler than extract. Silymarin Hepatoprotective Liver regeneration

entries.title: Collagen-Rich Foods
entries.text: Collagen-Rich Foods 💅 skin bone gut #c86b8f Building blocks of skin and connective tissue Glycine, proline, and hydroxyproline form skin's structural matrix Reduces wrinkle depth and improves skin elasticity Supports joint cartilage and reduces arthritis pain Gut lining repair through glutamine and glycine Improves nail strength and hair thickness Bone density support through collagen matrix Food sources: bone broth, chicken skin, pork rinds. Vitamin C is essential cofactor for collagen synthesis — pair together. Hydrolyzed collagen peptide supplements are bioavailable. Skin structure Joint health Vitamin C synergy

entries.title: Aloe Vera
entries.text: Aloe Vera 🌵 skin gut immune #8fbe6b Inside and out — healing plant Acemannan polysaccharide supports immune function Heals gut lining — used for IBS and acid reflux Topical application accelerates wound and burn healing Anti-inflammatory compounds reduce skin irritation Antioxidant vitamins C and E support skin health Antimicrobial properties against common skin bacteria Inner gel only — aloin (outer leaf) is a harsh laxative. Food-grade aloe vera juice for internal use. Widely available in Mexican markets. Acemannan Wound healing Gut soothing

entries.title: Green Tea (EGCG)
entries.text: Green Tea (EGCG) 🍵 longevity brain antiinflam detox #8fbe6b Most studied anti-aging beverage EGCG activates AMPK — cellular energy sensor linked to longevity Telomere-protective effects — reduces aging at cellular level Reduces risk of multiple cancers through multiple pathways Improves insulin sensitivity and metabolic health L-theanine + caffeine for calm, sustained cognitive function Reduces neuroinflammation and risk of Parkinson's and Alzheimer's 3–5 cups daily for maximum benefit. Steep at 70–80°C — boiling destroys EGCG. Matcha provides whole leaf with 10x the EGCG. EGCG AMPK Telomeres

entries.title: Fasting (Intermittent)
entries.text: Fasting (Intermittent) ⏳ longevity detox brain energy #9b7de8 Autophagy — cellular self-cleaning Autophagy: cellular cleaning process that removes damaged proteins and organelles Activates sirtuins — longevity proteins Improves insulin sensitivity and metabolic flexibility Reduces IGF-1 — associated with slower aging Neurogenesis: new brain cell growth during fasting periods BDNF increase supports cognitive function and mood Autophagy peaks at 16–18 hours of fasting. Combine with resistance training to preserve muscle. Electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium) during fasting prevent energy crashes. Autophagy Sirtuins BDNF

entries.title: Resveratrol Foods
entries.text: Resveratrol Foods 🍇 longevity heart antiinflam #9b7de8 Activates SIRT1 longevity gene Activates SIRT1 — the longevity sirtuin gene (same as caloric restriction) Protects mitochondrial function and reduces mitochondrial aging Anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective Crosses blood-brain barrier — neuroprotective Improves insulin sensitivity May reduce tumor growth through multiple mechanisms Food sources: red grape skin, blueberries, dark chocolate, peanuts. Red wine contains resveratrol but alcohol negates most benefits. Trans-resveratrol supplements have higher bioavailability. SIRT1 Mitochondria Grapes

entries.title: Ashwagandha
entries.text: Ashwagandha Withania somnifera 🌿 calm sleep Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A grounding adaptogen often used for stress resilience, sleep quality, and nervous-system recovery. May support perceived stress reduction and calmer cortisol rhythm Often used for sleep quality and recovery from burnout Can feel calming rather than stimulating for many people Avoid in pregnancy unless clinician-approved Use caution with thyroid medication, sedatives, autoimmune conditions, or liver concerns

entries.title: Rhodiola
entries.text: Rhodiola Rhodiola rosea 🌸 energy focus Traditional Use Preliminary Research A more activating adaptogen commonly used for fatigue, mental performance, and stamina. May support mental energy during stress or fatigue Often used for focus, endurance, and work capacity Can be useful when stress feels draining rather than anxious May feel stimulating; avoid late-day use if sleep-sensitive Use caution with bipolar disorder, stimulants, or anxiety sensitivity

entries.title: Holy Basil / Tulsi
entries.text: Holy Basil / Tulsi Ocimum tenuiflorum 🌱 calm immune Traditional Use Preliminary Research A gentle herb traditionally used for emotional balance, respiratory support, and daily stress tone. Supports a calmer stress response without heavy sedation Traditionally used for respiratory and immune balance May support blood sugar balance in some contexts Use caution with blood sugar medication or anticoagulants Pregnancy safety is not well established

entries.title: Reishi
entries.text: Reishi Ganoderma lucidum 🍄 immune sleep calm Traditional Use Preliminary Research A medicinal mushroom used for immune modulation, calm, and recovery rather than stimulation. Traditionally used to support immune balance and resilience May support relaxation and sleep quality for some people Contains beta-glucans and triterpenes studied for immune effects Use caution with anticoagulants, immune medications, or surgery Can cause digestive upset or dryness in some people

entries.title: Schisandra
entries.text: Schisandra Schisandra chinensis 🍒 focus energy Traditional Use Limited Evidence A berry used in traditional Chinese medicine for resilience, focus, liver support, and endurance. Traditionally used for stamina, concentration, and stress adaptation Often framed as liver and recovery support May support steady energy without heavy stimulation Can interact with liver-metabolized medications Use caution with reflux, pregnancy, or complex medication routines

entries.title: Eleuthero
entries.text: Eleuthero Eleutherococcus senticosus 🌾 energy immune Traditional Use Preliminary Research Sometimes called Siberian ginseng, used for resilience, endurance, and immune-season support. Traditionally used for stamina and adaptation to physical stress May support immune resilience during demanding periods Often used when fatigue is tied to workload or training Use caution with blood pressure issues or stimulant sensitivity Avoid confusing it with Panax ginseng; they are different plants

entries.title: Panax Ginseng
entries.text: Panax Ginseng Panax ginseng 🫚 energy focus immune Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A stronger, more stimulating adaptogen used for energy, cognition, libido, and immune resilience. May support fatigue, mental performance, and vitality Traditionally used for strength, recovery, and immune function Can be more energizing than calming adaptogens May affect blood sugar, blood pressure, sleep, and anticoagulant medication Avoid late use if prone to insomnia or jitters

entries.title: Cordyceps
entries.text: Cordyceps Cordyceps militaris / sinensis 🍄 energy immune Traditional Use Preliminary Research A medicinal mushroom associated with energy metabolism, training capacity, and immune support. Often used for stamina, exercise support, and recovery Studied for immune-modulating compounds May support energy without caffeine-like stimulation Use caution with autoimmune conditions or immune-suppressing medication Quality varies a lot between products

entries.title: Maca
entries.text: Maca Lepidium meyenii 🥔 energy Traditional Use Preliminary Research A Peruvian root used more for vitality, mood, libido, and endurance than direct stress sedation. May support energy, mood, and sexual wellbeing Often used as food-like daily nourishment Can fit vitality and hormone-support themed pages Use caution with hormone-sensitive conditions Can feel too energizing for some people

entries.title: Ginger
entries.text: Ginger Zingiber officinale Digestive culinary digestive Warming root used for nausea, digestion, circulation, and cold-weather meals. Fresh tea for nausea or sluggish digestion Grated into soups, stir-fries, rice bowls, or smoothies Pairs well with lemon, honey, turmeric, and mint Can aggravate reflux in some people Use caution with blood thinners or surgery at high supplemental doses

entries.title: Elderberry
entries.text: Elderberry Sambucus nigra Immune Season immune Dark berry used in syrups, lozenges, and teas for cold and flu-season support. Prepared syrup or lozenge during respiratory season Tea blends with ginger, cinnamon, or honey Best used as supportive care, not as a replacement for medical treatment Use cooked/prepared products; raw berries, leaves, and stems can be unsafe Use caution with autoimmune disease or immune-active medication

entries.title: Turmeric
entries.text: Turmeric Curcuma longa Anti-inflammatory culinary digestive Golden rhizome known for curcumin, a heavily studied anti-inflammatory compound with poor absorption unless paired well. Add to curries, soups, eggs, and rice bowls Pair with black pepper and fat for better absorption Use culinary amounts as the safest default High-dose extracts can interact with blood thinners Use caution with gallbladder issues or liver concerns

entries.title: Peppermint
entries.text: Peppermint Mentha x piperita Cooling Digestive culinary digestive Cooling aromatic herb used for digestive spasms, nausea, breath, and a refreshing tea. Tea after meals Fresh leaves in water, salads, sauces, and smoothies Enteric-coated peppermint oil is a separate stronger product Can worsen reflux Peppermint oil is concentrated and should not be used casually

entries.title: Chamomile
entries.text: Chamomile Matricaria chamomilla Calm calm Gentle flower tea commonly used for relaxation, sleep routines, and mild digestive tension. Evening tea Blend with lavender, lemon balm, or mint Good first herb when a gentle calm effect is desired Avoid if allergic to ragweed-family plants Use caution with sedatives or anticoagulants

entries.title: Valerian Root
entries.text: Valerian Root Valeriana officinalis Sleep / Tension calm Sedating root traditionally used for sleep support, nervous-system tension, and occasional muscle tightness linked to stress. Evening tea, capsule, or tincture when sleep and tension are connected Often paired with lemon balm or chamomile Best used when relaxation is the goal, not daytime focus Can cause drowsiness or grogginess Avoid combining with alcohol, sedatives, or driving until you know your response

entries.title: Lavender
entries.text: Lavender Lavandula angustifolia Calm / Topical calm Aromatic flower used in teas, aromatherapy, and diluted topical oils for relaxation and tension comfort. Aromatherapy during wind-down routines Diluted topical oil in massage blends Tea blends with chamomile or lemon balm Essential oils must be diluted for skin use Use caution with sedatives and avoid casual internal essential-oil use

entries.title: Arnica
entries.text: Arnica Arnica montana Topical Soreness calm Flower commonly used in topical creams and balms for muscle soreness, bruising, and post-activity discomfort. Topical cream or balm on intact skin after activity Use alongside rest, gentle mobility, and heat or ice as appropriate Best framed as comfort support, not a trigger-point cure Do not apply to broken skin Do not take internally unless medically supervised; can be toxic

entries.title: Tulsi / Holy Basil
entries.text: Tulsi / Holy Basil Ocimum tenuiflorum Adaptogen adaptogen calm immune Daily-use adaptogenic herb used for stress tone, respiratory season, and emotional steadiness. Tea during stressful work periods Blend with ginger or mint Useful when stress support should stay gentle May affect blood sugar or blood thinning Pregnancy safety is not well established

entries.title: Dandelion
entries.text: Dandelion Taraxacum officinale Bitter Digestive digestive Bitter leaf and root used for digestion, bile-flow support, and mineral-rich greens. Roasted root tea Young greens in salad if tolerated Bitter tea before meals for digestive support Avoid with bile duct obstruction or gallbladder attacks Use caution with diuretics, lithium, and ragweed-family allergy

entries.title: Echinacea
entries.text: Echinacea Echinacea spp. Immune Season immune Immune-season herb commonly used short-term at the first sign of a cold. Short-term tea, tincture, or capsule during cold season Often paired with elderberry, ginger, or honey Best framed as support, not guaranteed prevention Use caution with autoimmune disease or immunosuppressants Avoid if allergic to Asteraceae plants

entries.title: Garlic
entries.text: Garlic Allium sativum Immune Food culinary immune Pungent culinary herb with allicin-related sulfur compounds used for immune and antimicrobial-style food support. Crush fresh garlic and let it sit briefly before cooking Use in soups, dressings, beans, meats, and vegetables Pairs with ginger, lemon, oregano, and olive oil Can irritate reflux or gastritis Use caution with anticoagulants, surgery, or high-dose supplements

entries.title: Licorice Root
entries.text: Licorice Root Glycyrrhiza glabra Throat / Immune immune Sweet root used for throat soothing and upper-respiratory support; glycyrrhizin has been studied for antiviral activity. Tea or decoction for throat comfort DGL forms are used differently for digestive lining support Best used short-term unless clinician-guided Can raise blood pressure and lower potassium Avoid with hypertension, kidney disease, pregnancy, diuretics, heart medication, or corticosteroids unless clinician-guided

entries.title: Oregano
entries.text: Oregano Origanum vulgare Antimicrobial culinary immune Culinary herb whose carvacrol and thymol compounds are studied for antimicrobial activity; oregano oil is much stronger. Use culinary oregano freely in food Diluted topical or capsule products should be treated as concentrated supplements Pairs with garlic, thyme, rosemary, and olive oil Essential oil can irritate or burn tissue Avoid casual internal essential-oil use; use caution with pregnancy, blood thinners, or allergies

entries.title: Lemon Balm
entries.text: Lemon Balm Melissa officinalis Calm / Antiviral calm immune Gentle lemon-scented herb used for calm, sleep support, digestion, and topical herpes-simplex cold-sore support. Tea for nervous-system calm Topical cream preparations for cold-sore support Blends well with chamomile, lavender, and peppermint Can be sedating Check thyroid medication and sedative interactions

entries.title: Ashwagandha
entries.text: Ashwagandha Withania somnifera Adaptogen adaptogen calm Grounding adaptogen used for stress resilience, sleep quality, and recovery. Evening or with food depending on response Often used when stress feels wired, tense, or depleting Can pair with magnesium-focused evening routines Avoid in pregnancy unless clinician-approved Use caution with thyroid medication, sedatives, autoimmune conditions, or liver concerns

entries.title: Nettle
entries.text: Nettle Urtica dioica Mineral Support immune Mineral-rich leaf used as a nourishing tea and seasonal support herb. Long infusion for mineral support Spring rotation tea Pairs well with mint or lemon Can interact with diuretics, blood pressure medication, and blood sugar medication Handle fresh leaves carefully because they sting

entries.title: Rosemary
entries.text: Rosemary Salvia rosmarinus Culinary Antioxidant culinary Aromatic culinary herb with antioxidant compounds and a strong savory profile. Use in roasted vegetables, chicken, fish, beans, and olive-oil dressings Blend with thyme, sage, garlic, and lemon Good everyday food-first herb Culinary amounts are the safest default Avoid high-dose essential oil use, especially with seizure risk

entries.title: Green Tea
entries.text: Green Tea Camellia sinensis 🍵 antioxidant energy calm spring Moderate Traditional Use Clinical Evidence Lightly oxidised true tea rich in catechins (notably EGCG) and L-theanine, giving a calm-alert lift. Rich in antioxidant polyphenols studied for heart and metabolic health L-theanine pairs with caffeine for focused, jitter-free energy Traditionally a fresh, cleansing spring cup Caffeine can disturb sleep if drunk late Tannins may reduce iron absorption — drink between meals

entries.title: Matcha
entries.text: Matcha Camellia sinensis 🍵 antioxidant energy calm spring High Traditional Use Preliminary Research Stone-ground whole green tea leaf whisked into water, so you drink the whole leaf and more of its compounds. Concentrated catechins and L-theanine for sustained calm focus Whole-leaf form delivers more antioxidants than steeped green tea Central to the Japanese tea ceremony Higher caffeine than most steeped teas Source matters — choose tested, low-contaminant matcha

entries.title: White Tea
entries.text: White Tea Camellia sinensis 🤍 antioxidant calm spring Low Traditional Use Preliminary Research The least processed true tea — delicate, subtly sweet, and naturally lower in caffeine. Gentle antioxidant content with a soft, mild flavour Lower caffeine suits sensitive drinkers Light enough to enjoy hot in spring or iced in summer Still contains some caffeine Steep gently to avoid bitterness

entries.title: Oolong
entries.text: Oolong Camellia sinensis 🌗 antioxidant energy digestion autumn Moderate Traditional Use Preliminary Research Partially oxidised tea sitting between green and black, often roasted, with a rounded grounding character. Studied for metabolism and modest weight-management support Smooth, warming profile that suits cooler autumn days Traditionally sipped after meals to aid digestion Contains caffeine Effects on weight are modest, not dramatic

entries.title: Black Tea
entries.text: Black Tea Camellia sinensis ☕ energy antioxidant winter High Traditional Use Clinical Evidence Fully oxidised true tea — robust, warming, and the base of breakfast blends and chai. Highest caffeine of the true teas for a morning lift Theaflavins studied for heart health Warming and comforting through autumn and winter Caffeine can affect sleep and anxiety Strong tannins reduce iron absorption at meals

entries.title: Pu-erh
entries.text: Pu-erh Camellia sinensis 🟤 digestion energy winter Moderate Traditional Use Limited Evidence Aged, fermented dark tea from Yunnan with an earthy depth, traditionally sipped after rich meals. Traditionally used to support digestion after heavy food Warming, grounding character for cold months Studied preliminarily for cholesterol and metabolism Contains caffeine Quality and ageing vary widely between cakes

entries.title: Chamomile
entries.text: Chamomile Matricaria chamomilla 🌼 calm sleep digestion year-round Caffeine-free Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A classic floral tisane long loved as a gentle, soothing bedtime cup. Widely used to wind down and support sleep quality May ease mild digestive upset and tension Caffeine-free, so good any time including evenings Avoid if allergic to ragweed/daisy family Use caution with blood thinners or in pregnancy

entries.title: Peppermint
entries.text: Peppermint Mentha × piperita 🌿 digestion calm summer Caffeine-free Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A bright, cooling mint infusion that refreshes and settles the stomach. Menthol may relax the gut and ease bloating and IBS symptoms Cooling and refreshing — excellent iced in summer Caffeine-free pick-me-up without the jitters May worsen acid reflux or heartburn in some people Avoid strong doses with certain GERD conditions

entries.title: Ginger
entries.text: Ginger Zingiber officinale 🫚 digestion immune winter Caffeine-free Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A warming, spicy root infusion prized for nausea relief and cold-weather comfort. Well-studied for easing nausea, including motion and morning sickness Warming and circulation-boosting for cold months Often paired with lemon and honey for sore throats Large amounts may interact with blood thinners Can aggravate reflux for some people

entries.title: Hibiscus
entries.text: Hibiscus Hibiscus sabdariffa 🌺 antioxidant cleanse summer Caffeine-free Traditional Use Clinical Evidence A tart, ruby-red infusion that is gorgeous iced and studied for blood pressure. Trials suggest it may modestly lower blood pressure High in anthocyanin antioxidants and vitamin C Refreshingly tart and cooling — a summer favourite May lower blood pressure too far with hypotension or BP medication Avoid high doses in pregnancy

entries.title: Rooibos
entries.text: Rooibos Aspalathus linearis 🔴 antioxidant calm year-round Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A naturally sweet, caffeine-free South African red bush tea that is gentle on everyone. Caffeine-free and low in tannins, so smooth any time of day Contains antioxidants like aspalathin Naturally sweet — pleasant without milk or sugar Generally very well tolerated Limited human research on specific health claims

entries.title: Tulsi (Holy Basil)
entries.text: Tulsi (Holy Basil) Ocimum tenuiflorum 🌱 calm immune year-round Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A revered Ayurvedic adaptogen tea used for calm, balance, and daily resilience. Traditionally used to ease stress and support a steady mood Caffeine-free with gentle immune and respiratory support Pleasant warming, clove-like aroma year-round Use caution with blood sugar or anticoagulant medication Pregnancy safety is not well established

entries.title: Lemon Balm
entries.text: Lemon Balm Melissa officinalis 🍋 calm sleep spring Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A bright, lemony member of the mint family used to soothe nerves and lift mood. Traditionally calming for stress and restlessness May support relaxation and sleep, sometimes blended with chamomile Fresh, uplifting flavour for spring and evening May interact with thyroid medication or sedatives Caffeine-free but effects vary by person

entries.title: Echinacea
entries.text: Echinacea Echinacea purpurea 🌸 immune winter Caffeine-free Traditional Use Limited Evidence A daisy-family flower tea popular at the first sign of a cold. Commonly used to support the immune system in cold season Caffeine-free and warming Often blended with elderberry or ginger Avoid if allergic to ragweed/daisy family Caution with autoimmune conditions or immune medication

entries.title: Elderberry
entries.text: Elderberry Sambucus nigra 🫐 immune antioxidant winter Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A dark, antioxidant-rich berry infusion long used for winter immune support. Studied for easing the duration of cold and flu symptoms High in anthocyanin antioxidants Warming and comforting in winter Only use properly prepared berries — raw/unripe elder is toxic Check interactions with immune medication

entries.title: Nettle
entries.text: Nettle Urtica dioica ☘️ cleanse immune spring Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A green, mineral-rich tonic tea traditionally taken as a spring cleanse and for seasonal allergies. Classic spring tonic, rich in minerals like iron and magnesium Traditionally used for seasonal allergy and hay-fever support Caffeine-free and nourishing May interact with blood pressure or diuretic medication Limited high-quality human evidence

entries.title: Dandelion
entries.text: Dandelion Taraxacum officinale 🌼 cleanse digestion spring Caffeine-free Traditional Use Limited Evidence A slightly bitter root or leaf tea used as a digestive and gentle spring detox brew. Bitter compounds traditionally support digestion and the liver Leaf has a mild diuretic, cleansing reputation Roasted root makes a coffee-like caffeine-free cup Diuretic effect may interact with medication Avoid with gallbladder issues without medical advice

entries.title: Lemongrass
entries.text: Lemongrass Cymbopogon citratus 🌾 digestion calm summer Caffeine-free Traditional Use Limited Evidence A citrusy, cooling herb tea common across Southeast Asia, refreshing hot or iced. Traditionally settles digestion and relieves bloating Light, citrusy and cooling for summer Caffeine-free and aromatic Limited clinical evidence Avoid high doses in pregnancy

entries.title: Spearmint
entries.text: Spearmint Mentha spicata 🌿 digestion calm summer Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A softer, sweeter mint than peppermint, studied for hormonal balance and digestion. Some research on spearmint for hormonal acne and PCOS markers Gentle digestive soother, milder than peppermint Refreshing iced in warm weather Caffeine-free but evidence is still emerging Discuss hormonal use with a clinician

entries.title: Cinnamon
entries.text: Cinnamon Cinnamomum verum 🪵 digestion antioxidant winter Caffeine-free Traditional Use Preliminary Research A warming, sweet-spice infusion that brings cosy heat to winter cups and chai blends. Studied preliminarily for blood-sugar support Warming and naturally sweet without sugar A backbone spice in chai and mulled blends Cassia cinnamon is high in coumarin — keep amounts modest May interact with blood-sugar or liver medication

entries.title: Yerba Mate
entries.text: Yerba Mate Ilex paraguariensis 🧉 energy antioxidant autumn High Traditional Use Preliminary Research A naturally caffeinated South American brew giving an energising, focused lift with antioxidants. Strong, sustained energy and focus from natural caffeine Rich in polyphenol antioxidants Traditionally shared as a social, grounding ritual High caffeine — avoid late in the day Very hot mate consumed habitually is linked to throat-irritation risk

entries.title: Sage
entries.text: Sage Salvia officinalis 🍃 immune calm winter Caffeine-free Traditional Use Limited Evidence An earthy, savoury herb tea traditionally used for sore throats and as a winter gargle. Traditionally soothing for sore throats and mouth Warming and caffeine-free Some study interest in memory and menopausal symptoms Avoid high doses in pregnancy or with seizure disorders Limited human evidence for many claims

entries.title: Extra-Virgin Olive Oil
entries.text: Extra-Virgin Olive Oil 🫒 antiinflam antioxidant Strong Cornerstone fat of Mediterranean eating Oleocanthal Polyphenols Oleic acid (MUFA) Drizzle 1–2 tbsp raw over salads, vegetables, or finished dishes; use as your main cooking fat. Calorie-dense — use in place of less healthy fats, not on top of them Choose extra-virgin in dark bottles to preserve polyphenols Harvard Health

entries.title: Berries
entries.text: Berries 🫐 antioxidant antiinflam gut Moderate Blueberries, strawberries, raspberries Anthocyanins Vitamin C Polyphenols Fibre About 1 cup fresh or frozen in oats, yogurt, or smoothies. Whole fruit is preferable to juice for blood-sugar control Harvard Health NIH ODS: Vitamin C

entries.title: Cherries
entries.text: Cherries 🍒 antiinflam antioxidant Early Especially tart cherries Anthocyanins Polyphenols A handful of fresh cherries, or a small glass of unsweetened tart-cherry juice. Juice adds sugar — whole fruit is the better default Harvard Health

entries.title: Leafy Greens
entries.text: Leafy Greens 🥬 antiinflam antioxidant Moderate Spinach, kale, chard Carotenoids Vitamin C Vitamin K Polyphenols A couple of handfuls in salads, sautéed as a side, or blended into smoothies. High vitamin K can interact with warfarin — keep intake consistent if you take blood thinners Harvard Health NIH ODS: Vitamin C

entries.title: Tomatoes
entries.text: Tomatoes 🍅 antioxidant antiinflam Moderate Best absorbed cooked with a little fat Lycopene (carotenoid) Vitamin C Cooked with a little olive oil boosts lycopene — sauces, soups, roasted tomatoes. Acidic; may aggravate reflux in some people Harvard Health

entries.title: Citrus Fruits
entries.text: Citrus Fruits 🍊 antioxidant antiinflam Moderate Oranges, grapefruit, lemons Vitamin C Flavonoids One whole orange, or citrus segments in salads. Grapefruit interacts with several medications — check with a pharmacist NIH ODS: Vitamin C Harvard Health

entries.title: Nuts & Seeds
entries.text: Nuts & Seeds 🥜 antioxidant antiinflam Moderate Almonds, sunflower seeds Vitamin E Magnesium Healthy fats A small unsalted handful daily, or over salads and oats. Tree-nut allergy Calorie-dense — keep portions small NIH ODS: Vitamin E

entries.title: Whole Grains
entries.text: Whole Grains 🌾 antiinflam gut Moderate Oats, brown rice, whole wheat Fibre Beta-glucan B vitamins Swap refined grains for whole — oats at breakfast, whole-grain bread, brown rice. Choose certified gluten-free oats/grains if coeliac Harvard Health

entries.title: Legumes
entries.text: Legumes 🫘 antiinflam gut Moderate Beans, lentils, chickpeas Fibre Resistant starch Plant protein Polyphenols Add to soups, stews, and salads; aim for several servings a week. Introduce gradually to reduce gas Cook dried beans thoroughly Harvard Health

entries.title: Fatty Fish
entries.text: Fatty Fish 🐟 omega3 antiinflam Strong Salmon, sardines, mackerel, trout Omega-3s (EPA & DHA) Vitamin D Protein 2–3 servings a week — baked salmon, sardines on toast, mackerel in salads. Limit high-mercury fish in pregnancy High-dose omega-3 can have mild blood-thinning effects NIH ODS: Omega-3

entries.title: Walnuts
entries.text: Walnuts 🌰 omega3 antiinflam antioxidant Moderate Top plant omega-3 nut ALA omega-3 Vitamin E Polyphenols A small handful (about 28 g) as a snack or over salads and oats. Tree-nut allergy Calorie-dense NIH ODS: Omega-3 NIH ODS: Vitamin E

entries.title: Flaxseed
entries.text: Flaxseed 🌱 omega3 gut Moderate Grind for absorption ALA omega-3 Lignans Soluble fibre 1–2 tbsp ground (not whole) into oats, yogurt, or smoothies. Whole seeds pass through undigested — grind first Drink water with the added fibre NIH ODS: Omega-3

entries.title: Chia Seeds
entries.text: Chia Seeds ⚫ omega3 gut Early Soak before eating ALA omega-3 Soluble fibre 1–2 tbsp soaked in liquid (puddings, smoothies). Always hydrate before eating — dry chia can swell and cause choking NIH ODS: Omega-3

entries.title: Garlic
entries.text: Garlic 🧄 antimicrobial gut antiinflam Lab-based Studied for antimicrobial compounds Allicin Organosulfur compounds Crush fresh and let it stand ~10 min before light cooking; 1–2 cloves. Can thin the blood — caution with anticoagulants and before surgery May cause heartburn or odor Not a replacement for antibiotics PMC: Garlic

entries.title: Onions
entries.text: Onions 🧅 antimicrobial gut antioxidant Early Prebiotic allium Quercetin Inulin (prebiotic) Organosulfur compounds Use raw or cooked as a base for most savory dishes. Can trigger reflux or IBS symptoms (high-FODMAP) in some people PMC: Garlic

entries.title: Ginger
entries.text: Ginger 🫚 antimicrobial antiinflam gut Moderate Best human evidence for nausea Gingerols Shogaols Fresh grated in tea or stir-fries; about a 1-inch piece. Mild blood-thinning at high doses May worsen reflux for some NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Turmeric
entries.text: Turmeric 🟡 antiinflam antioxidant antimicrobial Lab-based Curcumin is poorly absorbed Curcumin Curcuminoids Add to curries, soups, and eggs with black pepper and a little fat to aid absorption. High-dose supplements may interact with blood thinners Rare reports of liver issues with concentrated extracts NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Green Tea
entries.text: Green Tea 🍵 antioxidant antimicrobial Moderate Catechin-rich brew Catechins (EGCG) Polyphenols 1–3 cups daily, steeped at about 70–80°C. Contains caffeine High-dose extracts linked to rare liver injury NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Herbs & Spices
entries.text: Herbs & Spices 🌿 antioxidant antiinflam antimicrobial Lab-based Rosemary, oregano, cinnamon Polyphenols Essential oils Use generously in everyday cooking in place of extra salt. Concentrated spice oils/supplements differ greatly from culinary amounts Harvard Health

entries.title: Honey (raw)
entries.text: Honey (raw) 🍯 antimicrobial Moderate Best evidence is topical wound care Hydrogen peroxide Phenolics Methylglyoxal (manuka) A teaspoon to sweeten; medical-grade honey is used on wounds under clinical care. Never give honey to infants under 12 months (botulism risk) Still a sugar — use sparingly NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Cranberry
entries.text: Cranberry 🔴 antimicrobial antioxidant Moderate Studied for UTI prevention Proanthocyanidins (PACs) Vitamin C Unsweetened juice or whole/dried berries; supplements standardized to PACs. Can interact with warfarin Many cranberry juices are high in added sugar NIH NCCIH NIH ODS: Vitamin C

entries.title: Oats
entries.text: Oats 🥣 gut antiinflam Moderate Beta-glucan soluble fibre Beta-glucan Soluble fibre Porridge or overnight oats; about 40–50 g dry. Choose certified gluten-free if coeliac Harvard Health

entries.title: Asparagus
entries.text: Asparagus 🌿 gut antioxidant Early Prebiotic vegetable Inulin (prebiotic) Folate Vitamin C Roasted or steamed as a side. Generally very safe; harmless urine odor Harvard Health

entries.title: Bananas (slightly green)
entries.text: Bananas (slightly green) 🍌 gut Early Resistant starch when underripe Resistant starch Prebiotic fibre Potassium A slightly underripe banana, or sliced into oats. Riper bananas have more sugar and less resistant starch Harvard Health

entries.title: Yogurt (live cultures)
entries.text: Yogurt (live cultures) 🥛 fermented gut Moderate Look for live and active cultures Live cultures (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) Calcium Protein Plain and unsweetened; add your own fruit. Dairy allergy or lactose intolerance — though yogurt is often better tolerated NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Kefir
entries.text: Kefir 🥛 fermented gut Early Diverse fermented dairy drink Diverse live cultures Protein Calcium Drink small amounts (about ½ cup) or blend into smoothies. Dairy-based versions contain some lactose (usually reduced) NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Sauerkraut & Kimchi
entries.text: Sauerkraut & Kimchi 🥬 fermented gut Early Fermented cabbage Live cultures Fibre Vitamin C 1–2 tbsp of unpasteurized (refrigerated) kraut or kimchi as a side. High in salt Heat and pasteurization kill the live cultures NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Miso & Tempeh
entries.text: Miso & Tempeh 🍲 fermented gut Early Fermented soy Live cultures (miso) Plant protein Isoflavones Stir miso into soup off the heat; pan-fry tempeh as a protein. Miso is high in salt Soy allergy NIH NCCIH

entries.title: Carbohydrates
entries.text: Carbohydrates macro Macronutrient 20–50g (keto) / 225–300g (standard) var(--accent) Primary energy source for brain and body. Reduced on keto to force fat burning. Carbohydrates are the body’s fastest-access fuel: they break down into glucose, which powers the brain, red blood cells, and high-intensity muscle work. Quality matters far more than quantity — fibrous, whole-food carbs digest slowly and steady blood sugar, while refined carbs spike it. Cutting carbs low enough (under ~50g/day) shifts the body into ketosis, burning fat for fuel instead. Supplies glucose, the brain’s preferred fuel Spares protein from being burned for energy Fibrous carbs feed gut bacteria and aid digestion Replenishes muscle glycogen for performance Low energy, brain fog, and irritability when very low Initial fatigue and "keto flu" during adaptation Constipation if fiber-rich carbs are dropped Rice Oats Vegetables Fruit Legumes Favor whole, high-fiber sources over refined sugar and flour Pair carbs with protein or fat to blunt blood-sugar spikes Time most carbs around activity if managing weight Excess refined carbs drive insulin resistance over time Very low-carb diets need extra sodium, potassium, and magnesium

entries.title: Protein
entries.text: Protein macro Macronutrient 90–120g / day var(--blue) Builds and repairs muscle, enzymes, and hormones. Essential for recovery. Protein supplies the amino acids that build and repair every tissue — muscle, skin, hair, enzymes, antibodies, and hormones. It is also the most satiating macronutrient, helping control appetite, and has the highest thermic effect (your body burns more calories digesting it). Spreading intake across meals maximizes muscle protein synthesis. Builds and repairs muscle and connective tissue Forms enzymes, antibodies, and peptide hormones Increases satiety and supports fat loss Preserves lean mass during weight loss and aging Muscle loss, weakness, and slow recovery Thinning hair and brittle nails Frequent infections and slow wound healing Swelling (edema) in severe deficiency Meat Eggs Fish Legumes Dairy Aim for ~0.7–1g per lb of lean body mass Spread across 3–4 meals (~25–40g each) Include a complete-protein or varied plant mix each day Very high intakes may stress kidneys in those with existing kidney disease Balance with vegetables and fiber for digestion

entries.title: Fats
entries.text: Fats macro Macronutrient 120–150g / day (keto) / 44–78g (standard) var(--green) Supports cell structure, hormones, and fat-soluble vitamin absorption. Dietary fat builds cell membranes, insulates nerves, and is required to absorb the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K. Fats are also the raw material for steroid hormones like testosterone and estrogen. On a ketogenic diet, fat replaces carbohydrate as the primary fuel. Emphasize mono- and polyunsaturated fats and omega-3s; minimize industrial trans fats. Forms cell membranes and the myelin around nerves Carries and absorbs vitamins A, D, E, and K Provides building blocks for steroid hormones Most energy-dense fuel (9 kcal/g) Dry skin and poor wound healing Hormonal disruption with chronically very low fat Deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins Constant hunger and low satiety Avocado Olive oil Nuts Fatty fish Eggs Prioritize olive oil, avocado, nuts, and fatty fish Get omega-3s (EPA/DHA) weekly from fish or algae Avoid partially hydrogenated (trans) fats Calorie-dense — portion awareness matters for weight Balance omega-6 to omega-3 ratio (limit seed-oil excess)

entries.title: Sugar
entries.text: Sugar macro Target 0g added (target) var(--red) Excess added sugar drives insulin resistance, inflammation, and metabolic harm. Added sugar delivers rapidly absorbed glucose and fructose with no fiber, vitamins, or minerals. Chronic excess overloads the liver, raises triglycerides, and drives insulin resistance, fatty liver, and inflammation. Naturally occurring sugars inside whole fruit come packaged with fiber and nutrients and behave very differently from added sugar. Provides quick energy (but with no micronutrients) Whole-fruit sugars come bundled with fiber and polyphenols No deficiency exists — added sugar is not an essential nutrient (Limit) sodas Candy Baked goods Sweetened drinks Eliminate added sugars; get sweetness from whole fruit Read labels for hidden sugars (syrups, "-ose" words) Use cinnamon, vanilla, or berries to satisfy cravings Drives insulin resistance, fatty liver, and weight gain Liquid sugar (soda, juice) is the most metabolically harmful form

entries.title: Magnesium
entries.text: Magnesium mineral Mineral 400mg / day var(--blue) Powers energy, sleep, muscle function, and bone health — used in 300+ enzymes. Magnesium is a cofactor in more than 300 enzyme reactions, including every step that makes and uses ATP (cellular energy). It relaxes muscles and the nervous system, supports healthy blood pressure and blood sugar, and is needed to activate vitamin D. Most people fall short because modern soils and refined foods are magnesium-poor. Activates ATP for cellular energy production Relaxes muscles and calms the nervous system Regulates blood pressure and blood sugar Required to convert vitamin D to its active form Muscle cramps, twitches, and spasms Insomnia and difficulty relaxing Anxiety, irritability, and low stress tolerance Fatigue and heart palpitations Pumpkin seeds Spinach Dark chocolate Almonds Black beans Glycinate and citrate forms absorb best; oxide is poorly absorbed Take in the evening to support sleep Pair with vitamin D and K2 for bone health High doses can cause loose stools Those with kidney disease should consult a clinician

entries.title: Potassium
entries.text: Potassium mineral Mineral 4,700mg / day var(--green) Regulates blood pressure, fluid balance, nerve signals, and muscle contractions. Potassium is the main intracellular electrolyte, working in opposition to sodium to control fluid balance, blood pressure, and the electrical signals that fire nerves and contract muscles — including the heartbeat. Most people get far too little because it is concentrated in whole plants and fresh foods rather than processed ones. Balances fluids and counteracts sodium’s blood-pressure effect Conducts nerve impulses and muscle contractions Supports a steady, healthy heart rhythm Helps prevent kidney stones and bone loss Muscle weakness, cramps, and fatigue Heart palpitations or irregular rhythm Constipation Elevated blood pressure Avocado Banana Sweet potato Salmon Leafy greens Aim for food sources — supplements are capped low for safety Increase intake on low-carb/keto diets and with heavy sweating Those with kidney disease or on certain BP meds must limit potassium — consult a clinician Avoid high-dose supplements without medical guidance

entries.title: Sodium
entries.text: Sodium mineral Mineral 3,000–5,000mg / day (active/keto) var(--teal) Essential for hydration, nerve function, muscle contraction, and digestion. Sodium is the primary extracellular electrolyte, governing blood volume, hydration, nerve firing, and muscle contraction. While excess from processed food is a real problem, very low sodium — common on whole-food, low-carb, or athletic diets — causes its own issues. Athletes and keto dieters excrete more sodium and often need extra. Maintains blood volume and hydration Enables nerve signaling and muscle contraction Supports nutrient absorption in the gut Partners with potassium to balance fluids Headaches, lightheadedness, and brain fog Muscle cramps and weakness Fatigue and low blood pressure (esp. on keto) Sea salt Bone broth Pickles Olives Celery On keto or with heavy training, add salt deliberately Balance with potassium and magnesium for true hydration Excess from processed foods raises blood pressure in salt-sensitive people Those with hypertension or heart/kidney disease should moderate intake

entries.title: Calcium
entries.text: Calcium mineral Mineral 1,000mg / day var(--blue) Essential for bone strength, muscle contraction, and nerve transmission. About 99% of the body’s calcium is stored in bones and teeth, but the small circulating amount is critical for muscle contraction, nerve signaling, blood clotting, and heartbeat. Calcium needs vitamin D for absorption and vitamin K2 to direct it into bone rather than arteries. Builds and maintains bone and tooth density Triggers muscle contraction (including the heart) Enables nerve signaling and blood clotting Supports enzyme and hormone release Low bone density, osteopenia, and osteoporosis Muscle cramps and spasms Numbness or tingling in fingers Brittle nails Dairy Sardines (with bones) Kale Fortified plant milks Almonds Take with vitamin D and K2 for proper bone deposition Split doses (~500mg) for better absorption Prioritize food sources over high-dose supplements Excess supplemental calcium may calcify arteries without K2 Can interfere with iron and zinc absorption if taken together

entries.title: Iron
entries.text: Iron mineral Mineral 8mg (men) / 18mg (women) var(--red) Carries oxygen in the blood via hemoglobin — the most common deficiency worldwide. Iron is the core of hemoglobin, the molecule that ferries oxygen from lungs to tissues, and of myoglobin in muscle. It also drives energy production and immune function. Heme iron (from animal foods) absorbs far better than non-heme (plant) iron. Iron deficiency is the most common nutrient deficiency on earth, especially in menstruating women. Transports oxygen via hemoglobin and myoglobin Supports energy production in mitochondria Aids immune function and cognition Needed for neurotransmitter synthesis Fatigue and weakness Pale skin and shortness of breath Cold hands and feet Brittle nails, hair loss, and restless legs Red meat Liver Spinach Lentils Pumpkin seeds Pair plant (non-heme) iron with vitamin C to boost absorption Avoid tea/coffee and calcium around iron-rich meals Test ferritin before supplementing Excess iron is pro-oxidant and harmful — don’t supplement without testing Men and post-menopausal women rarely need extra

entries.title: Zinc
entries.text: Zinc mineral Mineral 10–12mg / day var(--purple) Key for immunity, wound healing, enzyme activity, and testosterone. Zinc is essential to over 300 enzymes and to the gene-regulating "zinc finger" proteins. It is central to immune defense, wound healing, taste and smell, skin health, and male hormone production. The body has no dedicated zinc store, so a steady intake is needed. Plant phytates reduce absorption, putting plant-based eaters at higher risk. Powers immune cell function and wound healing Supports testosterone and reproductive health Required for taste, smell, and skin integrity Acts as an antioxidant cofactor Frequent colds and slow healing Hair loss and skin problems (acne, eczema) Loss of taste or smell Low libido and poor appetite Oysters Beef Pumpkin seeds Chickpeas Cashews Animal sources absorb 2–3x better than plant sources Soak/sprout grains and legumes to cut phytates Balance with copper — long-term zinc depletes it High-dose zinc depletes copper and can impair immunity Avoid taking with calcium or iron supplements

entries.title: Copper
entries.text: Copper mineral Mineral ~900mcg / day var(--accent) Important for collagen formation, iron metabolism, and antioxidant defense. Copper works alongside iron to form red blood cells, builds the collagen and elastin that hold connective tissue together, and powers key antioxidant enzymes. It must stay in balance with zinc — high zinc intake is a common, overlooked cause of copper deficiency. Helps form red blood cells and absorb iron Builds collagen and elastin for skin and vessels Drives antioxidant enzymes (SOD) Supports nerve and immune function Anemia not corrected by iron Easy bruising and weak connective tissue Low white blood cells and frequent infection Premature graying of hair Liver Oysters Dark chocolate Cashews Sesame seeds Balance with zinc (roughly 1mg copper per 10mg zinc) A few cashews or a square of dark chocolate covers most needs Excess copper is toxic to the liver High supplemental zinc is the main driver of deficiency

entries.title: Selenium
entries.text: Selenium mineral Mineral 55mcg / day var(--purple) Supports immune function, thyroid hormone conversion, and antioxidant protection. Selenium is built into selenoproteins that defend cells from oxidative damage and convert thyroid hormone T4 into its active form T3. It also supports immune surveillance and reproductive health.
