Just two Brazil nuts a day typically meet the requirement — but the window between enough and too much is narrow. Powers glutathione peroxidase antioxidant defense Converts thyroid hormone T4 to active T3 Supports immune response and fertility Fatigue and brain fog Impaired thyroid function Weakened immunity Muscle weakness Brazil nuts Tuna Eggs Sardines Sunflower seeds 2 Brazil nuts/day usually meets needs — don’t overdo it Pairs with iodine for healthy thyroid function Toxic in excess (selenosis): hair loss, nail changes, garlic breath Do not stack high-dose supplements with Brazil nuts

entries.title: Iodine
entries.text: Iodine mineral Mineral 150mcg / day var(--accent) Crucial for thyroid hormone production and metabolic regulation. Iodine is a raw material for thyroid hormones, which set the body’s metabolic rate and are vital for brain development in pregnancy and infancy. Iodine deficiency is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide; iodized salt and seafood are the main dietary sources. Builds thyroid hormones T3 and T4 Sets metabolic rate and energy use Critical for fetal and infant brain development Goiter (enlarged thyroid) Fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance Brain fog and depression Developmental delays in infants Iodized salt Seaweed (kelp, nori) Cod Shrimp Dairy Use iodized salt or eat seafood/seaweed weekly Pairs with selenium for balanced thyroid function Excess (often from kelp/seaweed) can also disrupt the thyroid Those with thyroid conditions should consult a clinician

entries.title: Manganese
entries.text: Manganese mineral Mineral 1.8–2.3mg / day var(--green) Supports glucose metabolism, bone development, and antioxidant defenses. Manganese activates enzymes for bone formation, carbohydrate and amino-acid metabolism, and wound healing. It is also the metal at the core of mitochondrial superoxide dismutase (MnSOD), a frontline antioxidant. Deficiency is rare because small amounts in plants, grains, and tea cover daily needs. Cofactor for bone-building and metabolic enzymes Core of the mitochondrial antioxidant MnSOD Aids carbohydrate and collagen metabolism Poor bone development Impaired glucose tolerance Slow wound healing (deficiency is uncommon) Whole grains Nuts Leafy greens Tea Pineapple A varied whole-food diet easily covers needs Tea is one of the richest everyday sources Excess (often from contaminated water) can affect the nervous system

entries.title: Chromium
entries.text: Chromium mineral Mineral 25–35mcg / day var(--pink) Helps regulate blood sugar and improves insulin sensitivity. Chromium enhances the action of insulin, helping cells take up glucose efficiently. While the body needs only trace amounts, adequate chromium supports stable blood sugar and may help reduce carbohydrate cravings. Refined diets are low in chromium. Potentiates insulin and glucose uptake Supports stable blood sugar May help curb sugar cravings Impaired blood-sugar control Increased sugar cravings Fatigue after carb-heavy meals (deficiency is rare) Broccoli Whole grains Lean beef Green beans Brewer’s yeast Whole foods provide enough for most people May be useful for blood-sugar management under guidance High-dose supplements have limited proven benefit Consult a clinician if on diabetes medication

entries.title: Fiber
entries.text: Fiber fiber Fiber 25–38g / day var(--green) Aids digestion, steadies blood sugar, feeds gut bacteria, and lowers cholesterol. Fiber is the indigestible part of plants. Soluble fiber forms a gel that slows digestion, lowers cholesterol, and steadies blood sugar; insoluble fiber adds bulk and keeps things moving. Both feed beneficial gut bacteria, which ferment fiber into short-chain fatty acids that nourish the colon and calm inflammation. Feeds gut bacteria, producing short-chain fatty acids Slows glucose absorption and steadies blood sugar Binds cholesterol and bile acids Adds bulk for regular, healthy digestion Constipation and irregular digestion Blood-sugar swings and hunger Poor gut microbiome diversity Higher cholesterol Vegetables Legumes Chia seeds Oats Berries Increase slowly with plenty of water to avoid gas Psyllium husk is an effective soluble-fiber supplement Aim for variety to feed diverse gut bacteria Ramping up too fast causes bloating Very high intake can reduce mineral absorption

entries.title: Water
entries.text: Water fiber Hydration 12–14 cups / day var(--blue) Maintains hydration, regulates temperature, supports digestion, and flushes waste. Water makes up roughly 60% of the body and is the medium for nearly every biochemical reaction. It transports nutrients, cushions joints, regulates temperature through sweat, and helps the kidneys clear waste. True hydration depends on electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium) as much as on fluid volume. Transports nutrients and removes waste Regulates body temperature via sweat Lubricates joints and cushions tissues Supports digestion and kidney function Fatigue, headache, and poor concentration Dark urine and constipation Dizziness and muscle cramps Dry skin and lips Plain water Electrolyte water Herbal teas Coconut water Water-rich produce Add electrolytes during heat, exercise, or low-carb diets Use urine color (pale straw) as a hydration gauge Spread intake through the day rather than all at once Overhydration without electrolytes can dangerously dilute sodium

entries.title: Vitamin A
entries.text: Vitamin A vitamin Fat-Soluble 700–900mcg / day var(--accent) Immunity, eye health, and cell growth — essential for night vision. Vitamin A maintains vision (especially in low light), keeps the immune system and skin barrier strong, and directs healthy cell growth. Preformed retinol from animal foods is used directly; plant beta-carotene must be converted, and some people convert poorly. Because it is fat-soluble and stored, both deficiency and excess are possible. Forms rhodopsin for night vision Supports immune defense and mucous barriers Directs cell growth and differentiation Maintains healthy skin Night blindness and dry eyes Frequent infections Dry, rough skin Impaired growth in children Beef liver Sweet potato Carrots Eggs Spinach Eat with fat to absorb Preformed A (liver, eggs) is more reliable than beta-carotene A little liver weekly is a potent source Excess preformed A is toxic and harmful in pregnancy Don’t megadose retinol supplements

entries.title: Vitamin D
entries.text: Vitamin D vitamin Fat-Soluble 600–800 IU (most need 2,000–5,000 IU) var(--accent) Bone health, immunity, calcium absorption, and mood regulation. Vitamin D acts more like a hormone than a vitamin: it regulates calcium absorption for bone health, modulates the immune system, and influences mood. The skin makes it from sunlight, but latitude, season, sunscreen, and indoor lifestyles leave a large share of people deficient. It works together with vitamin K2 and magnesium. Drives calcium absorption for strong bones Modulates immune function Supports mood and reduces seasonal lows Influences muscle strength Fatigue and low mood Frequent infections Bone pain and muscle weakness Hair loss Sunlight Sardines Mackerel Egg yolks Cod liver oil Test blood 25-OH vitamin D and dose to reach ~40–60 ng/mL Take with a fat-containing meal Pair with K2 and magnesium Fat-soluble — very high chronic doses can cause toxicity Get levels tested rather than guessing

entries.title: Vitamin E
entries.text: Vitamin E vitamin Fat-Soluble 15mg / day var(--accent) Antioxidant protection for cell membranes, skin, and immune health. Vitamin E is the body’s main fat-soluble antioxidant, protecting cell membranes and LDL cholesterol from oxidative damage. It supports skin, immune function, and healthy blood vessels. Natural vitamin E is a family of eight compounds; whole-food sources deliver the full spectrum. Protects cell membranes from oxidation Supports immune function Maintains healthy skin Helps prevent LDL oxidation Nerve and muscle weakness (rare) Impaired immunity Vision problems over time Sunflower seeds Almonds Avocado Spinach Olive oil Get it from whole foods for the full tocopherol/tocotrienol spectrum Eat with fat for absorption High-dose synthetic supplements may increase bleeding risk Avoid megadoses with blood thinners

entries.title: Vitamin K2
entries.text: Vitamin K2 vitamin Fat-Soluble 90–120mcg / day var(--accent) Directs calcium into bones and away from arteries; supports clotting. Vitamin K2 activates proteins that escort calcium into bones and teeth and keep it out of arteries and soft tissue — making it the crucial partner to vitamin D and calcium. (K1, from greens, mainly supports blood clotting.) K2 comes from fermented foods and animal fats and is scarce in modern diets. Activates osteocalcin to bind calcium to bone Activates MGP to keep calcium out of arteries Supports healthy blood clotting Works synergistically with vitamin D3 Higher risk of arterial calcification Weaker bones despite adequate calcium Easy bruising (clotting forms) Natto Hard cheese (Gouda) Egg yolks Butter (grass-fed) Liver Take alongside vitamin D3 and calcium MK-7 form lasts longest in the body Natto is by far the richest source Can interact with warfarin and other blood thinners — consult a clinician

entries.title: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)
entries.text: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) vitamin Water-Soluble 1.1–1.2mg / day var(--blue) Converts carbohydrates into usable energy and supports nerve function. Thiamine is essential for turning carbohydrates into ATP and for healthy nerve and heart function. Needs rise with high-carb or high-alcohol intake. Because it is water-soluble and not stored, deficiency can develop within weeks of poor intake — classically seen in alcoholism and refined-carb diets. Converts carbohydrates into cellular energy Supports nerve signal transmission Maintains healthy heart muscle Fatigue and irritability Nerve tingling and numbness Poor memory and concentration Beriberi in severe cases Sunflower seeds Pork Legumes Whole grains Trout Needs increase with high-carb and high-alcohol intake Whole grains and legumes are steady sources Excess is excreted in urine; toxicity is very rare

entries.title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)
entries.text: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) vitamin Water-Soluble 1.1–1.3mg / day var(--blue) Drives energy production and antioxidant defense via FAD and FMN. Riboflavin forms the coenzymes FAD and FMN, which carry electrons through energy metabolism and help regenerate the antioxidant glutathione. It also helps activate vitamins B6 and folate. It is abundant in dairy, eggs, and almonds, but is degraded by light. Forms FAD/FMN coenzymes for energy metabolism Regenerates glutathione antioxidant Helps activate vitamin B6 and folate Supports healthy skin and eyes Cracks at the corners of the mouth Sore, inflamed tongue Sensitivity to light and eye fatigue Greasy, scaly skin Eggs Almonds Dairy Lean meat Mushrooms Store foods away from light (riboflavin degrades in sunlight) Often helpful for some migraine sufferers under guidance Harmless excess turns urine bright yellow

entries.title: Vitamin B3 (Niacin)
entries.text: Vitamin B3 (Niacin) vitamin Water-Soluble 14–16mg / day var(--blue) Powers energy metabolism, DNA repair, and brain function via NAD+. Niacin builds NAD+ and NADP+, coenzymes central to energy production, DNA repair, and cellular signaling. The body can also make some niacin from the amino acid tryptophan. High pharmacological doses are sometimes used to improve cholesterol, but they cause flushing and require medical oversight. Forms NAD+/NADP+ for energy and DNA repair Supports brain function and skin health Helps regulate cholesterol at high doses Fatigue and brain fog Skin rash in sun-exposed areas Digestive upset Pellagra in severe cases Chicken breast Tuna Mushrooms Peanuts Turkey Protein-rich diets supply tryptophan that converts to niacin Therapeutic doses for cholesterol need medical supervision High-dose niacin causes flushing and can stress the liver

entries.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
entries.text: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) vitamin Water-Soluble 5mg / day var(--blue) Builds coenzyme A for metabolism and hormone production. Pantothenic acid is the backbone of coenzyme A, which is required to metabolize fats, carbohydrates, and proteins and to synthesize hormones and the neurotransmitter acetylcholine. It is found in nearly all foods ("pantos" means everywhere), so deficiency is rare. Forms coenzyme A for energy metabolism Supports adrenal hormone synthesis Aids acetylcholine production Helps maintain healthy skin Fatigue and irritability Numbness or burning feet (rare) Poor coordination Avocado Chicken Sweet potato Eggs Mushrooms Widely available in whole foods — deficiency is uncommon Very safe; excess is excreted

entries.title: Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)
entries.text: Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) vitamin Water-Soluble 1.3–2mg / day var(--blue) Builds neurotransmitters and red blood cells; supports mood. Vitamin B6 is a cofactor for more than 100 enzymes, especially those that build neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, GABA), form hemoglobin, and metabolize amino acids and homocysteine. It is important for mood, sleep, and PMS symptoms. Unusually, very high long-term supplemental doses can cause nerve damage. Synthesizes serotonin, dopamine, and GABA Helps form hemoglobin for oxygen transport Lowers homocysteine with folate and B12 Supports immune function Low mood and irritability PMS symptoms Cracked lips and sore tongue Numbness or tingling Turkey Salmon Spinach Bananas Chickpeas Works with magnesium for mood and PMS support Get it mostly from food Chronic high-dose supplements (>100mg) can cause nerve damage

entries.title: Vitamin B7 (Biotin)
entries.text: Vitamin B7 (Biotin) vitamin Water-Soluble 30mcg / day var(--blue) Supports hair, nails, and skin, plus fat and carbohydrate metabolism. Biotin is a cofactor for carboxylase enzymes that metabolize fats, carbohydrates, and amino acids. It is best known for supporting hair, skin, and nail health. Gut bacteria make some biotin, and it is widespread in food, so deficiency is uncommon — though raw egg whites can block its absorption. Cofactor for fat and carbohydrate metabolism Supports keratin for hair and nails Maintains healthy skin Aids blood-sugar regulation Thinning hair Brittle nails Scaly red skin rash Fatigue Egg yolks Salmon Nuts Sweet potato Seeds Cook egg whites — raw whites bind biotin Whole eggs (yolk + cooked white) are ideal High-dose biotin can skew some lab tests (e.g., thyroid, troponin) — tell your doctor

entries.title: Vitamin B9 (Folate)
entries.text: Vitamin B9 (Folate) vitamin Water-Soluble 400mcg / day var(--blue) Essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and fetal development. Folate is required to make and repair DNA and to form red blood cells, so demand is highest during rapid growth and pregnancy — where it prevents neural-tube defects. Natural folate comes from greens and legumes; folic acid is the synthetic form in supplements and fortified foods. Some people with MTHFR variants convert it less efficiently. Builds and repairs DNA Forms red blood cells Prevents neural-tube defects in pregnancy Lowers homocysteine with B6 and B12 Fatigue and weakness Mouth sores and swollen tongue Megaloblastic anemia Neural-tube defects in pregnancy Leafy greens Lentils Asparagus Avocado Beans Critical before and during early pregnancy Methylfolate is a useful form for some MTHFR variants High folic acid can mask a B12 deficiency — check both

entries.title: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
entries.text: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) vitamin Water-Soluble 2.4mcg / day var(--blue) Forms red blood cells, maintains nerves, and synthesizes DNA. Vitamin B12 is needed to make red blood cells, build the myelin that insulates nerves, and synthesize DNA. It is found almost exclusively in animal foods, so vegans and many older adults (who absorb it poorly) are at high risk. Deficiency can cause permanent neurological damage if left untreated. Forms healthy red blood cells Maintains the myelin nerve sheath Synthesizes DNA Lowers homocysteine with folate and B6 Fatigue and weakness Numbness and tingling Memory problems and brain fog Glossitis and mood changes Salmon Beef Eggs Dairy Clams Vegans and adults over 50 should supplement Methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin are active forms Sublingual or injection helps if absorption is poor Deficiency can cause irreversible nerve damage — don’t ignore symptoms

entries.title: Vitamin C
entries.text: Vitamin C vitamin Water-Soluble 75–90mg / day var(--green) Builds collagen, supports immunity, and boosts iron absorption. Vitamin C is a powerful water-soluble antioxidant and an essential cofactor for collagen synthesis — making it vital for skin, blood vessels, gums, and wound healing. It also regenerates other antioxidants, supports immune cells, and dramatically improves absorption of plant iron. Humans cannot make it, so daily intake is required. Builds collagen for skin, vessels, and joints Supports immune cell function Regenerates vitamin E and other antioxidants Enhances non-heme iron absorption Bleeding or swollen gums Slow wound healing Easy bruising Fatigue (scurvy in severe cases) Bell peppers Kale Broccoli Citrus Strawberries Eat raw or lightly cooked — heat degrades it Take with plant iron to boost absorption Smokers and stressed people need more Very high doses can cause digestive upset and kidney stones in some

entries.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids
entries.text: Omega-3 Fatty Acids other Essential Fat 1.1–1.6g / day (more for EPA/DHA) var(--teal) Anti-inflammatory fats vital for brain, heart, and joint health. Omega-3s — especially EPA and DHA from marine sources — are structural fats in the brain and retina and powerful regulators of inflammation. They support heart rhythm, triglyceride levels, mood, and joint comfort. Plant ALA (flax, chia) converts to EPA/DHA only inefficiently, so fatty fish or algae oil is preferred. Build brain and retinal cell membranes Resolve inflammation Lower triglycerides and support heart rhythm Support mood and joint comfort Dry skin and brittle hair Poor concentration and low mood Joint stiffness Elevated triglycerides Salmon Sardines Mackerel Chia seeds Walnuts Prioritize EPA/DHA from fish or algae over plant ALA Aim for 2 servings of fatty fish weekly Balance against omega-6 by limiting seed oils High doses can thin blood — caution with anticoagulants

entries.title: Omega-6 Fatty Acids
entries.text: Omega-6 Fatty Acids other Essential Fat 12–17g / day var(--pink) Essential fats for skin, hair, and cell function — best kept in balance. Omega-6 fats are essential for skin barrier integrity, cell signaling, and growth. The issue in modern diets is not deficiency but imbalance: industrial seed oils flood the diet with omega-6, crowding out anti-inflammatory omega-3. A healthier ratio comes from eating more omega-3 and fewer refined oils. Maintain the skin barrier Support cell membrane structure and signaling Aid normal growth and development Dry, scaly skin Hair loss Poor wound healing (deficiency is rare) Nuts Seeds Poultry Eggs Whole grains Most people get plenty — focus on raising omega-3, not omega-6 Limit refined seed/vegetable oils to improve the ratio Excess from processed oils may promote inflammation when omega-3 is low

entries.title: Choline
entries.text: Choline other Other Nutrient 425–550mg / day var(--blue) Supports brain, memory, liver function, and cell membranes. Choline is needed to make acetylcholine (a memory and muscle-control neurotransmitter) and phosphatidylcholine (a major cell-membrane building block). It is essential for liver fat metabolism and for fetal brain development. The body makes only small amounts, so diet matters — egg yolks and liver are standout sources. Builds acetylcholine for memory and muscle control Forms cell membranes (phosphatidylcholine) Transports fat out of the liver Supports fetal brain development Fatigue and memory issues Fatty liver Muscle damage Higher needs in pregnancy Egg yolks Beef liver Chicken Fish Soybeans Two eggs supply a large share of daily needs Especially important during pregnancy Very high doses can cause a fishy body odor and low blood pressure

entries.title: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10)
entries.text: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) other Other Nutrient 30–200mg / day (no set RDA) var(--accent) Antioxidant that powers cellular energy and protects the heart. CoQ10 sits in the mitochondria, where it shuttles electrons to generate ATP, and acts as a fat-soluble antioxidant. The heart and other high-energy organs are richest in it. Natural levels decline with age, and statin medications lower it — making supplementation worthwhile for some. Drives mitochondrial ATP (energy) production Acts as a fat-soluble antioxidant Supports heart muscle function Fatigue and low exercise tolerance Muscle aches (notably on statins) Declines naturally with age Organ meats Fatty fish Beef Peanuts Spinach Ubiquinol form absorbs better, especially with age Take with a fatty meal Often paired with statin therapy under guidance May interact with blood thinners — consult a clinician

entries.title: Lutein & Zeaxanthin
entries.text: Lutein & Zeaxanthin other Other Nutrient 6–10mg / day (no set RDA) var(--green) Eye-protective carotenoids that filter blue light and guard the retina. Lutein and zeaxanthin are carotenoid antioxidants that concentrate in the macula of the eye, where they filter damaging blue light and neutralize oxidative stress. Higher intake is linked to a lower risk of age-related macular degeneration and cataracts, and may support cognition. Filter high-energy blue light in the retina Protect the macula from oxidative damage May support memory and processing speed Greater susceptibility to macular degeneration Increased eye strain over time Kale Spinach Egg yolks Corn Bell peppers Eat with fat for absorption Egg yolks deliver a highly bioavailable form Very safe; high intake may harmlessly tint skin

entries.title: Probiotics
entries.text: Probiotics other Other Nutrient No set RDA var(--purple) Beneficial live bacteria that support gut, digestion, and immunity. Probiotics are live beneficial microbes that, in adequate amounts, support a balanced gut microbiome. They aid digestion, compete with harmful bacteria, produce vitamins and short-chain fatty acids, and influence immunity and even mood through the gut-brain axis. Different strains do different things. Balance the gut microbiome Aid digestion and nutrient production Support immune regulation Influence mood via the gut-brain axis Digestive irregularity and bloating Greater susceptibility after antibiotics Reduced microbiome diversity Yogurt Kefir Sauerkraut Kimchi Miso Rotate fermented foods for strain diversity Pair with prebiotic fiber to feed them Strain and dose matter — match to the goal Immunocompromised people should consult a clinician first

entries.title: Prebiotics
entries.text: Prebiotics other Other Nutrient No set RDA var(--teal) Fibers that feed beneficial gut bacteria and improve digestion. Prebiotics are specialized fibers (like inulin and resistant starch) that humans can’t digest but beneficial gut bacteria ferment into short-chain fatty acids such as butyrate. They nourish the microbiome from within, complementing probiotics, and support gut-barrier and metabolic health. Feed beneficial gut bacteria Produce butyrate to nourish the colon Support gut-barrier integrity Aid mineral absorption Less microbiome diversity Sluggish digestion Reduced short-chain fatty acid production Garlic Onions Leeks Asparagus Green bananas Increase slowly to avoid gas Cooked-then-cooled potato/rice adds resistant starch Combine with probiotics for synergy Can worsen symptoms in some IBS/SIBO cases — adjust to tolerance

entries.title: Collagen
entries.text: Collagen other Other Nutrient 2.5–10g / day (no set RDA) var(--pink) Structural protein for skin elasticity, joints, and connective tissue. Collagen is the most abundant protein in the body, forming the scaffolding of skin, tendons, ligaments, cartilage, and bone. Natural production declines with age. Supplemental collagen peptides supply the specific amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline) that support skin elasticity and joint comfort; vitamin C is required to build it. Provides skin elasticity and hydration Supports joint cartilage and tendons Strengthens connective tissue and bone matrix Supplies glycine for sleep and gut lining Reduced skin elasticity and more wrinkles Joint aches and slower tissue repair Weaker tendons and ligaments Bone broth Collagen peptides Skin-on fish Chicken skin Gelatin Pair with vitamin C, which is required to synthesize collagen Peptides dissolve easily into coffee or smoothies Not a complete protein — don’t rely on it as your only protein

entries.title: Creatine
entries.text: Creatine other Other Nutrient 3–5g / day (no set RDA) var(--blue) Fuels rapid energy for muscle performance and brain function. Creatine helps regenerate ATP for short, intense efforts, making it one of the most evidence-backed supplements for strength and power. Beyond muscle, it supports brain energy and may aid cognition, especially under stress or sleep deprivation. The body makes some and stores it mostly in muscle; meat is the main dietary source. Rapidly regenerates ATP for power output Increases strength and lean muscle gains Supports brain energy and cognition Buffers fatigue in intense exercise Lower power output and strength Vegetarians/vegans tend to have lower stores Red meat Herring Salmon Pork (Supplement: monohydrate) Creatine monohydrate, 3–5g daily, is the gold standard No need to "load" — consistency matters most Vegetarians often see the biggest benefit May cause mild water retention initially Drink adequate water; safe for healthy kidneys

entries.title: Carnitine
entries.text: Carnitine other Other Nutrient 500–2,000mg / day (no set RDA) var(--accent) Shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria for energy and supports the brain. L-carnitine transports long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondria, where they are burned for energy — making it central to fat metabolism and endurance. The body produces it from lysine and methionine, and red meat is the richest food source, so intake is lower on plant-based diets. Transports fatty acids into mitochondria for fuel Supports energy production and endurance Aids recovery and reduces exercise fatigue Supports brain and heart energy Fatigue and low exercise tolerance Muscle weakness Lower stores on vegetarian/vegan diets Red meat Pork Fish Chicken Dairy Acetyl-L-carnitine crosses into the brain for cognitive support Most useful for those eating little red meat High doses may cause GI upset; consult a clinician if you have heart/kidney conditions

entries.title: Superfoods
entries.text: Reference Guide Super foods "Superfood" is a marketing word, not a scientific category — but the label is often stuck on genuinely nutrient-dense foods worth eating. This guide separates the evidence from the hype, covers the popular picks, and shows how to use them well. First, the honest part. No single food is "super" enough to offset an otherwise poor diet, and no regulator defines the term — the EU has banned health claims using the word "superfood" since 2007 unless backed by specific authorised evidence. What follows treats "superfood" as useful shorthand for foods with an unusually high ratio of nutrients to calories , while flagging how strong the evidence actually is for each. What Actually Makes a Food "Super" The real criteria Nutrient Density The defensible idea behind the buzzword: some foods deliver far more vitamins, minerals, fibre, or protective plant compounds per calorie than others. That density — not novelty or price — is what matters. Phytochemicals — polyphenols, carotenoids, glucosinolates that act as antioxidants and signalling molecules Micronutrient load — concentrated vitamins and minerals relative to energy Fibre & healthy fats — feed the gut and support heart and brain health Low caloric cost — you can eat enough to matter without overshooting energy needs The catch Where Hype Creeps In Most "superfood" claims come from lab or animal studies using doses far higher than a normal serving. Impressive antioxidant numbers in a test tube rarely translate cleanly to the human body. Exotic ≠ better — kale and lentils often beat costly imported powders Dose mismatch — a study's effect may need 10× a realistic serving Single-food focus — overall dietary pattern outperforms any one item Marketing premiums — the label often adds cost, not nutrition Popular Superfoods & The Evidence The most-marketed picks, with what they genuinely offer and how strong the human evidence is. Evidence tags: Strong consistent human trials · Moderate promising but mixed · Early mostly lab/animal or small studies. Several link through to their full breakdown elsewhere on the site. Berry Strong 🫐 Blueberries One of the best-studied "superfoods." Anthocyanins (the blue pigment) are linked to better memory, blood-pressure, and vascular function in human trials. Rich in vitamin C, vitamin K, manganese, and fibre Frozen are as nutritious as fresh — and cheaper brain antioxidant on Food Categories → Algae Moderate 🟢 Spirulina A blue-green algae that is roughly 60% protein by weight and dense in iron and B vitamins. Small trials suggest benefits for blood lipids and exercise fatigue. Complete protein with all essential amino acids Source matters — buy from brands that test for contaminants protein immune on Food Categories → Algae Early 🌱 Chlorella A freshwater algae studied mainly for binding heavy metals and supporting detox pathways. Human evidence is limited but promising for cholesterol and immune markers. High in chlorophyll, iron, B12-analogues, and protein Cracked-cell-wall forms are far better absorbed detox immune full breakdown → Leaf Early 🌿 Moringa The "miracle tree" — leaves are exceptionally rich in vitamin A, calcium, and iron. Most claims rest on nutrient content and early studies rather than large human trials. Gram-for-gram high in vitamins A and C, calcium, potassium Dried leaf powder concentrates the nutrients (and any contaminants) antioxidant energy on Food Categories → Root Moderate 🟤 Maca Root An Andean adaptogen traditionally used for energy, libido, and hormonal balance. Several small trials support effects on mood and sexual function. Provides minerals, fibre, and unique macamides Start low — it is stimulating for some people hormone libido as an adaptogen → Berry Early 🫐 Açaí An Amazonian palm berry high in anthocyanins and healthy fats. Antioxidant content is genuinely high, but human health outcomes are not yet well established. Usually sold as frozen pulp or freeze-dried powder Watch for added sugar in açaí bowls and blends antioxidant skin on Food Categories → Bean Moderate 🍫 Raw Cacao Unprocessed chocolate, packed with flavanols and magnesium. Cocoa flavanols have solid evidence for blood-flow and modest blood-pressure benefits. One of the richest food sources of magnesium Heat and Dutch-processing destroy much of the flavanol content heart mood on Food Categories → Crucifer Strong 🥦 Broccoli & Greens The everyday "superfood." Cruciferous and leafy greens have some of the strongest evidence of any food group for lower cancer and all-cause mortality risk. Sulforaphane (broccoli) activates the body's antioxidant defences Cheap, available, and effective — the opposite of exotic hype longevity detox full breakdown → Everyday Superfoods That Outperform the Hype The most evidence-backed nutrient-dense foods are usually affordable and unglamorous. These earn the label more honestly than most imported powders — and most already have a home elsewhere on the site. Food Why It Earns It Best Use Fatty fish EPA/DHA omega-3s for heart, brain, and mood — among the best-evidenced of all Salmon, sardines, mackerel 2–3×/week Leafy greens Folate, vitamin K, nitrates, and fibre at almost no caloric cost Kale , spinach, chard — daily, raw or lightly cooked Berries Anthocyanins and fibre with strong cognitive and vascular evidence A handful daily frozen is fine Garlic Allicin and organosulfur compounds for immune and cardiovascular support Crushed and rested 10 min before cooking Turmeric Curcumin, anti-inflammatory — needs black pepper and fat to absorb With pepper + oil in cooking Fermented foods Live cultures that diversify the gut microbiome Kefir , yoghurt, sauerkraut, kimchi Nuts & seeds Healthy fats, vitamin E, magnesium, plant protein Walnuts , almonds, pumpkin and chia seeds Legumes Fibre, plant protein, and resistant starch for gut and blood sugar Lentils, chickpeas, beans — a staple of long-lived populations Extra-virgin olive oil Monounsaturated fat and polyphenols central to the Mediterranean diet Cold, as a finishing oil How to Use Superfoods Well 1 Pattern Over Powder A varied diet of vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, fish, nuts, and quality fats beats any single "miracle" food. Use superfoods to upgrade an already-balanced plate, not to rescue a poor one. 2 Spend Where Evidence Is Strongest Berries, leafy greens, cruciferous vegetables, fatty fish, and legumes have the deepest human evidence and are inexpensive. Treat costly imported powders as optional extras, not essentials. 3 Mind Preparation & Quality How you prepare a food often decides whether its compounds survive: rest crushed garlic, pair turmeric with pepper and fat, eat cruciferous greens lightly cooked. For powders and algae, buy from brands that test for heavy metals and contaminants. Key takeaway. "Superfood" is a useful nudge toward nutrient-dense eating and an empty word when it is a price tag. The foods with the best evidence are mostly ordinary — greens, berries, fish, legumes, olive oil — and the exotic options range from genuinely useful (spirulina, cacao) to merely trendy. Build the pattern first let the superfoods be the seasoning. This guide is educational and not a substitute for personalised medical or dietary advice.

entries.title: Food for Mood
entries.text: Reference Guide Food for Mood A practical guide to nutrient-dense foods that support steady energy, mood, brain health, hydration, and healthy weight gain without relying on processed calories. For education, not treatment. Food can support mood and energy, but it is not a replacement for mental-health care, medication, or medical advice. If depression, anxiety, fatigue, or appetite changes are persistent or severe, work with a clinician. What to Prioritize The strongest pattern is simple: build meals around whole foods, enough calories, steady protein, complex carbohydrates, healthy fats, minerals, and fermented or fiber-rich foods. Energy Complex Carbohydrates Use slow-digesting carbohydrates to help prevent energy crashes while keeping meals nutrient-dense. Quinoa, brown rice, oats, and sweet potatoes Black beans, lentils, and chickpeas Pair with protein and fat for steadier energy steady energy fiber Weight Gain Healthy Fats Healthy fats raise calories without needing large food volume, which helps when gaining weight cleanly. Avocado, olive oil, nuts, and seeds Walnuts, almonds, chia, flax, and pumpkin seeds Coconut oil can fit, but olive oil is the better daily default calorie support omega fats Repair Protein Foods Protein supports muscle, satiety, neurotransmitter building blocks, and recovery from training. Eggs, lean meats, fish, Greek yogurt, tofu, and tempeh Include a protein source at most meals Use smoothies or yogurt bowls when appetite is low recovery amino acids Mood-Supporting Foods Folate Dark Leafy Greens Spinach, kale, and Swiss chard supply folate and minerals that support normal brain and nervous-system function. Add to eggs, rice bowls, smoothies, soups, or sides Cook greens when raw meals feel hard to digest folate minerals Gut-Brain Fermented Foods Kimchi, sauerkraut, yogurt, and kefir support gut health, which is linked with mood and immune regulation. Start small: 1 to 2 tablespoons of fermented vegetables Choose plain yogurt or kefir to avoid added sugar fermented microbiome Brain Fats Fatty Fish Salmon, sardines, and mackerel provide omega-3 fats that support brain health and mood regulation. Aim for 2 to 3 servings weekly if tolerated Use walnuts, chia, or flax when fish is not available omega-3 brain health Antioxidants Berries and Dark Chocolate Berries bring antioxidants and fiber. Dark chocolate at 85% cacao or higher can be a small mood-supporting treat. Use berries in oats, yogurt, or smoothies Keep dark chocolate portions small because it still contains caffeine and calories polyphenols stress support Depression and Anxiety Support These foods and drinks are useful support layers because they target inflammation, minerals, relaxation, and neurotransmitter cofactors. They should complement, not replace, mental-health care. Food or Drink Main Support How to Use Notes Turmeric + black pepper Curcumin and inflammation support Add to curries, soups, eggs, or rice bowls with a little fat Use caution with blood thinners or high-dose supplements Nuts and seeds Magnesium, zinc, healthy fats Small handfuls, seed toppings, nut butter, or trail mix Great for healthy weight gain watch allergies Green tea L-theanine plus gentle caffeine 1 to 3 cups earlier in the day Can promote calm alertness, but caffeine sensitivity varies Bananas Vitamin B6 and carbohydrates Add to smoothies, oats, yogurt, or post-workout meals Useful when appetite or energy is low Hydration and Herbal Support Hydration Water and Electrolytes Dehydration can worsen fatigue, headaches, low mood, and poor concentration. Drink water steadily through the day Add electrolytes after sweating, workouts, heat, or low intake Tea Herbal Teas Chamomile can be relaxing, peppermint can feel refreshing, and ashwagandha is often used as an adaptogen for stress. Keep herbs gentle and rotate based on response Check medication and condition interactions for adaptogens Fast Meal Smoothies and Juices Smoothies are the better default for weight gain because they keep fiber and can carry protein and healthy fats. Blend greens, berries, banana, yogurt, seeds, and nut butter Use juices more sparingly because they remove fiber Foods to Minimize Processed foods, refined sugars, artificial sweeteners, excess caffeine, excess alcohol, trans fats, and fried foods can drain energy, worsen inflammation, or make mood and appetite less stable. Weight-Gain Emphasis Simple Rule Add calories without lowering quality Keep carbs complex and nutrient-dense, then increase healthy fats and protein around the meals you already tolerate well. Add olive oil, avocado, nuts, seeds, Greek yogurt, eggs, salmon, tofu, or tempeh Use smoothies when it is easier to drink calories than chew them Choose whole-food calorie density instead of refined sugar or fried foods

entries.title: How-To
entries.text: Practical Guide How-To Reference Herbs & Spices Tinctures Aromatherapy Neuro & Body Chemicals Shakes & Detox Macro Classification Herbs & Spices — How to Use Methods of preparation, optimal dosing, and best uses for culinary and medicinal herbs and spices. Culinary Spices Turmeric Anti-inflammatory · Spice Add to curries, soups, golden milk, and smoothies. Always combine with black pepper (piperine) to increase absorption by 2,000%. Culinary: 1–3g / day (about 1/2 tsp) Supplement: 500–1,000mg curcumin extract Golden milk: 1 tsp turmeric + pinch black pepper + warm milk + honey Heat stable Fat-soluble Pairs with black pepper Cayenne Pepper Metabolism · Spice Add to meals, sauces, or dissolve 1/8 tsp in warm lemon water on an empty stomach to stimulate circulation and metabolism. Capsaicin activates TRPV1 receptors — boosts thermogenesis Start with tiny amounts — 1/16 tsp — and build up Topical cream: 0.025–0.075% capsaicin for joint pain Stimulant Metabolic Cinnamon Blood Sugar · Spice Add 1/2–1 tsp to oatmeal, coffee, smoothies, or yogurt. Ceylon cinnamon is safer for daily use than Cassia (lower coumarin). Take before carb-heavy meals to blunt blood sugar spike Use Ceylon variety for daily supplementation Dose: 1–6g / day for blood sugar effects Ceylon preferred Pre-meal Ginger Digestion · Nausea · Anti-inflammatory Use fresh, powdered, or as tea. Grate fresh ginger into meals, teas, or juices. Dried ginger is 5–10x more concentrated in shogaols. Nausea: 1g fresh ginger or tea before travel Anti-inflammatory: 2–3g/day fresh or 1g dried Digestive: chew a small piece before meals Pairs with lemon and honey in tea Fresh or dried Heat stable Garlic Antimicrobial · Cardiovascular Crush and let sit 10 minutes before cooking — this activates allicin. Eat raw for maximum benefit. Cooking reduces but doesn't eliminate benefits. Raw: 1–2 cloves / day for immune and cardiovascular effects Aged garlic extract: 600–1,200mg if sensitive to raw Crush → rest 10 min → cook for preserved allicin Crush first Raw is strongest Sage, Thyme & Oregano Antimicrobial · Antioxidant Use fresh or dried in cooking. Oil of oregano is a concentrated supplement — very different from culinary oregano. Sage tea: 2g dried sage in hot water — supports memory Thyme tea: soothes cough and respiratory infections Oil of oregano: 1–3 drops under tongue for antimicrobial use Always dilute oil of oregano with carrier oil for topical use Culinary dose safe Oil = concentrated Medicinal Herbs (How to Prepare) Ashwagandha Adaptogen · Cortisol · Stress Powder: 1/2 tsp in warm milk (traditional Ayurvedic method — enhances absorption) Capsule: 300–600mg KSM-66 or Sensoril extract (standardized) Take at night — supports sleep quality Cycle: 8 weeks on, 2 weeks off recommended Takes 4–8 weeks to feel full effect Holy Basil (Tulsi) Adaptogen · Anxiety · Blood Sugar Tea: steep 5–7 fresh leaves or 1 tsp dried in hot water for 5 min Capsule: 300–600mg standardized extract Drink 1–3 cups daily for stress and blood sugar support Safe for daily long-term use — gentler than ashwagandha Milk Thistle Liver Protection · Detox Capsule: 140–200mg silymarin (standardized to 70–80%) Tea: 1 tsp crushed seeds steeped 10 min — less potent than extract Take 20 min before meals for best liver protection Safe for long-term use — often taken for months during detox Dandelion Liver · Kidney · Digestion Root tea: roast and steep for liver support (2 tsp dried root / 8oz water) Leaf tea: diuretic, rich in vitamins (1 tbsp dried leaves) Tincture: 2–8ml three times daily Whole plant is usable — root, leaf, and flower Echinacea Immune Boost · Antiviral Tea: 1–2 tsp dried herb steeped 15 min — drink 3x/day during illness Tincture: 2–3ml 3x/day at onset of cold Capsule: 300–500mg dried extract 3x/day Best taken at first sign of illness — not for continuous prevention Cycle: use for max 10 days, break 3 days, repeat if needed Ginseng Energy · Cognitive · Adaptogen Tea: 2–3g dried root simmered 15 min in 2 cups water Capsule: 200–400mg standardized extract (morning or early afternoon) Tincture: 2–4ml 2x/day — Asian and American differ in effect Asian ginseng: energizing. American ginseng: calming. Siberian: adaptogen Avoid taking late in day — can affect sleep Tinctures — What They Are & How to Make Them A tincture is a concentrated herbal extract made by soaking herbs in alcohol or glycerin. Far more potent than teas and lasts for years. How to Make a Basic Tincture Method: Folk method (ratio method) — ideal for home preparation. No precise measuring required. 1 Choose your herb Fresh herbs: fill jar loosely. Dried herbs: fill jar 1/2 full. Roots/bark: coarsely chop and fill 1/3–1/2 jar. 2 Choose your menstruum (solvent) Alcohol (vodka or grain alcohol 40–60% ABV): best for most herbs. Glycerin: alcohol-free option — less potent, sweeter. Apple cider vinegar: mild — for culinary herbs. 3 Combine Fill jar with herbs. Pour menstruum until herbs are fully submerged with 1–2 inches of liquid above. Seal tightly with lid. 4 Macerate Store in a cool dark place for 4–6 weeks. Shake daily if possible. Fresh herbs: 4 weeks. Dried herbs and roots: 6–8 weeks. 5 Strain and bottle Strain through cheesecloth or fine mesh. Press all liquid out of the plant material. Bottle in dark glass dropper bottles. Label with herb, date, and menstruum. 6 Store and use Alcohol tinctures: 5–10 year shelf life. Glycerin: 1–3 years. Typical dose: 1–4ml (20–80 drops) in water, 1–3x daily. Always start with lowest dose. Popular Tinctures & Their Uses Valerian Root Sleep · Anxiety Strong sedative tincture. Strongly flavored — mix in juice or strong tea to mask taste. Dose: 2–4ml 30–60 min before bed Works best after 2–4 weeks of consistent use Do not combine with alcohol or sedative medications Elderberry Immune · Antiviral Elderberry tincture or syrup — one of the most effective natural antivirals for respiratory illness. Dose: 1–2ml 3–4x daily during illness Prevention: 1ml daily during flu season Syrup: 1 tbsp daily (easier for children) Licorice Root Gut · Adrenal · Antiviral Soothing to gut lining. Supports adrenal function. Use DGL (deglycyrrhizinated) form if concerned about blood pressure. Dose: 1–3ml 3x daily before meals for gut issues Short-term use: max 4–6 weeks without break Avoid in high blood pressure unless using DGL form Frankincense (Boswellia) Inflammation · Joint Pain Available as resin, capsule, or tincture. Powerful for arthritis and inflammatory conditions. Tincture: 2–4ml 2x daily for joint pain Standardized extract: 300–400mg 2–3x daily Apply diluted resin oil topically for localized joint pain Rhodiola Rosea Energy · Fatigue · Adaptogen Best taken in the morning. Remarkable for mental and physical fatigue without stimulant side effects. Tincture: 1–2ml in morning, fasted Capsule: 200–600mg standardized (3% rosavins, 1% salidroside) Cycle: 6–12 weeks on, 2 weeks off Reishi Mushroom Immune · Sleep · Anti-cancer Dual-extract tincture (water + alcohol) captures both beta-glucans and triterpenes. Single extraction misses compounds. Tincture: 2–3ml 2x daily Best taken in evening — calming and sleep-supportive Look for dual-extract on label Aromatherapy — How to Use Essential Oils Essential oils interact with the limbic system through the olfactory nerve — the only sense with direct brain access. Effects are fast (within 15 seconds of inhalation). Methods of Use Diffusion Most Common Method Add 5–15 drops to an ultrasonic diffuser with water. Run for 30–60 min, then break. Continuous diffusion reduces effectiveness. Best for mood, sleep, and respiratory effects Diffuse lavender at night for sleep Diffuse peppermint or rosemary in morning for focus Citrus oils for energy and mood elevation Topical Application Always Dilute First Never apply essential oils undiluted to skin. Always dilute in a carrier oil (coconut, jojoba, almond) before skin contact. Dilution ratio: 2% = 12 drops per 1 fl oz carrier oil For children or sensitive skin: 0.5–1% Apply to pulse points: wrists, temples, neck, behind ears Test patch first — wait 24h for sensitivity reaction Inhalation (Direct) Fastest Effect Add 1–2 drops to palms, cup over nose and breathe deeply for 3–5 breaths. Or use an inhaler stick. Best for acute anxiety, nausea, headache Peppermint: instant headache and nausea relief Lavender: rapid anxiety reduction Use inhaler sticks for on-the-go use Bath & Steam Full Body Absorption Mix 5–10 drops with a tablespoon of carrier oil or bath salts before adding to bath — never add directly to water (oils float and can irritate skin). Eucalyptus steam: 3–5 drops in bowl of hot water, cover head with towel Bath: lavender + magnesium flakes for sleep and muscle recovery Foot soak: 5 drops in warm water with Epsom salts Essential Oils by Effect Oil Primary Effect Secondary Effect How to Use Lavender Calm / Sleep Anxiety relief, wound healing Diffuse at night, apply to temples diluted Peppermint Focus / Energy Headache relief, digestion Diffuse in morning, inhale for headaches Rosemary Memory / Focus Hair growth, circulation Diffuse while studying, diluted scalp massage Eucalyptus Respiratory Antimicrobial, pain relief Steam inhalation, chest rub diluted Frankincense Grounding / Spiritual Anti-anxiety, anti-inflammatory Diffuse for meditation, apply to forehead diluted Lemon / Citrus Energy / Mood Antimicrobial, focus Diffuse in morning or work space Tea Tree Antimicrobial Acne, wound healing Apply diluted to skin, diffuse for air purification Bergamot Anti-anxiety / Mood Sleep support Diffuse or diluted on skin — photosensitive, avoid sun Ylang Ylang Libido / Romance Blood pressure reduction Diffuse or diluted pulse points — use sparingly Clary Sage Hormone Balance PMS relief, anxiety Diluted abdomen massage, diffuse during cycle Neuro & Body Chemicals — Food & Lifestyle Links Every mood, motivation, focus, and energy state is driven by specific chemicals. Here's how food and lifestyle directly influence each one. Serotonin — Happiness & Calm 90% produced in gut · Mood · Sleep · Appetite Serotonin is the foundation of emotional stability, contentment, and sleep quality. Foods: tryptophan-rich foods — turkey, eggs, cheese, pumpkin seeds, salmon Gut health: 90% of serotonin is made in the gut — probiotics are essential Sunlight: natural serotonin synthesis — 20–30 min direct sun daily Exercise: increases tryptophan crossing blood-brain barrier Cofactors: B6, zinc, magnesium required for synthesis Depleted by: chronic stress, alcohol, processed food, gut dysbiosis Tryptophan → 5-HTP → Serotonin Dopamine — Motivation & Reward Drive · Pleasure · Focus · Addiction Dopamine drives motivation, desire, and the reward cycle. Low dopamine = apathy, procrastination, and depression. Foods: tyrosine-rich — chicken, eggs, almonds, avocado, bananas Cold exposure: cold showers increase dopamine 250% Exercise: intense cardio significantly boosts dopamine Mucuna pruriens: natural L-DOPA precursor supplement Cofactors: iron, B6, vitamin C required for synthesis Depleted by: sugar, social media dopamine loops, sleep deprivation Tyrosine → L-DOPA → Dopamine Cortisol — Stress Hormone Stress · Inflammation · Metabolism · Wakefulness Cortisol peaks in the morning (normal) and should decline through the day. Chronically elevated cortisol devastates health. Lower with: ashwagandha, rhodiola, holy basil, magnesium Lower with: meditation (reduces cortisol 20%), cold exposure, sleep Vitamin C in high doses reduces post-exercise cortisol spike Phosphatidylserine: 400mg reduces exercise-induced cortisol Elevated by: caffeine (especially before sleep), chronic stress, poor sleep, inflammatory diet Adaptogens help Sleep is key GABA — Calm & Relaxation Anxiety relief · Inhibitory neurotransmitter · Sleep GABA is the brain's primary inhibitory (calming) neurotransmitter. Low GABA = anxiety, racing thoughts, and insomnia. Foods: fermented foods (increase GABA precursors), green tea (L-theanine) L-theanine: 200–400mg promotes GABA and alpha brain waves without sedation Magnesium: regulates GABA receptors — 400mg before bed Valerian root: binds GABA receptors similarly to benzodiazepines Depleted by: alcohol (short-term increases, long-term depletes), chronic stress L-theanine Magnesium glycinate Melatonin — Sleep Hormone Circadian rhythm · Sleep onset · Antioxidant Melatonin is produced from serotonin when darkness falls. Light exposure — especially blue light — suppresses production. Foods: tart cherry juice, walnuts, eggs, fatty fish — natural melatonin sources Block blue light 2 hours before bed (orange glasses) Keep bedroom completely dark — even small light sources disrupt production Supplement: 0.5–1mg is often more effective than 5–10mg doses Depleted by: blue light, night shift work, alcohol, caffeine late in day Oxytocin — Bonding & Trust Connection · Love · Anti-anxiety · Wound healing Oxytocin is released through touch, eye contact, social bonding, and orgasm. It counters cortisol and promotes healing. Foods: magnesium and vitamin D support oxytocin signaling Physical touch: hugging for 20+ seconds triggers oxytocin release Eye contact, genuine conversation, petting animals all boost oxytocin Exercise and cold water also stimulate release Depleted by: social isolation, chronic stress, loneliness Insulin — Blood Sugar Regulation Glucose metabolism · Fat storage · Inflammation Insulin sensitivity is one of the most important health markers. Insulin resistance drives obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline. Improve with: cinnamon, berberine, apple cider vinegar before meals Fasting: intermittent fasting dramatically improves insulin sensitivity Exercise: muscle contraction absorbs glucose without insulin (walk after meals) Chromium and magnesium improve insulin signaling Impaired by: sugar, refined carbs, seed oils, sedentary lifestyle, poor sleep Adrenaline (Epinephrine) — Fight or Flight Emergency response · Focus under stress · Heart rate Short-term adrenaline is normal and healthy. Chronically elevated adrenaline from stress, caffeine, or poor sleep damages health. Regulate with: adaptogens (ashwagandha, rhodiola), breath work (4-7-8 breathing) Cold exposure: brief cold triggers controlled adrenaline release for resilience Magnesium: blunts excessive adrenaline response Elevated by: caffeine, excessive exercise, chronic stress, processed sugar Shakes — Recipes & Protocols Protein shakes, detox shakes, recovery shakes, and mineral shakes with exact ingredients and preparation steps. Protein Shakes Classic Muscle Builder High Protein · Post-Workout · ~40g protein Ingredients 1 scoop whey or plant protein (25–30g protein) 1 cup whole milk or oat milk 1 frozen banana 1 tbsp almond butter 1 tsp creatine monohydrate 1/2 tsp vanilla extract Ice to blend Protocol Consume within 30–60 min post-workout Add leucine if using plant protein Replace banana with berries for lower sugar Add spinach (tasteless) for micronutrients Keto Protein Shake Low Carb · High Fat · ~35g protein Ingredients 1 scoop unflavored or vanilla whey 1 cup full-fat coconut milk 1 tbsp MCT oil 1 tbsp almond butter 1/2 avocado Pinch of sea salt Stevia to taste Protocol Keep carbs under 5g total MCT oil promotes ketone production Take in morning or post-workout Add collagen peptides for joint support Detox Shakes Heavy Metal Detox Shake Chlorophyll · Chelation · Morning Protocol Ingredients 1 cup wild blueberries (frozen) 1 tsp spirulina powder 1 tsp chlorella powder 1 cup coconut water 1 handful fresh cilantro 1 tbsp Atlantic dulse (seaweed) 1/2 cup water or ice Protocol Take on empty stomach in morning Cilantro + chlorella chelate heavy metals Spirulina binds and removes them from GI tract Wild blueberries repair oxidative damage from metals Cycle: 5 days on, 2 days off for 4–6 weeks Liver Detox Green Shake Liver Support · Anti-inflammatory · Daily Use Ingredients 2 cups spinach or kale 1 cucumber 1/2 lemon (juiced) 1 inch fresh ginger 1 inch fresh turmeric (or 1 tsp powder) 1 cup water or coconut water 1 green apple (optional sweetness) Protocol Drink on empty stomach 20 min before breakfast Ginger and turmeric reduce liver inflammation Lemon stimulates bile and liver enzyme production Add milk thistle powder for enhanced liver protection Lymph & Antioxidant Support Shake Food-first support &middot Antioxidants &middot Immune nutrition Ingredients 1 cup kale or spinach 1/2 cup blueberries 1/2 cup pineapple 1 small beet, raw or cooked 1 tbsp chia seeds 1 tbsp ground flaxseed 1 tbsp fresh ginger 1/2 tsp turmeric powder 1/2 cup coconut water or almond milk Optional: 1 scoop plant-based protein powder Protocol Blend until smooth, adding liquid as needed. Use as a nutrient-dense food support shake, not as a cancer treatment. Protein powder can help if appetite is low or protein needs are higher. If in active cancer treatment, ask the oncology team or dietitian about turmeric, ginger, antioxidants, protein powders, and supplement-like add-ins. Mineral & Recovery Shakes Potassium-Magnesium Electrolyte Shake Electrolytes · Muscle Recovery · Keto-Friendly Ingredients 1 frozen banana 2 cups spinach 1 cup coconut water 1 tbsp hemp seeds 1 tbsp pumpkin seeds 1/4 tsp Himalayan salt Ice Protocol Post-workout or after sweating heavily Provides natural potassium, magnesium, and sodium Add 1/4 tsp magnesium glycinate powder for extra boost Replaces most commercial electrolyte drinks Adaptogen Focus Shake Brain · Stress · Morning Energy Ingredients 1 cup oat milk or almond milk 1 tsp ashwagandha powder 1 tsp lion's mane mushroom powder 1 tbsp MCT oil or coconut oil 1 tsp cacao powder 1/2 tsp cinnamon Honey or stevia to taste Protocol Take in morning as coffee replacement or addition Lion's mane promotes NGF and cognitive function Ashwagandha blunts cortisol spike from morning stress MCT provides clean brain energy Add collagen for joint and skin benefits Macro Classification — Top Foods by Nutrient % Percentage of calories from protein, DHA/omega-3, and carbohydrates in the top 10 richest food sources. Helps you build precise macronutrient-targeted meals. Top 10 Protein-Rich Foods (by % of calories) Food Protein % Protein per 100g Notes Spirulina (dried) 57% 57g Complete protein, all essential amino acids Chicken breast (skinless) 75% 31g Lean, high-protein, low-fat Tuna (canned in water) 88% 29g Highest protein-to-calorie ratio of common foods Turkey breast 74% 30g Rich in tryptophan for serotonin Shrimp 84% 24g Low fat, very high protein percentage Cod (white fish) 85% 23g Extremely lean, high protein Eggs (whole) 35% 13g Complete protein with all essential amino acids + healthy fat Cottage cheese (low-fat) 69% 12g Casein protein — slow-digesting, ideal before bed Lentils (cooked) 27% 9g Best plant protein source with fiber Pumpkin seeds 22% 19g High zinc, magnesium, and complete amino acids Top 10 DHA / Omega-3 Rich Foods Food DHA + EPA (per 100g) Type Notes Mackerel 5,134mg DHA + EPA Highest omega-3 of all common fish Salmon (Atlantic) 2,260mg DHA + EPA Also rich in vitamin D and B12 Sardines (canned) 1,480mg DHA + EPA Affordable and sustainable — with bones for calcium Herring 2,366mg DHA + EPA Underrated source, high in B12 Oysters 672mg DHA + EPA Also richest zinc source in nature Cod liver oil 19,000mg DHA + EPA Supplement — also very high in vitamin A and D Flaxseed 22,800mg ALA only Must convert to DHA/EPA — conversion is poor (<5%) Chia seeds 17,550mg ALA only High ALA — best combined with fish sources Walnuts 9,080mg ALA only Best nut source — also rich in antioxidants Algae oil 400–500mg DHA + EPA Vegan DHA source — where fish get their omega-3 from Top 10 Complex Carbohydrate Foods (Highest Quality Carbs) Food Carbs per 100g Fiber Why It's Top-Tier Sweet potato 20g 3g High in beta-carotene, potassium, and slow-release energy Oats (rolled) 66g 10g Beta-glucan fiber lowers cholesterol and feeds gut bacteria Quinoa (cooked) 21g 2.8g Complete protein + complex carbs — unique combination Lentils (cooked) 20g 8g High fiber slows digestion — excellent blood sugar stability Brown rice 77g 3.5g Whole grain with B vitamins and manganese Chickpeas 27g 8g Resistant starch feeds gut bacteria, slow energy release Banana 23g 2.6g Potassium + natural sugars + resistant starch when underripe Beets 10g 2.8g Nitrates improve athletic performance and blood flow Barley (cooked) 28g 6g Highest fiber of all grains — powerful cholesterol lowering Butternut squash 12g 2g Low glycemic, high in vitamin A and potassium ⚠️ Protein percentages refer to % of total calories from protein. A food like tuna at 88% means nearly all its calories come from protein.
